- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.
- **`shell-scripts`** — A directory of standalone shell scripts (bash, zsh, sh) treated as one component. Plural by convention because a single shell file is not itself a component.
- **`sql-scripts`** — A directory of standalone SQL scripts — typically migrations or seed data — treated as one component. Plural by the same convention as `shell-scripts`.
- **`codegen-tool`** — A code-generation tool whose output is consumed by other components — e.g., a schema compiler, a scaffolding generator, or a template processor. The tool's implementation language lives in the `language` field.
- **`config-repo`** — A repository that exists primarily to hold configuration (dotfiles, infrastructure-as-code, declarative deployment specs) rather than code.
- **`docs-repo`** — A repository whose purpose is documentation — prose, reference material, or a specification authored in Markdown or similar.
- **`spec`** — A specification document or collection of documents describing behaviour that other components implement. Distinguished from a docs-repo by its role as a contract rather than reference prose.
- **`external`** — A third-party component referenced from a manifest (e.g., a published crate or npm package). Externals live in `external-components.yaml`, not `components.yaml`.
- **`non-component`** — A directory that looks like a candidate on paper but is confirmed not to be a component — for example, a `.git`-rooted directory whose contents are just metadata, or an empty placeholder.
","DIR_RELATIVE":"microprocess","LIFECYCLE_SCOPES":"- **`design`** — Design-time activities — specifications, architecture documents, RFCs.
- **`codegen`** — Code generation — schema compilers, scaffolding tools, template processors.
- **`build`** — Build-time activities — compilation, linking, packaging.
- **`test`** — Test-time activities — unit, integration, or acceptance tests.
- **`deploy`** — Deploy-time activities — packaging, provisioning, orchestration.
- **`runtime`** — Runtime activities — the live behaviour of the deployed system.
- **`dev-workflow`** — Developer workflow — tools used during development but not at runtime.
","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"microprocess/Dockerfile":"FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS base

WORKDIR /app

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build

WORKDIR /app

COPY ServiceDotNet ./microprocess/ServiceDotNet
COPY --from=shared_backend . ./shared-backend

RUN dotnet build "./microprocess/ServiceDotNet/PgServiceDotNet.csproj" -c Release -o /app/build

FROM build AS publish

WORKDIR /app

RUN dotnet publish "./microprocess/ServiceDotNet/PgServiceDotNet.csproj" -c Release -o /app/publish /p:UseAppHost=false

FROM base AS final

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=publish /app/publish .
COPY ./appsettings.js ./appsettings.js

ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "PgServiceDotNet.dll"]"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[{"level":2,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"2024-12.1.2"},{"level":3,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"Improvements"},{"level":2,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"2024-12.1.1"},{"level":3,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"Improvements"},{"level":2,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"2024-12.1"},{"level":3,"path":"microprocess/CHANGELOG.md","text":"Bug response.rationale: The directory contains a Dockerfile that builds a containerized .NET service (PgServiceDotNet). It is a multi-stage Docker build that compiles a C# service and packages it into an ASP.NET 8.0 runtime image, representing the deployment packaging artefact for a microservice.

{"COMPONENT_KINDS":"- **`rust-library`** — A Rust crate exposing a library interface. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[lib]` section with no `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/lib.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-cli`** — A Rust crate that produces one or more executable binaries. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/main.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-proc-macro`** — A Rust procedural-macro crate. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[lib] proc-macro = true`. Compile-time only; the crate ships no runtime artefact, only a compiler plugin consumed by other crates at build time.
- **`workspace`** — A multi-crate (or multi-package) workspace root. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]`, a `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or similar workspace manifests. The root is a component in its own right; its members are child components.
- **`node-library`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package exposing a library interface. Recognised by `package.json` with a `main` or `exports` field and no `bin` field. Whether the source is JS or TS is recorded in the `language` field, not the kind.
- **`node-cli`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package that installs one or more CLI executables. Recognised by `package.json` with a `bin` field.
- **`react-app`** — A React-based frontend application — typically a Create React App, Next.js, Remix, or Vite-based React project. Distinguished from `website` by an explicit React-framework manifest or a top-level `react` / `react-dom` runtime dependency. Use `website` for framework-agnostic frontends.
- **`react-library`** — A library of reusable React components, distributed as an npm package. Recognised by `package.json` declaring `react` as a `peerDependency` plus a library-style entry point.
- **`python-library`** — A Python library package — recognised by `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` without a runnable entry point. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`python-app`** — A runnable Python application — typically `pyproject.toml` declaring `[project.scripts]` or `[tool.poetry.scripts]`, or a top-level `__main__.py`. Distinguished from `python-library` by a clear executable entry point.
- **`dart-library`** — A Dart package exposing a library interface. Recognised by a `pubspec.yaml` whose `lib/` tree is the public surface, with no top-level `bin/` directory.
- **`dart-app`** — A non-Flutter Dart application — `pubspec.yaml` plus a `bin/` directory containing the entry point. Use `flutter-app` when the manifest depends on the Flutter SDK.
- **`flutter-app`** — A Flutter application targeting iOS, Android, web, or desktop. Recognised by `pubspec.yaml` declaring `flutter:` as a top-level key and depending on the `flutter` SDK. Distinct from `dart-app` because Flutter has its own build/deploy chain.
- **`dotnet-library`** — A .NET class library — recognised by a `.csproj` / `.fsproj` / `.vbproj` whose `<OutputType>` is `Library` (the default) and which has no `Main` entry point.
- **`dotnet-service`** — A .NET long-running service — recognised by a project file whose `<OutputType>` is `Exe` and which uses a service host (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.App` framework reference, or a `Program.cs` calling `IHostBuilder`).
- **`website`** — A framework-agnostic static or server-rendered web frontend (e.g., a docs site, a Hugo / Jekyll / Astro project) that does not declare itself as a React app. Use `react-app` for React frontends.
- **`service`** — A long-running server process whose internal language is either unknown or not yet captured by a more specific `<lang>-service` kind — typically a Dockerised opaque binary or a polyglot server.
- **`docker-image`** — A directory rooted at a single `Dockerfile` that builds one container image. The image's contents may themselves be a separate component (e.g., a `node-cli` packaged into the image); this kind describes the packaging artefact.
- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.
- **`shell-scripts`** — A directory of standalone shell scripts (bash, zsh, sh) treated as one component. Plural by convention because a single shell file is not itself a component.
- **`sql-scripts`** — A directory of standalone SQL scripts — typically migrations or seed data — treated as one component. Plural by the same convention as `shell-scripts`.
- **`codegen-tool`** — A code-generation tool whose output is consumed by other components — e.g., a schema compiler, a scaffolding generator, or a template processor. The tool's implementation language lives in the `language` field.
- **`config-repo`** — A repository that exists primarily to hold configuration (dotfiles, infrastructure-as-code, declarative deployment specs) rather than code.
- **`docs-repo`** — A repository whose purpose is documentation — prose, reference material, or a specification authored in Markdown or similar.
- **`spec`** — A specification document or collection of documents describing behaviour that other components implement. Distinguished from a docs-repo by its role as a contract rather than reference prose.
- **`external`** — A third-party component referenced from a manifest (e.g., a published crate or npm package). Externals live in `external-components.yaml`, not `components.yaml`.
- **`non-component`** — A directory that looks like a candidate on paper but is confirmed not to be a component — for example, a `.git`-rooted directory whose contents are just metadata, or an empty placeholder.
","DIR_RELATIVE":"realm-api","LIFECYCLE_SCOPES":"- **`design`** — Design-time activities — specifications, architecture documents, RFCs.
- **`codegen`** — Code generation — schema compilers, scaffolding tools, template processors.
- **`build`** — Build-time activities — compilation, linking, packaging.
- **`test`** — Test-time activities — unit, integration, or acceptance tests.
- **`deploy`** — Deploy-time activities — packaging, provisioning, orchestration.
- **`runtime`** — Runtime activities — the live behaviour of the deployed system.
- **`dev-workflow`** — Developer workflow — tools used during development but not at runtime.
","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"realm-api/Dockerfile":"#See https://aka.ms/customizecontainer to learn how to customize your debug container and how Visual Studio uses this Dockerfile to build your images for faster debugging.

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS base

WORKDIR /app

EXPOSE 80

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build

WORKDIR /app

#COPY realm_api/Dull.RealmAPI ./realm_api/Dull.RealmAPI
#COPY shared-backend ./shared-backend

COPY Dull.RealmAPI ./realm_api/Dull.RealmAPI
COPY --from=shared_backend . ./shared-backend

RUN dotnet build "./realm_api/Dull.RealmAPI/Dull.RealmAPI/Dull.RealmAPI.csproj" -c Release -o /app/build

FROM build AS publish

WORKDIR /app

RUN dotnet publish "./realm_api/Dull.RealmAPI/Dull.RealmAPI/Dull.RealmAPI.csproj" -c Release -o /app/publish /p:UseAppHost=false

FROM base AS final

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=publish /app/publish .

ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "Dull.RealmAPI.dll"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[{"level":1,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Realm API"},{"level":2,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Notes"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"CORS"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Authentication/authorization"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Websocket topics"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Websocket authentication"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Websocket protocol"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Logging"},{"level":3,"path":"realm-api/README.md","text":"Extending with additional tables"}],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["realm-api/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[]}}

- **`rust-proc-macro`** — A Rust procedural-macro crate. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[lib] proc-macro = true`. Compile-time only; the crate ships no runtime artefact, only a compiler plugin consumed by other crates at build time.
- **`workspace`** — A multi-crate (or multi-package) workspace root. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]`, a `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or similar workspace manifests. The root is a component in its own right; its members are child components.
- **`node-library`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package exposing a library interface. Recognised by `package.json` with a `main` or `exports` field and no `bin` field. Whether the source is JS or TS is recorded in the `language` field, not the kind.
- **`node-cli`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package that installs one or more CLI executables. Recognised by `package.json` with a `bin` field.
- **`react-app`** — A React-based frontend application — typically a Create React App, Next.js, Remix, or Vite-based React project. Distinguished from `website` by an explicit React-framework manifest or a top-level `react` / `react-dom` runtime dependency. Use `website` for framework-agnostic frontends.
- **`react-library`** — A library of reusable React components, distributed as an npm package. Recognised by `package.json` declaring `react` as a `peerDependency` plus a library-style entry point.
- **`python-library`** — A Python library package — recognised by `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` without a runnable entry point. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`python-app`** — A runnable Python application — typically `pyproject.toml` declaring `[project.scripts]` or `[tool.poetry.scripts]`, or a top-level `__main__.py`. Distinguished from `python-library` by a clear executable entry point.
- **`dart-library`** — A Dart package exposing a library interface. Recognised by a `pubspec.yaml` whose `lib/` tree is the public surface, with no top-level `bin/` directory.
- **`dart-app`** — A non-Flutter Dart application — `pubspec.yaml` plus a `bin/` directory containing the entry point. Use `flutter-app` when the manifest depends on the Flutter SDK.
- **`flutter-app`** — A Flutter application targeting iOS, Android, web, or desktop. Recognised by `pubspec.yaml` declaring `flutter:` as a top-level key and depending on the `flutter` SDK. Distinct from `dart-app` because Flutter has its own build/deploy chain.
- **`dotnet-library`** — A .NET class library — recognised by a `.csproj` / `.fsproj` / `.vbproj` whose `<OutputType>` is `Library` (the default) and which has no `Main` entry point.
- **`dotnet-service`** — A .NET long-running service — recognised by a project file whose `<OutputType>` is `Exe` and which uses a service host (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.App` framework reference, or a `Program.cs` calling `IHostBuilder`).
- **`website`** — A framework-agnostic static or server-rendered web frontend (e.g., a docs site, a Hugo / Jekyll / Astro project) that does not declare itself as a React app. Use `react-app` for React frontends.
- **`service`** — A long-running server process whose internal language is either unknown or not yet captured by a more specific `<lang>-service` kind — typically a Dockerised opaque binary or a polyglot server.
- **`docker-image`** — A directory rooted at a single `Dockerfile` that builds one container image. The image's contents may themselves be a separate component (e.g., a `node-cli` packaged into the image); this kind describes the packaging artefact.
- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.
- **`shell-scripts`** — A directory of standalone shell scripts (bash, zsh, sh) treated as one component. Plural by convention because a single shell file is not itself a component.
- **`sql-scripts`** — A directory of standalone SQL scripts — typically migrations or seed data — treated as one component. Plural by the same convention as `shell-scripts`.
- **`codegen-tool`** — A code-generation tool whose output is consumed by other components — e.g., a schema compiler, a scaffolding generator, or a template processor. The tool's implementation language lives in the `language` field.
- **`config-repo`** — A repository that exists primarily to hold configuration (dotfiles, infrastructure-as-code, declarative deployment specs) rather than code.
- **`docs-repo`** — A repository whose purpose is documentation — prose, reference material, or a specification authored in Markdown or similar.
- **`spec`** — A specification document or collection of documents describing behaviour that other components implement. Distinguished from a docs-repo by its role as a contract rather than reference prose.
- **`external`** — A third-party component referenced from a manifest (e.g., a published crate or npm package). Externals live in `external-components.yaml`, not `components.yaml`.
- **`non-component`** — A directory that looks like a candidate on paper but is confirmed not to be a component — for example, a `.git`-rooted directory whose contents are just metadata, or an empty placeholder.
","DIR_RELATIVE":"secure-file-scanner","LIFECYCLE_SCOPES":"- **`design`** — Design-time activities — specifications, architecture documents, RFCs.
- **`codegen`** — Code generation — schema compilers, scaffolding tools, template processors.
- **`build`** — Build-time activities — compilation, linking, packaging.
- **`test`** — Test-time activities — unit, integration, or acceptance tests.
- **`deploy`** — Deploy-time activities — packaging, provisioning, orchestration.
- **`runtime`** — Runtime activities — the live behaviour of the deployed system.
- **`dev-workflow`** — Developer workflow — tools used during development but not at runtime.
","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"secure-file-scanner/Dockerfile":"﻿FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS builder
WORKDIR /App
COPY ./Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI ./secure-file-scanner/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI
COPY --from=shared-backend . ./shared-backend
WORKDIR /App/secure-file-scanner/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI
RUN dotnet restore
RUN dotnet publish -o /App/out

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install only ClamAV (no daemon)
RUN apt-get update && \
  apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends clamav && \
  rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Disable clamd notification in freshclam.conf by commenting it out (since we don't have clamd)
#    Setting "NotifyClamd false" didn't work, because that made freshclam think 'false' is a path - open to better solutions
RUN sed -i 's/^NotifyClamd/# NotifyClamd/' /etc/clamav/freshclam.conf || echo "NotifyClamd already disabled"

# Prepare writable dirs for UID 1000
#    - /var/log/clamav : freshclam log
#    - /var/lib/clamav : signature DB + freshclam.dat
RUN mkdir -p /var/log/clamav /var/lib/clamav && \
  touch /var/log/clamav/freshclam.log && \
  chown -R 1000:1000 /var/log/clamav /var/lib/clamav

WORKDIR /App
COPY --from=builder /App/out .
COPY --from=builder /App/secure-file-scanner/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI/appsettings.json .

ENTRYPOINT [ "/App/Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI" ]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["secure-file-scanner/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"secure-file-scanner/clamav_update.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"secure-file-scanner/download_clamav_artifacts.sh"}]}}

response.rationale: This directory contains a Dockerfile that builds a containerized .NET service combining the Dull.SecureFileScannerAPI (a C# dotnet application) with ClamAV malware scanning. The Dockerfile is the primary artefact, with supporting bash scripts for ClamAV maintenance, making this a docker-image component.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"secure-file-transfer/Dockerfile":"﻿FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS base
WORKDIR /app
EXPOSE 80

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build
WORKDIR /app

COPY ./Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/*.csproj ./secure-file-transfer/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/
COPY --from=shared_backend . ./shared-backend

RUN dotnet restore "./secure-file-transfer/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI.csproj"

COPY Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI ./secure-file-transfer/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI
RUN dotnet build "./secure-file-transfer/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI.csproj" -c Release -o /app/build

FROM build AS publish
WORKDIR /app
RUN dotnet publish "./secure-file-transfer/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI/Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI.csproj" -c Release -o /app/publish /p:UseAppHost=false

FROM base AS final
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=publish /app/publish .
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI.dll"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["secure-file-transfer/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[]}}

response.rationale: A Dockerfile that builds and packages a .NET service (Dull.SecureFileTransferAPI) into a container image. The multi-stage build compiles a C# project and produces a dotnet executable entry point for runtime deployment.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"session-recording-server/Dockerfile":"# WARNING: this file is no longer used and the version used in realm provisioning lives in 
# the root of the project directory with the other docker files

FROM buildpack-deps:noble AS nginx-build
WORKDIR /build
RUN echo test
RUN apt update && \
    apt -y full-upgrade && \
    apt -y install build-essential libpcre3 libpcre3-dev libssl-dev zlib1g-dev git && \
    git clone https://github.com/sergey-dryabzhinsky/nginx-rtmp-module.git -b f615a9422c15ca785788ce34df6eae272d6909b6 && \
    wget https://nginx.org/download/nginx-1.26.0.tar.gz && \
    tar -xf nginx-1.26.0.tar.gz && \
    cd nginx-1.26.0 && \
    ./configure --with-http_mp4_module --with-http_auth_request_module --add-module=../nginx-rtmp-module && \
    make CFLAGS="-Wno-error=deprecated-declarations"

FROM rust:1-slim-bookworm AS policyapi-build
WORKDIR /build
COPY policy_api /build
RUN cargo build --release

FROM ubuntu:noble 
ARG NGINX_VERSION=nginx-1.26.0
RUN apt -y update && \
    apt -y full-upgrade && \
    apt -y install make libc6 libcrypt1 libpcre2-8-0 libssl3t64 zlib1g iproute2 curl gettext-base

COPY --from=nginx-build /build/nginx-1.26.0 /build/nginx-1.26.0
COPY --from=nginx-build /build/nginx-rtmp-module /build/nginx-rtmp-module
COPY entrypoint.sh /app/entrypoint.sh
COPY check_ports.sh /app/check_ports.sh
COPY --from=policyapi-build /build/target/release/policy_api /app

EXPOSE 80
EXPOSE 1980
EXPOSE 1935

RUN cd /build/nginx-1.26.0 && \
    ls -la ../ && \
    make install && \
    mkdir /var/lock/nginx && \
    rm -rf /build 

WORKDIR /app

CMD ["/app/entrypoint.sh"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[{"level":1,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Nginx Docker Container for Session Recording"},{"level":2,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Overview"},{"level":2,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Usage"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Building policy_api"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Building container"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Invocation of policy_api"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Expected configuration"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/README.md","text":"Further work"},{"level":1,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Session recording back end - policy API service"},{"level":2,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Overview"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Endpoints provided"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Further work"}],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["session-recording-server/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"session-recording-server/check_ports.sh"},{"interpreter":"/bin/bash","path":"session-recording-server/entrypoint.sh"}]}}

response.rationale: This is a Docker image definition that packages an Nginx reverse proxy with RTMP support and a custom Rust-based policy API service. The Dockerfile uses multi-stage builds to compile both nginx and the policy_api binary, then bundles them into a runtime container.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"session-recording-server/policy_api/Cargo.toml":"[package]
name = "policy_api"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

# See more keys and their definitions at https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/manifest.html

[dependencies]
hyper = { version = "1.4.1", features = ["http1", "client", "server"] }
hyper-util = { version = "0.1.3", features = ["tokio"] }
http-body-util = "0.1.1"
tokio = { version = "1.37.0", features = ["full"] }
uuid = { version = "1.8.0", features = ["v4", "serde"] }
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
serde_urlencoded = "0.7.1"
chrono = "0.4"
tokio-rustls = "0.22.0"
rustls-native-certs = "0.8.0"
tokio-postgres = { version = "0.7.13", features = ["with-uuid-1"] }
deadpool-postgres = "0.14.1"
thiserror = "2.0.18"
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[{"level":1,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Session recording back end - policy API service"},{"level":2,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Overview"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Endpoints provided"},{"level":3,"path":"session-recording-server/policy_api/README.md","text":"Further work"}],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["session-recording-server/policy_api/Cargo.toml"],"shebangs":[]}}

The dependencies (hyper, tokio, tokio-postgres) and architecture indicate a long-running server process.

- **`rust-cli`** — A Rust crate that produces one or more executable binaries. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/main.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-proc-macro`** — A Rust procedural-macro crate. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[lib] proc-macro = true`. Compile-time only; the crate ships no runtime artefact, only a compiler plugin consumed by other crates at build time.
- **`workspace`** — A multi-crate (or multi-package) workspace root. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]`, a `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or similar workspace manifests. The root is a component in its own right; its members are child components.
- **`node-library`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package exposing a library interface. Recognised by `package.json` with a `main` or `exports` field and no `bin` field. Whether the source is JS or TS is recorded in the `language` field, not the kind.
- **`node-cli`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package that installs one or more CLI executables. Recognised by `package.json` with a `bin` field.
- **`react-app`** — A React-based frontend application — typically a Create React App, Next.js, Remix, or Vite-based React project. Distinguished from `website` by an explicit React-framework manifest or a top-level `react` / `react-dom` runtime dependency. Use `website` for framework-agnostic frontends.
- **`react-library`** — A library of reusable React components, distributed as an npm package. Recognised by `package.json` declaring `react` as a `peerDependency` plus a library-style entry point.
- **`python-library`** — A Python library package — recognised by `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` without a runnable entry point. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`python-app`** — A runnable Python application — typically `pyproject.toml` declaring `[project.scripts]` or `[tool.poetry.scripts]`, or a top-level `__main__.py`. Distinguished from `python-library` by a clear executable entry point.
- **`dart-library`** — A Dart package exposing a library interface. Recognised by a `pubspec.yaml` whose `lib/` tree is the public surface, with no top-level `bin/` directory.
- **`dart-app`** — A non-Flutter Dart application — `pubspec.yaml` plus a `bin/` directory containing the entry point. Use `flutter-app` when the manifest depends on the Flutter SDK.
- **`flutter-app`** — A Flutter application targeting iOS, Android, web, or desktop. Recognised by `pubspec.yaml` declaring `flutter:` as a top-level key and depending on the `flutter` SDK. Distinct from `dart-app` because Flutter has its own build/deploy chain.
- **`dotnet-library`** — A .NET class library — recognised by a `.csproj` / `.fsproj` / `.vbproj` whose `<OutputType>` is `Library` (the default) and which has no `Main` entry point.
- **`dotnet-service`** — A .NET long-running service — recognised by a project file whose `<OutputType>` is `Exe` and which uses a service host (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.App` framework reference, or a `Program.cs` calling `IHostBuilder`).
- **`website`** — A framework-agnostic static or server-rendered web frontend (e.g., a docs site, a Hugo / Jekyll / Astro project) that does not declare itself as a React app. Use `react-app` for React frontends.
- **`service`** — A long-running server process whose internal language is either unknown or not yet captured by a more specific `<lang>-service` kind — typically a Dockerised opaque binary or a polyglot server.
- **`docker-image`** — A directory rooted at a single `Dockerfile` that builds one container image. The image's contents may themselves be a separate component (e.g., a `node-cli` packaged into the image); this kind describes the packaging artefact.
- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.
- **`shell-scripts`** — A directory of standalone shell scripts (bash, zsh, sh) treated as one component. Plural by convention because a single shell file is not itself a component.
- **`sql-scripts`** — A directory of standalone SQL scripts — typically migrations or seed data — treated as one component. Plural by the same convention as `shell-scripts`.
- **`codegen-tool`** — A code-generation tool whose output is consumed by other components — e.g., a schema compiler, a scaffolding generator, or a template processor. The tool's implementation language lives in the `language` field.
- **`config-repo`** — A repository that exists primarily to hold configuration (dotfiles, infrastructure-as-code, declarative deployment specs) rather than code.
- **`docs-repo`** — A repository whose purpose is documentation — prose, reference material, or a specification authored in Markdown or similar.
- **`spec`** — A specification document or collection of documents describing behaviour that other components implement. Distinguished from a docs-repo by its role as a contract rather than reference prose.
- **`external`** — A third-party component referenced from a manifest (e.g., a published crate or npm package). Externals live in `external-components.yaml`, not `components.yaml`.
- **`non-component`** — A directory that looks like a candidate on paper but is confirmed not to be a component — for example, a `.git`-rooted directory whose contents are just metadata, or an empty placeholder.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"tools/MSI-Scanner/Dockerfile":"FROM public.ecr.aws/debian/debian:stable-slim
RUN apt update && \
    apt -y full-upgrade && \
    apt -y install osslsigncode \
         python3 \
         python3-pip \
         python3-virtualenv \
         7zip && \
    cd / && \ 
    python3 -m virtualenv --python=python3 msiscanner && \
    cd /msiscanner && \
    ls -laR && \
    . bin/activate && \
    /msiscanner/bin/python -m pip install requests boto3 awslambdaric

WORKDIR /msiscanner 

COPY src/sigcheck.py .
ENTRYPOINT [ "/msiscanner/bin/python", "-m", "awslambdaric" ]
CMD [ "sigcheck.handler" ]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["tools/MSI-Scanner/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[]}}

response.rationale: A Dockerfile at the root of the directory defines a container image that bundles a Python-based MSI security scanner (sigcheck) configured as an AWS Lambda function. The image is a packaging artefact for deployment rather than a standalone application component.

# We need to *modify* the default Nginx-container entrypoint.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"tools/docker-network-services/rdp/Dockerfile":"ARG TAG=noble
FROM ubuntu:$TAG AS builder

RUN apt-get update && \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND="noninteractive" apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    autoconf \
    build-essential \
    ca-certificates \
    dpkg-dev \
    git \
    sudo && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Build latest xrdp from source
RUN git clone https://github.com/neutrinolabs/xrdp.git
WORKDIR /xrdp
RUN ./scripts/install_xrdp_build_dependencies_with_apt.sh max && \
    git checkout 5d8901d5df970ebdb5727ab451ed14091118ed66 && \
    ./bootstrap && \
    ./configure && \
    make -j8 && \
    make install

WORKDIR /

# Build latest xorgxrdp from source
RUN git clone https://github.com/neutrinolabs/xorgxrdp.git
WORKDIR /xorgxrdp
RUN ./scripts/install_xorgxrdp_build_dependencies_with_apt.sh && \
    git checkout b32e8c6fbc1a4a42a774d9e4cb2774ebf9bbac9a && \
    ./bootstrap && \
    ./configure && \
    make -j8 && \
    make install

# Build the final image
FROM ubuntu:$TAG

RUN apt-get update && \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND="noninteractive" apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    dbus-x11 \
    git \
    locales \
    pavucontrol \
    software-properties-common \
    sudo \
    vim \
    x11-xserver-utils \
    xfce4 \
    xfce4-goodies \
    xubuntu-icon-theme \
    xserver-xorg-core && \
    add-apt-repository -y ppa:mozillateam/ppa && \
    echo "Package: *"  > /etc/apt/preferences.d/mozilla-firefox && \
    echo "Pin: release o=LP-PPA-mozillateam" >> /etc/apt/preferences.d/mozilla-firefox && \
    echo "Pin-Priority: 1001" >> /etc/apt/preferences.d/mozilla-firefox && \
    apt-get update && \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND="noninteractive" apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends firefox && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* && \
    deluser --remove-home ubuntu && \
    locale-gen en_US.UTF-8

COPY --from=builder /etc/X11/xrdp /etc/X11/xrdp
COPY --from=builder /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/drivers/
COPY --from=builder /usr/lib/xorg/modules/input/* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/input/
COPY --from=builder /usr/lib/xorg/modules/libxorgxrdp* /usr/lib/xorg/modules/
COPY --from=builder /usr/local /usr/local
COPY --from=builder /etc/xrdp /etc/xrdp

ENV LANG=en_US.UTF-8
COPY entrypoint.sh /usr/bin/entrypoint
EXPOSE 3389/tcp
ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/entrypoint"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["tools/docker-network-services/rdp/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/rdp/entrypoint.sh"}]}}

response.rationale: A single Dockerfile that builds a containerized RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) server based on Ubuntu with xrdp and xorgxrdp compiled from source, accompanied by a bash entrypoint script. This is a clear docker-image component with a specific runtime purpose.

WORKDIR /pyenv
RUN apt -y update && \
	apt -y full-upgrade && \
	apt -y install  python3 python3-pip python3-virtualenv && \
	cd / && \
	python3 -m virtualenv --python=python3 pyenv && \

	. bin/activate && \
	pip install twisted

COPY server.py .
CMD /pyenv/entry-point.sh

","DIR_RELATIVE":"tools/docker-network-services/ssh","LIFECYCLE_SCOPES":"- **`design`** — Design-time activities — specifications, architecture documents, RFCs.
- **`codegen`** — Code generation — schema compilers, scaffolding tools, template processors.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"tools/docker-network-services/ssh/Dockerfile":"FROM debian:trixie-slim
RUN apt -y update && \
	apt -y full-upgrade && \
	apt -y install openssh-server openssl && \
	ssh-keygen -A && \
        chmod 0700 /root/.ssh && \
        echo "root:$(openssl rand 96 | openssl enc -A -base64)" | chpasswd && \
        ln -s /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub /root/.ssh/authorized_keys && \
	echo "PasswordAuthentication yes\nPermitEmptyPasswords no" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/passwdauth.conf && \
	adduser --disabled-password insecure01 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure02 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure03 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure04 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure05 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure06 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure07 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure08 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure09 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure10 && \
	echo "insecure01:password01" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure02:password02" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure03:password03" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure04:password04" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure05:password05" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure06:password06" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure07:password07" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure08:password08" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure09:password09" | chpasswd && \
        echo "insecure10:password10" | chpasswd && \
	mkdir -p /var/run/sshd && \ 
	mkdir -p /root/.ssh 
WORKDIR /dull
COPY entry-point.sh / 
CMD /entry-point.sh
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["tools/docker-network-services/ssh/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/bin/bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/ssh/entry-point.sh"}]}}

response.rationale: This directory contains a single Dockerfile that builds a container image providing an SSH service with pre-configured test users, along with an entry-point script. It is a packaging artefact that produces one container image for use in a docker-compose or similar deployment.

- **`rust-cli`** — A Rust crate that produces one or more executable binaries. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/main.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-proc-macro`** — A Rust procedural-macro crate. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[lib] proc-macro = true`. Compile-time only; the crate ships no runtime artefact, only a compiler plugin consumed by other crates at build time.
- **`workspace`** — A multi-crate (or multi-package) workspace root. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]`, a `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or similar workspace manifests. The root is a component in its own right; its members are child components.
- **`node-library`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package exposing a library interface. Recognised by `package.json` with a `main` or `exports` field and no `bin` field. Whether the source is JS or TS is recorded in the `language` field, not the kind.
- **`node-cli`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package that installs one or more CLI executables. Recognised by `package.json` with a `bin` field.

Distinguished from `website` by an explicit React-framework manifest or a top-level `react` / `react-dom` runtime dependency. Use `website` for framework-agnostic frontends.
- **`react-library`** — A library of reusable React components, distributed as an npm package. Recognised by `package.json` declaring `react` as a `peerDependency` plus a library-style entry point.
- **`python-library`** — A Python library package — recognised by `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` without a runnable entry point. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`python-app`** — A runnable Python application — typically `pyproject.toml` declaring `[project.scripts]` or `[tool.poetry.scripts]`, or a top-level `__main__.py`. Distinguished from `python-library` by a clear executable entry point.
- **`dart-library`** — A Dart package exposing a library interface. Recognised by a `pubspec.yaml` whose `lib/` tree is the public surface, with no top-level `bin/` directory.
- **`dart-app`** — A non-Flutter Dart application — `pubspec.yaml` plus a `bin/` directory containing the entry point. Use `flutter-app` when the manifest depends on the Flutter SDK.
- **`flutter-app`** — A Flutter application targeting iOS, Android, web, or desktop. Recognised by `pubspec.yaml` declaring `flutter:` as a top-level key and depending on the `flutter` SDK. Distinct from `dart-app` because Flutter has its own build/deploy chain.

- **`dotnet-service`** — A .NET long-running service — recognised by a project file whose `<OutputType>` is `Exe` and which uses a service host (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.App` framework reference, or a `Program.cs` calling `IHostBuilder`).

- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"tools/docker-network-services/telnet/Dockerfile":"FROM debian:trixie-slim
RUN apt -y update && \
	apt -y full-upgrade && \
	apt -y install telnetd adduser && \
	echo 'telnet	stream	tcp	nowait	root	/usr/sbin/tcpd	/usr/sbin/telnetd' > /etc/inetd.conf && \
	adduser --disabled-password insecure01 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure02 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure03 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure04 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure05 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure06 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure07 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure08 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure09 && \
        adduser --disabled-password insecure10 && \
	echo "insecure01:password01" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure02:password02" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure03:password03" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure04:password04" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure05:password05" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure06:password06" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure07:password07" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure08:password08" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure09:password09" | chpasswd && \ 
        echo "insecure10:password10" | chpasswd  
WORKDIR /dull
COPY ./entry-point.sh /
CMD ["/entry-point.sh"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["tools/docker-network-services/telnet/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/bin/bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/telnet/entry-point.sh"}]}}

","MANIFEST_CONTENTS":{"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/Dockerfile":"FROM debian:12


# noVNC webport, connect via http://IP:6901/?password=vncpassword
ENV DISPLAY=:1 \
    VNC_PORT=5901 \
    NO_VNC_PORT=6901
EXPOSE $VNC_PORT $NO_VNC_PORT

### Envrionment config
ENV HOME=/headless \
    TERM=xterm \
    STARTUPDIR=/dockerstartup \
    INST_SCRIPTS=/headless/install \
    NO_VNC_HOME=/headless/noVNC \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    VNC_COL_DEPTH=24 \
    VNC_RESOLUTION=1280x1024 \
    VNC_PW=vncpassword \
    VNC_VIEW_ONLY=false
WORKDIR $HOME

### Add all install scripts for further steps
ADD ./src/common/install/ $INST_SCRIPTS/
ADD ./src/debian/install/ $INST_SCRIPTS/

### Install some common tools
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/tools.sh
ENV LANG='en_US.UTF-8' LANGUAGE='en_US:en' LC_ALL='en_US.UTF-8'

### Install custom fonts
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/install_custom_fonts.sh

### Install xvnc-server & noVNC - HTML5 based VNC viewer
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/tigervnc.sh
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/no_vnc.sh

### Install firefox and chrome browser
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/firefox.sh
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/chrome.sh

### Install xfce UI
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/xfce_ui.sh
ADD ./src/common/xfce/ $HOME/

### configure startup
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/libnss_wrapper.sh
ADD ./src/common/scripts $STARTUPDIR
RUN $INST_SCRIPTS/set_user_permission.sh $STARTUPDIR $HOME

RUN mkdir -p /dull
RUN useradd -ms /bin/bash vncuser \
    && apt install -y sudo \
    && usermod -aG sudo vncuser \
    && echo "vncuser ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /dull/dull" >> /etc/sudoers

USER root
ENTRYPOINT ["/dockerstartup/vnc_startup.sh"]
CMD ["--wait"]
"},"RATIONALE_BUNDLE":{"doc_headings":[],"is_git_root":false,"manifests":["tools/docker-network-services/vnc/Dockerfile"],"shebangs":[{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/install/firefox.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/install/no_vnc.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/install/set_user_permission.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/scripts/chrome-init.sh"},{"interpreter":"/bin/bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/scripts/vnc_startup.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/common/xfce/wm_startup.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/icewm/wm_startup.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/chrome.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/icewm_ui.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/install_custom_fonts.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/libnss_wrapper.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/tigervnc.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/tools.sh"},{"interpreter":"/usr/bin/env bash","path":"tools/docker-network-services/vnc/src/debian/install/xfce_ui.sh"}]}}

response.rationale: A single Dockerfile at the root of the directory defining a container image that packages a VNC server with desktop environment (xfce/icewm), browsers, and noVNC web interface. The supporting shell scripts in src/ are build-time installation helpers pulled into the image.

entries.inputs:
{"COMPONENT_KINDS":"- **`rust-library`** — A Rust crate exposing a library interface. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[lib]` section with no `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/lib.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-cli`** — A Rust crate that produces one or more executable binaries. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` containing a `[[bin]]` section, or by `src/main.rs` alongside a manifest. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`rust-proc-macro`** — A Rust procedural-macro crate. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[lib] proc-macro = true`. Compile-time only; the crate ships no runtime artefact, only a compiler plugin consumed by other crates at build time.
- **`workspace`** — A multi-crate (or multi-package) workspace root. Recognised by `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]`, a `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or similar workspace manifests. The root is a component in its own right; its members are child components.
- **`node-library`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package exposing a library interface. Recognised by `package.json` with a `main` or `exports` field and no `bin` field. Whether the source is JS or TS is recorded in the `language` field, not the kind.
- **`node-cli`** — A Node.js / TypeScript package that installs one or more CLI executables. Recognised by `package.json` with a `bin` field.
- **`react-app`** — A React-based frontend application — typically a Create React App, Next.js, Remix, or Vite-based React project. Distinguished from `website` by an explicit React-framework manifest or a top-level `react` / `react-dom` runtime dependency. Use `website` for framework-agnostic frontends.
- **`react-library`** — A library of reusable React components, distributed as an npm package. Recognised by `package.json` declaring `react` as a `peerDependency` plus a library-style entry point.
- **`python-library`** — A Python library package — recognised by `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` without a runnable entry point. Typical lifecycles: build, runtime.
- **`python-app`** — A runnable Python application — typically `pyproject.toml` declaring `[project.scripts]` or `[tool.poetry.scripts]`, or a top-level `__main__.py`. Distinguished from `python-library` by a clear executable entry point.
- **`dart-library`** — A Dart package exposing a library interface. Recognised by a `pubspec.yaml` whose `lib/` tree is the public surface, with no top-level `bin/` directory.
- **`dart-app`** — A non-Flutter Dart application — `pubspec.yaml` plus a `bin/` directory containing the entry point. Use `flutter-app` when the manifest depends on the Flutter SDK.
- **`flutter-app`** — A Flutter application targeting iOS, Android, web, or desktop. Recognised by `pubspec.yaml` declaring `flutter:` as a top-level key and depending on the `flutter` SDK. Distinct from `dart-app` because Flutter has its own build/deploy chain.
- **`dotnet-library`** — A .NET class library — recognised by a `.csproj` / `.fsproj` / `.vbproj` whose `<OutputType>` is `Library` (the default) and which has no `Main` entry point.
- **`dotnet-service`** — A .NET long-running service — recognised by a project file whose `<OutputType>` is `Exe` and which uses a service host (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.App` framework reference, or a `Program.cs` calling `IHostBuilder`).
- **`website`** — A framework-agnostic static or server-rendered web frontend (e.g., a docs site, a Hugo / Jekyll / Astro project) that does not declare itself as a React app. Use `react-app` for React frontends.
- **`service`** — A long-running server process whose internal language is either unknown or not yet captured by a more specific `<lang>-service` kind — typically a Dockerised opaque binary or a polyglot server.
- **`docker-image`** — A directory rooted at a single `Dockerfile` that builds one container image. The image's contents may themselves be a separate component (e.g., a `node-cli` packaged into the image); this kind describes the packaging artefact.
- **`docker-compose-bundle`** — A directory whose primary artefact is a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yaml`) declaring a multi-image deployment. Used for grouping a set of related services into one component.
- **`installer`** — A directory whose purpose is producing an end-user installer package — e.g., a WiX MSI definition, a `.dmg` build script, a Debian `.deb` packaging tree, or an RPM spec. Distinct from `docker-image` because installers target host installation rather than container runtime.
- **`shell-scripts`** — A directory of standalone shell scripts (bash, zsh, sh) treated as one component. Plural by convention because a single shell file is not itself a component.
- **`sql-scripts`** — A directory of standalone SQL scripts — typically migrations or seed data — treated as one component. Plural by the same convention as `shell-scripts`.
- **`codegen-tool`** — A code-generation tool whose output is consumed by other components — e.g., a schema compiler, a scaffolding generator, or a template processor. The tool's implementation language lives in the `language` field.
- **`config-repo`** — A repository that exists primarily to hold configuration (dotfiles, infrastructure-as-code, declarative deployment specs) rather than code.
- **`docs-repo`** — A repository whose purpose is documentation — prose, reference material, or a specification authored in Markdown or similar.
- **`spec`** — A specification document or collection of documents describing behaviour that other components implement. Distinguished from a docs-repo by its role as a contract rather than reference prose.
- **`external`** — A third-party component referenced from a manifest (e.g., a published crate or npm package). Externals live in `external-components.yaml`, not `components.yaml`.
- **`non-component`** — A directory that looks like a candidate on paper but is confirmed not to be a component — for example, a `.git`-rooted directory whose contents are just metadata, or an empty placeholder.

- **`codegen`** — Code generation — schema compilers, scaffolding tools, template processors.
- **`build`** — Build-time activities — compilation, linking, packaging.
- **`test`** — Test-time activities — unit, integration, or acceptance tests.
- **`deploy`** — Deploy-time activities — packaging, provisioning, orchestration.
- **`runtime`** — Runtime activities — the live behaviour of the deployed system.
- **`dev-workflow`** — Developer workflow — tools used during development but not at runtime.

RUN pip install psycopg2 python-dateutil

response.notes: The pipeline is organized as 10 composable Salsa query layers (L0=filesystem inputs, L1=file enumeration/manifests/doc headings, L2=candidate component generation, L3=LLM classification, L4=tree assembly and id allocation, L5=surface extraction, L6=edge proposal, L7=graph-structural analysis, L8=sub-carve recursion and fixedpoint driver, L9=YAML projection and cache persistence). All output files are written atomically. Exit codes: 0=success, 1=general error, 2=LLM budget exhausted (no output written), 3=LLM backend setup failed (no output written). The evaluation/harness crate is intentionally isolated from atlas-engine and atlas-llm. LLM backend selection and per-operation routing is driven by .atlas/config.yaml; the claude subprocess is invoked as: claude -p <prompt> --output-format stream-json --model <id>. The codex subprocess is invoked with --sandbox read-only --ephemeral flags. The llm-cache.json is schema-versioned and content-addressed by prompt SHA for deterministic re-runs.
response.purpose: Atlas is a Rust-based codebase analysis and design recovery tool. It ingests an arbitrary filesystem tree (with optional .gitignore filtering), discovers component boundaries via a 10-layer Salsa-backed query graph (L0–L9), classifies components using LLM-driven analysis, extracts per-component interaction surfaces (Stage 1), proposes inter-component edges (Stage 2), and emits four atomic YAML artifacts plus a content-addressed LLM response cache for reproducible re-runs. It supports multiple LLM backends (Claude Code subprocess, Codex subprocess, Anthropic HTTP, OpenAI HTTP, OpenRouter HTTP) and routes different pipeline operations to different providers via a config file.

response.notes: The agent workspace root (agent/Cargo.toml) is a thin Cargo workspace manifest whose members are all under agent/rust-client/. The nss_module directory exists under rust-client but is NOT a declared workspace member in agent/Cargo.toml. The agent connects to the central API exclusively via a named IPC stream called 'dull-web' rather than a routable network address — this stream is provided by the dull/agent-api component. In-place upgrades work by writing an 'Intent' state to the ProcessState table in connector.db3 and then exec()-replacing the running process with the new binary (Unix) or spawning a replacement service (Windows). Three background task categories exist: Replicated* tasks pull from API into local DB; Published* tasks push local state to API; Trimmed*/Flushed*/Batched* tasks handle housekeeping and log shipping. SQLite databases use SQLCipher encryption; the encryption key is derived from device credentials stored in the DB itself. The realm-manager directory (dull/agent/rust-client/realm-manager) and nss-module (dull/agent/rust-client/nss-module) appear in the filesystem under the agent workspace but are not workspace members in agent/Cargo.toml.
response.purpose: The dull/agent component is a Rust workspace (version 2026.3.1, package name dull-agent) implementing a network connector agent daemon. It manages encrypted network segments (TCP/TLS/mTLS tunnels), replicates configuration and certificates from a central realm management API, records session activity, and performs in-place self-upgrades. The agent runs as a system service on Linux (via systemd) and Windows (native service API), authenticates to the central API server over a named IPC stream, subscribes to real-time WebSocket notifications for configuration changes, and persists all state to local SQLite databases encrypted with SQLCipher. It coordinates connection approvals (including MFA flows), maintains hostname/IP mappings, and rotates/flushes diagnostic logs back to the API.

response.notes: connector-lib exposes its API in two layers simultaneously: a raw C FFI layer (extern "C" functions returning C-compatible pointer/integer types for use from any native host) and a higher-level Dart binding layer generated by flutter_rust_bridge 2.x (src/api.rs, targeting dull/connector-lib-dart). The formats/ subdirectory is a collection of RustCrypto ecosystem crates (der, pkcs8, spki, x509-cert, cms, etc.) vendored or forked inside the component; these are compile-time library dependencies, not runtime interactions. The library creates a 4-thread Tokio runtime named 'dull-connector-worker' at startup and tears it down (with a 5 s grace period) at stop(). It reads system socket tables and process metadata via the netstat2 and sysinfo crates plus direct winapi/libc calls — it never opens outbound connections itself; those are opened by agent-core segments it manages. Certificate operations are entirely in-memory using rcgen and rustls; no PEM/DER files are read from or written to disk. Windows service control (SCManager) is supported for querying and toggling service state. Log output to files is the only filesystem write the component performs.
response.purpose: connector-lib is a Rust library that wraps an async Tokio runtime and exposes network-segment lifecycle management to host programs via a C FFI boundary (extern "C" functions) and a Dart/Flutter bridge (flutter_rust_bridge). It orchestrates named segments defined by agent-core's SegmentDefinition type, handles in-memory X.509/TLS certificate generation and storage, introspects OS-level TCP/UDP socket state and per-process identity (username, PID, session), maintains ring-buffer logs and session-event logs accessible to the host, and implements the AmbientApp/AmbientNaming/AmbientDb trait interfaces expected by agent-core so that segments can interact with the host environment without knowing they are embedded in a mobile or desktop application.

response.notes: This is a vendored copy of the open-source RustCrypto/formats GitHub repository embedded as a Cargo workspace at connector-lib/formats/. All 24 member crates are also published independently to crates.io. The workspace uses a global [patch.crates-io] block so that intra-workspace dependency resolution always uses local paths rather than registry versions. Several external RustCrypto crates (p256, ecdsa, cbc, scrypt, whirlpool, salsa20) are also patched to pending pre-release git revisions. The dependency graph within the workspace flows from low-level primitives (base16ct, base32ct, base64ct, const-oid, der, pem-rfc7468) through mid-level key formats (spki, pkcs1, pkcs5, pkcs8, sec1) to high-level protocol structures (cms, x509-cert, x509-ocsp, x509-tsp, cmpv2, crmf, pkcs12, gss-api). The catalog lists each sub-directory as a separate component (e.g. dull/connector-lib/formats/der, dull/connector-lib/formats/x509-cert), plus fuzz sub-crates for tls-codec and x509-cert, a derive-macro sub-crate for der and tls-codec, an OID-database-generator utility under const-oid/oiddbgen, and a test-support helper under x509-cert/test-support. The serdect crate provides constant-time serde helpers bridging base16ct and serde. The tai64 crate implements TAI64(N) timestamp encoding with optional serde and zeroize support.
response.purpose: A Rust Cargo workspace (the RustCrypto Formats project) containing 24 tightly-integrated library crates that implement cryptography-related format encoders and decoders, covering PKCS, PKIX, CMS, X.509, TLS codec, and related standards. The crates range from foundational primitives (constant-time base16/32/64 encoding, ASN.1 DER codec, OID handling) through key-format layers (SPKI, PKCS#1/#5/#8/#12, SEC1) to higher-level certificate and protocol structures (X.509, OCSP, TSP, CMP, CRMF, CMS, GSS-API, TLS codec, TAI64 timestamps). All crates are pure in-memory, no-std-compatible libraries with no networking, file I/O, or external process invocations in production code. They are designed for use as dependencies by other Rust components that need to parse or serialise cryptographic data structures.

response.notes: The oiddbgen subcrate (separately catalogued as dull/connector-lib/formats/const-oid/oiddbgen) embeds RFC/CSV/ASN source files via include_str! at its own compile time and writes generated Rust source to stdout; the developer pipes this through rustfmt into src/db/gen.rs which is committed to the repo. The gen.rs file itself carries the comment '!! DO NOT EDIT !!: This file is auto-generated by oiddbgen.' The db.rs module also explicitly directs contributors to patch oiddbgen to add new OID entries. The library itself is no_std and performs no I/O at runtime; all file consumption is during the code-generation phase run by oiddbgen.
response.purpose: const-oid is a Rust library providing a const-friendly, heapless (no_std) implementation of the ISO/IEC Object Identifier (OID) standard (ITU X.660), with support for BER/DER encoding and decoding. It exposes ObjectIdentifier and ObjectIdentifierRef types, dot-delimited string parsing (including in const contexts without proc macros), BER/DER byte-slice parsing and serialization, arc iteration, and AssociatedOid/DynAssociatedOid traits that let other types statically declare their OID. An optional 'db' feature provides a compiled-in OID name database generated from IANA LDAP parameters, multiple IETF RFCs, NIST FIPS, TCG, and Belarusian STB standards by a companion code-generator tool (oiddbgen) that lives in the same directory tree.

response.notes: The crate is `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` and enforces canonical DER at decode time, rejecting non-canonical BER encodings unless BER mode is explicitly selected via `EncodingRules`. The `derive` feature delegates to the sibling `der/derive` proc-macro crate (catalog id `dull/connector-lib/formats/der/derive`). The `pem` feature integrates with `pem-rfc7468` (catalog id `dull/connector-lib/formats/pem-rfc7468`) to add PEM label-aware readers/writers. The `oid` feature adds `ObjectIdentifier` support via `const-oid` (catalog id `dull/connector-lib/formats/const-oid`). The README explicitly names `x509-cert` and `x509-ocsp` as downstream consumers. No filesystem I/O, network I/O, or subprocess execution occurs anywhere in this crate — all operations are purely in-memory byte-slice transformations.
response.purpose: The `der` crate is a pure-Rust library implementing Distinguished Encoding Rules (DER) and Basic Encoding Rules (BER) for ASN.1 as specified in ITU-T X.690. It provides a comprehensive suite of encoding and decoding traits (`Encode`, `Decode`, `EncodeValue`, `DecodeValue`), reader/writer abstractions, and concrete implementations for all standard ASN.1 primitive and constructed types (integers, strings, OIDs, sequences, sets, etc.). The crate is designed `no_std`-compatible with optional `alloc`/`std` features, making it suitable for embedded and cryptographic applications. It serves as the foundational encoding layer for the broader RustCrypto formats ecosystem (pkcs8, x509-cert, cms, etc.) and exposes procedural derive macros (`Sequence`, `Choice`, `Enumerated`, `ValueOrd`) via an optional `derive` feature backed by the companion `der_derive` proc-macro crate.

response.notes: The crate is `#![no_std]` and supports three optional feature layers: `alloc` (enables owned key types, OtherPrimeInfos, and Encode traits), `pem` (enables PEM serialisation/deserialisation, requires alloc), and `std` (enables filesystem read/write methods via std::path::Path). The optional `pkcs8` feature adds bridging: DecodeRsaPrivateKey and DecodeRsaPublicKey are blanket-implemented for any type implementing the corresponding pkcs8 TryFrom conversions, and EncodeRsaPrivateKey/EncodeRsaPublicKey are blanket-implemented for types implementing pkcs8::EncodePrivateKey/EncodePublicKey. Test fixtures under tests/examples/ include 2048-bit and 4096-bit RSA keys (including a 3-prime variant) in both DER and PEM formats. The `zeroize` feature (pulled through `der/zeroize`) zeroes sensitive key material. File I/O paths are caller-supplied at runtime; no well-known fixed paths are used.
response.purpose: A pure Rust `no_std`-compatible library implementing Public-Key Cryptography Standards (PKCS) #1: RSA Cryptography Specifications Version 2.2 (RFC 8017). It provides ASN.1 DER and PEM encoding/decoding of RSA private keys (RSAPrivateKey), RSA public keys (RSAPublicKey), RSASSA-PSS parameters (RsaPssParams), and RSAES-OAEP parameters (RsaOaepParams). It also provides traits (DecodeRsaPrivateKey, DecodeRsaPublicKey, EncodeRsaPrivateKey, EncodeRsaPublicKey) for loading and saving keys from/to files or byte slices, and optionally bridges into PKCS#8 key wrapping when the `pkcs8` feature is enabled.

response.notes: The crate is `#![no_std]` by default, using `extern crate alloc`; std I/O (Read/Write) traits are gated behind the `std` feature. The `derive` feature pulls in the companion `tls_codec_derive` proc-macro crate (path = ./derive). The `mls` feature restricts QUIC variable-length vector max length to 30-bit values instead of the RFC 9000 default of 62-bit. The `conditional_deserialization` feature enables attribute-driven conditional field deserialization via the derive macro. The `serde` feature adds optional Serde interop. Fuzzing is supported via a separate fuzz crate (./fuzz) using cargo-fuzz. Benchmarks are provided for both tls_vec and quic_vec via Criterion.
response.purpose: tls_codec is a pure Rust library implementing the TLS (de)serialization codec as defined in RFC 8446, with extensions for MLS (Messaging Layer Security) and QUIC variable-length integer encoding (RFC 9000). It provides core traits (Size, Serialize, SerializeBytes, Deserialize, DeserializeBytes) and a rich set of data structures (TlsVec*, TlsSlice*, TlsByteVec*, SecretTlsVec*, VLBytes, VLByteSlice, U24) along with optional procedural-macro derive support for custom struct/enum (de)serialization. The crate is `no_std`-compatible with an optional `std` feature, and includes zeroize support for secret-bearing types.

response.notes: The crate is `no_std` compatible (uses `extern crate alloc`). Optional Cargo features gate major capability blocks: `builder` enables certificate/CSR construction (depends on `async-signature`, `signature`); `sct` enables Certificate Transparency SCT list parsing (depends on `tls_codec`); `pem` enables PEM encode/decode; `hazmat` exposes a `Raw` profile that disables RFC 5280 conformance checks. The companion subcrate at `./test-support` (catalog id `dull/connector-lib/formats/x509-cert/test-support`) provides `openssl` and `zlint` wrappers used only in integration tests, not in the library itself. A `./fuzz` subcrate (catalog id `dull/connector-lib/formats/x509-cert/fuzz`) provides cargo-fuzz targets for `CertReq` and `CertReqInfo` parsing. The Cargo.toml references local workspace crates `const-oid`, `der`, `spki`, and optionally `tls_codec`, which correspond to catalog components `dull/connector-lib/formats/const-oid`, `dull/connector-lib/formats/der`, `dull/connector-lib/formats/spki`, and `dull/connector-lib/formats/tls-codec` respectively, but those names do not appear literally in any README or code comment so they are omitted from `explicit_cross_component_mentions`.
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of the X.509 Public Key Infrastructure Certificate format as described in RFC 5280. The crate provides ASN.1/DER-encoded types for X.509 v1–v3 certificates (`Certificate`, `TbsCertificate`), Certificate Revocation Lists (`CertificateList`, `TbsCertList`), PKCS#10 Certification Signing Requests (`CertReq`, `CertReqInfo`), trust anchors (`TrustAnchorChoice`, `TrustAnchorInfo`, RFC 5914), and the full suite of PKIX extensions (key usage, name constraints, CRL distribution points, AIA, SCT lists, etc.). It also provides builder types (`CertificateBuilder`, `RequestBuilder`) for constructing and cryptographically signing certificates and CSRs using the `signature` crate trait abstraction. A companion test-support subcrate shells out to `openssl` and `zlint` binaries for external certificate validation in tests.

response.notes: The monorepo uses pnpm workspaces with Turborepo for task orchestration. API clients (launcher-api-client, realm-api-client, secure-portal-api-client) are code-generated from live OpenAPI/Swagger JSON endpoints using @hey-api/openapi-ts and openapi-merge-cli; the Makefile starts backend Docker containers (launcherapi, realmapi, secure-file-transfer) before fetching their specs. The regen_models.sh driver script also invokes Dart AOT tools for desktop launcher model generation targeting the connector-lib-dart domain. Feature flags are read from features.json files generated at build time from FLAG__* environment variables. realm-manager and launcher-web both support runtime config injection via window.__APP_CONFIG__ (falling back to import.meta.env VITE_* variables) to allow config without rebuilding Docker images. SignalR WebSocket is used in launcher-web for real-time LauncherSettings updates from the backend. Keycloak authentication (realm: dull-manager) is required by realm-manager, launcher-web, and secure-portal apps. Sentry error tracking is wired in realm-manager, secure-portal, and launcher-web. The shared @dull/ui package is built on React Aria Components for accessibility. Docker multi-stage builds target a 'production' stage; the build-docker-image and push-docker-image shell scripts support feature/latest/stable/release tagging strategies.
response.purpose: A Turborepo-powered frontend monorepo containing all Node.js/TypeScript frontends for the DULL platform. It ships five applications — launcher-web (zero-trust RDP/file-transfer launcher), realm-manager (admin dashboard for users/devices/policies), secure-portal (file upload/download portal), index-page (root navigation), and keycloak-theme (custom Keycloak login UI) — plus ten shared packages (ui, icons, form, shared, generic, config, flags, launcher-api-client, realm-api-client, secure-portal-api-client). API clients are code-generated from OpenAPI specs served by backend services, authenticated via Keycloak JWTs, and apps use SignalR WebSockets for real-time updates.

response.notes: Dev proxy in vite.config.mts routes /secure-file-transfer/api to http://secure-file-transfer:5190/, /secure-file-scanner/api to http://secure-file-scanner:5191/, /launcherapi/api to http://launcherapi:5188/, and /launcherapi/hubs (WebSocket) to ws://launcherapi:5188/ — these hostnames correspond to catalog components dull/secure-file-transfer, dull/secure-file-scanner, and dull/launcher-api respectively, but they are referenced only as proxy targets rather than by catalog name. The rust/ subdirectory (IronRDP/WebRDP) is a sibling within this path scope and its compiled WASM output is consumed at src/wasm/*.wasm; the catalog lists those as separate components (e.g. dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust). Runtime config injection is supported via window.__APP_CONFIG__ (server-side), which overrides Vite build-time env vars (VITE_KEYCLOAK_CLIENT, VITE_KEYCLOAK_LOCATION, VITE_LAUNCHERAPI_URL, etc.). Feature flags JSON is copied from the @dull/flags package at build time via a custom Vite plugin. The Keycloak realm is hardcoded as dull-manager. The callToApi() wrapper in src/api/index.jsx applies SQL escape sanitization to all string-typed API parameters before dispatch. HTTP 412/428 (PreconditionRequired) responses are intercepted globally to trigger MFA or password-change flows before retrying the original request. The Sentry DSN (https://a616a12eb86449bd9562fd311b20e3f7@o419780.ingest.sentry.io/5598008) is hardcoded in src/index.tsx. localStorage key installer_id stores a per-browser UUID used as a launcher identity.
response.purpose: launcher-web is a React-based single-page web application ("Dull Web Launcher") that provides a browser UI for end users to browse and connect to remote access launch targets (RDP, SSH, web sessions), manage secure file transfers, and handle authentication preconditions such as MFA enrollment, MFA challenge flows, and password management. It authenticates via Keycloak OAuth/OIDC, communicates with the Launcher API over both REST and three SignalR WebSocket hubs (for real-time MFA status, user settings, and password-reset status), proxies file operations through dedicated Secure File Transfer and Secure File Scanner services, and reports errors to Sentry. The application is built with Vite, served at the /web-launcher base path, and consumes auto-generated API client code from the launcher-api-client package.

response.notes: The workspace Cargo.toml builds only three members (common, webrdp, IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-async) even though the IronRDP subtree contains many more crates; the others are available as path dependencies but not workspace-managed here. The `ironrdp-web` crate (path `./IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-web`) is a direct [dependency] of the top-level `wasm-rdp` package but is NOT listed as a workspace member. Canvas element IDs `#rdp-container` and `#rdp-canvas` are hardcoded in webrdp, implying the consuming HTML page must supply those elements. The Meet-Subscriber module writes a 'calling card' (publisher topic bytes) over the stream and reads a confirmation before handing the stream to RDP—this is a pub-sub rendezvous protocol specific to the Dull platform. Base64-encoded client key/certificate and JSON segment config are injected at runtime from JavaScript (not read from disk). The build target is unconditionally `wasm32-unknown-unknown` (via .cargo/config.toml). wasm-pack is invoked via cargo-make (Makefile.toml in webrdp/) with `--target web --out-name webrdp`; an install step copies the pkg output and `assets/*` to `$INSTALL_PATH`.
response.purpose: A Rust crate compiled to WebAssembly (cdylib, package name `wasm-rdp`) that implements a modular stream-pipeline RDP client for the browser. It exports JavaScript-callable async functions (`do_connect`, `do_disconnect`, `get_current_allocated_bytes`) via wasm-bindgen. Connection establishment chains configurable named modules—WebSocket transport (WS-Client), mutual-TLS wrapping (MTLS-Client), pub-sub rendezvous (Meet-Subscriber), and IronRDP-based RDP session—driven by a JSON 'segment' configuration supplied by the JavaScript host. In-memory thread-local certificate storage supports client-side X.509 certificate generation and enrollment. The workspace root also declares three sub-crates as workspace members: `common`, `webrdp`, and `IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-async`.

response.notes: The component spans the entire IronRDP workspace at frontend/apps/launcher-web/rust/IronRDP, which contains many sub-crates that are separately catalogued (ironrdp-web, iron-remote-desktop, iron-remote-desktop-rdp, iron-svelte-client, ironrdp-connector, ironrdp-server, ironrdp-pdu, etc.). Network server mode binds TCP (default 3389) with configurable address via RdpServerOptions.addr. Network client mode connects to RDP servers over TCP then upgrades to TLS; the WASM web client uses a WebSocket transport instead of raw TCP. HTTPS is used only for optional KDC proxy (Kerberos auth). Files consumed include .rdp configuration files (parsed by ironrdp-rdpfile), DER-encoded X.509 certificates and RSA private keys for smart card authentication. Files produced include optional log files (--log-file flag), TLS session secrets written to SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable path (for Wireshark decryption), and BMP images in the screenshot example binary. No external processes are spawned. The README references Devolutions Gateway, Devolutions Server, and Devolutions Hub as production consumers, but these are external projects not in the catalog. The SSLKEYLOGFILE feature is gated behind a feature flag in ironrdp-tls.
response.purpose: IronRDP is a collection of Rust crates implementing the Microsoft Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP), with a focus on security. It provides both RDP client and server implementations, WebAssembly (WASM) bindings for web-browser-based remote desktop sessions, virtual channel support (clipboard via CLIPRDR, audio via RDPSND, multi-monitor via DisplayControl, dynamic virtual channels via DRDYNVC), multiple graphics codecs (raw bitmap, RLE, RDP 6.0 compression, Microsoft RemoteFX), and TLS/CredSSP/Kerberos authentication. The component forms the core RDP engine for the launcher-web frontend, exposing WASM bindings consumed by web-based remote desktop UI clients (iron-remote-desktop, iron-svelte-client) and shipping a standalone native CLI client (ironrdp-client).

response.notes: The README explicitly names `ironrdp-web` as the reference consumer. The `make_bridge!` macro is the primary integration surface: implementors call it with their concrete `RemoteDesktopApi` type to emit a full wasm-bindgen WASM API that the iron-remote-desktop JS WebComponent calls. SessionBuilder exposes proxy_address and auth_token builder methods, indicating the consumer WASM module connects to a remote host (potentially via a proxy), but this crate itself establishes no connections — that is left to the concrete implementation. No filesystem I/O or network I/O occurs within this crate.
response.purpose: iron-remote-desktop is a Rust helper library crate for building WebAssembly (WASM) modules that are compatible with the `iron-remote-desktop` WebComponent. It defines a set of Rust traits (`RemoteDesktopApi`, `Session`, `SessionBuilder`, `SessionBuilderExt`, `SessionTerminationInfo`, `DeviceEvent`, `InputTransaction`, `ClipboardData`, `ClipboardItem`, `IronError`) that a concrete remote-desktop protocol implementation must implement. It also provides the `make_bridge!` macro which, given a type implementing `RemoteDesktopApi`, generates all wasm-bindgen-annotated structs and methods that the `iron-remote-desktop` JavaScript WebComponent expects to call from the browser. Logger initialisation (via `tracing-web` to the browser console) is also handled here. The README explicitly points to `ironrdp-web` as the canonical example consumer.
