network_endpoints: rdp://<destination-configurable> (RDP server target, delegated to injected WASM module)
wss://<proxyAddress-configurable> (Devolutions Gateway WebSocket proxy, delegated to injected WASM module)
response.notes: The component is distributed as an npm package (`@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop`, version 0.10.1 in public/package.json). The `public/` subdirectory contains the built distribution (iron-remote-desktop.js + index.d.ts + package.json), corresponding to the catalog entry `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/iron-remote-desktop-2/public`. All actual protocol-level networking (RDP over WebSocket via Devolutions Gateway) is handled entirely by the injected WASM module — the TypeScript layer passes credentials (username, password, authToken JWT), addresses (destination, proxyAddress), and optional parameters (desktopSize, preConnectionBlob, kdc_proxy_url, use_display_control) to the WASM SessionBuilder but performs no direct network I/O itself. Clipboard polling interval is 100ms when full Clipboard API is available. The README notes that `iron-svelte-client` is the recommended environment for testing changes to this component. Build uses Vite with vite-plugin-wasm and vite-plugin-top-level-await to support WASM module loading. KDC proxy URL parameter supports MS-KKDCP (Kerberos Key Distribution Center Proxy Protocol) as documented in Microsoft's learn.microsoft.com specification.
response.purpose: A TypeScript/Svelte reusable web component library (`@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop`, npm package `@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop`) that provides a backend-agnostic `<iron-remote-desktop>` HTML custom element for remote desktop protocol connectivity in the browser. It acts as a UI, input, and clipboard bridge between the browser DOM and an injected WebAssembly module (such as `ironrdp-web`), exposing a `UserInteraction` TypeScript API for connecting to a remote desktop via a Devolutions Gateway proxy, sending mouse and keyboard input, managing bidirectional clipboard synchronization, and controlling an HTML5 canvas renderer. The README explicitly states it contains 'TypeScript interfaces exposed by WebAssembly bindings from ironrdp-web and used by iron-svelte-client'. The WASM module is injected at runtime via the `module` component attribute, making the component protocol-agnostic at the TypeScript layer.

network_endpoints: wss://devolutions-gateway-proxy (RDP session WebSocket, address configured at runtime via SessionBuilder.proxyAddress)
http://kdc-proxy-url (KDC proxy HTTP POST for Kerberos CredSSP auth, configured at runtime via kdcProxyUrl Extension)
https://kdc-proxy-url (KDC proxy HTTPS POST for Kerberos CredSSP auth)
response.notes: The component's main.ts imports all exported types directly from the pre-built WASM package at ../../../crates/ironrdp-web/pkg/ironrdp_web, meaning it depends on a prior wasm-pack build of the ironrdp-web crate. The vite.config.ts builds only an ES module format (not UMD/CJS), but public/package.json references iron-remote-desktop-rdp.js as its main entry — the dist is published to JFrog Artifactory as @devolutions/iron-remote-desktop-rdp. The README explicitly names iron-remote-desktop (whose RemoteDesktopModule interface this package implements) and iron-svelte-client (used for development testing). Extension factory functions (preConnectionBlob, displayControl, kdcProxyUrl, outboundMessageSizeLimit, enableCredssp) are thin wrappers that construct Extension objects passed to the underlying WASM session. No filesystem I/O occurs at runtime since the package runs in a browser context.
response.purpose: iron-remote-desktop-rdp is an NPM library package (@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop-rdp) that provides an RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) backend for the iron-remote-desktop web component framework. It wraps pre-built WebAssembly bindings from the ironrdp-web Rust crate, re-exporting them as an ES module with a typed TypeScript API. The package exposes session lifecycle types (SessionBuilder, Session), input event types (InputTransaction, DeviceEvent), clipboard types (ClipboardData, ClipboardItem), configuration helpers (Extension factory functions for PCB, KDC proxy, display control, CredSSP), and RDP file parsing (RdpFile). At runtime it establishes RDP connections routed through a Devolutions Gateway WebSocket proxy and optionally performs HTTP/HTTPS requests to a KDC proxy for Kerberos/CredSSP authentication.

response.notes: This is a zero-implementation facade crate using Rust's feature-flag system for modular opt-in. Default features enable only 'core' and 'pdu'; all other modules require explicit feature activation. The data_formats field lists the re-exported module names, which correspond to RDP protocol domains rather than individual named types (those live in each sub-crate). Network endpoint evidence comes solely from bundled examples (screenshot.rs and server.rs), not the library code itself — the crate's src/lib.rs contains only pub use re-exports. Dev-dependencies include ironrdp-blocking and ironrdp-cliprdr-native used exclusively in examples. The private '__bench' feature gates internal benchmarking support in ironrdp-server.
response.purpose: A meta crate that re-exports multiple IronRDP sub-crates under a single unified namespace for consumer convenience. It contains no implementation logic of its own — all functionality is delegated to 15 optional workspace-internal dependencies (ironrdp-core, ironrdp-pdu, ironrdp-connector, ironrdp-acceptor, ironrdp-session, ironrdp-server, ironrdp-graphics, ironrdp-input, ironrdp-svc, ironrdp-dvc, ironrdp-cliprdr, ironrdp-rdpdr, ironrdp-rdpsnd, ironrdp-displaycontrol, ironrdp-echo) each exposed behind a Cargo feature flag. The crate is described in its README and Cargo.toml as 'A meta crate re-exporting IronRDP crates for convenience'. Bundled examples demonstrate both client (screenshot over RDP via blocking API) and server (async RDP server) usage patterns.

consumes_files: <DVC plugin DLL path via --dvc-plugin, Windows only>
network_endpoints: wss://<rdcleanpath_url> (RDCleanPath WebSocket proxy to RDP server)
response.notes: The binary reads the IRONRDP_LOG environment variable for tracing-subscriber filter directives, and the SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable to dump TLS session secrets for Wireshark decryption. When credentials or server address are not supplied via CLI flags, the inquire crate is used to prompt the user interactively. The WebSocket transport path (connect_ws / RDCleanPath) depends on the devolutions-gateway 'transport' crate (pulled from a specific git revision of https://github.com/Devolutions/devolutions-gateway) for wrapping tungstenite WebSocket frames as AsyncRead/AsyncWrite. On Windows only, clipboard support uses WinClipboard from ironrdp-cliprdr-native and DVC COM plugins are loaded via LoadLibraryW through ironrdp-dvc-com-plugin. DVC named-pipe proxies expose a named-pipe server per channel so that out-of-process code can exchange DVC PDUs with the active RDP session. The initial window size is hardcoded to 1024×768; the connector's default desktop resolution is 1920×1080.
produces_files: <path given by SSLKEYLOGFILE env var — TLS session secrets>
response.purpose: ironrdp-client is a portable, full-featured RDP desktop client CLI binary built on the IronRDP crate suite. It connects to RDP servers directly over TCP (default port 3389), optionally routed through a Devolutions Gateway (MSTSGU protocol) or a WebSocket-based RDCleanPath proxy. The client renders the remote desktop using software rasterisation (softbuffer) inside a winit window with no GPU requirement. It handles keyboard and mouse input, clipboard synchronisation (native Windows clipboard or a stub), audio playback via CPAL, smartcard and file device redirection via RDPDR, and DVC named-pipe proxies that allow external processes to participate in Dynamic Virtual Channels. On Windows it also supports loading DVC client plugin COM DLLs. Configuration may be supplied via CLI arguments, interactive prompts, or an .rdp property-set file.

response.notes: The crate exposes two public type aliases — CliprdrClient (Cliprdr<Client>) and CliprdrServer (Cliprdr<Server>) — allowing the same state machine to serve both sides of the RDP clipboard channel. All network I/O is delegated to the SvcProcessor/SvcMessage abstraction from ironrdp-svc; this crate never touches sockets directly. The CliprdrBackend trait's now_ms() method has a default std::time::Instant implementation but must be overridden for WASM environments (use Performance.now()) and tests (use a controllable counter). A private __test Cargo feature (gated with the visibility crate) exposes internal fields for use by ironrdp-testsuite-core/ironrdp-testsuite-extra without making them part of the public API. Security-hardening details embedded in the implementation include: MAX_LOCKED_FILE_LISTS=100 and MAX_OUTGOING_LOCKS=100 to bound memory from malicious Lock PDU floods; MAX_PENDING_FILE_REQUESTS=1000 to cap pending transfer state; sanitize_file_path() strips path traversal sequences and absolute prefixes from remote-supplied filenames; is_windows_device_name() is exposed as a public helper for backends writing to Windows filesystems. The README explicitly directs users to the ironrdp-cliprdr-native crate for concrete backend implementations. The Cargo.toml declares ironrdp-core, ironrdp-pdu, and ironrdp-svc as public path dependencies (workspace-relative paths under ../ironrdp-{core,pdu,svc}).
response.purpose: ironrdp-cliprdr is a Rust library that implements the MS-RDPECLIP clipboard static virtual channel (CLIPRDR) as specified in [MS-RDPECLIP]. It provides: PDU encoding and decoding for all clipboard channel message types (FormatList, FormatDataRequest/Response, FileContentsRequest/Response, Lock/Unlock, Capabilities, MonitorReady, TemporaryDirectory); a state-machine processor (`Cliprdr<R>`) for both client and server roles implementing `SvcProcessor` from ironrdp-svc; a `CliprdrBackend` trait defining the interface that OS-specific clipboard implementations must fulfill; file transfer support via delayed rendering per MS-RDPECLIP section 1.3.1.4 including clipboard locking, file list snapshots, and concurrent download tracking; and security utilities (path traversal sanitization, Windows device name detection, absolute-path rejection).

response.notes: The crate is no_std compatible (with an 'std' feature flag). DrdynvcClient implements SvcClientProcessor and SvcProcessor from ironrdp-svc, making it pluggable into the broader IronRDP SVC framework. DrdynvcServer implements SvcServerProcessor. Channel name constant is 'drdynvc\0' (8-byte static channel name). The DvcChannelListener trait enables dynamic registration of channel handlers at runtime, supporting multiple incoming CREATE_REQ for the same channel name. PDU size limit for data fragments is 1590 bytes (DrdynvcDataPdu::MAX_DATA_SIZE). CompleteData (internal module) handles reassembly of split DVC messages across multiple DataFirst/Data PDU sequences.
response.purpose: ironrdp-dvc is a Rust library implementing the DRDYNVC (Dynamic Virtual Channel) protocol for RDP, as specified in MS-RDPEDYC. It provides both client-side (DrdynvcClient) and server-side (DrdynvcServer) implementations of the DRDYNVC static virtual channel, which multiplexes multiple named dynamic virtual channels (DVCs) over a single RDP static virtual channel. The crate exposes traits (DvcProcessor, DvcClientProcessor, DvcServerProcessor, DvcChannelListener) that consumers implement to handle application-specific DVC traffic. It handles the full DVC lifecycle: capability negotiation, channel creation handshake, data reassembly of fragmented PDUs, and channel closure. It also includes the complete PDU codec (encode/decode) for all DRDYNVC message types.

response.notes: The crate's Cargo.toml marks several numeric-trait dependencies (num-derive, num-traits, byteorder) as 'to be removed', indicating active refactoring. The README documents an ongoing migration: deprecated re-exports of ironrdp-core items (ReadCursor, WriteCursor, WriteBuf, Encode, Decode) are preserved for backward compatibility but callers are directed to import directly from ironrdp_core. The ironrdp-error and ironrdp-core crates are re-exported publicly, making them part of the effective public API surface. Feature flags 'qoi' and 'qoiz' enable optional image codec support via external qoi/qoiz crates. The crate uses x509-cert, pkcs1, der-parser, md-5, sha1, and num-bigint for certificate/security PDU parsing, but these remain fully internal with no external I/O. All data structures follow a 'resilient parsing' design: unknown enum variants use newtype structs rather than Unknown variants to preserve round-trip encoding fidelity when new protocol versions add values.
response.purpose: ironrdp-pdu is a pure Rust library that provides encoding and decoding of Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Protocol Data Units (PDUs). It covers the full RDP protocol stack from low-level transport framing (TPKT, TPDU, X.224) through MCS/GCC connection negotiation, capability exchange, security negotiation, input events, graphics output (fast-path, bitmap, surface commands), virtual channels, codec structures (RemoteFX), clipboard, and licensing. All types are plain data structures with public fields; the library performs no I/O, networking, or process spawning. It re-exports from ironrdp-core and ironrdp-error and is designed to be consumed by higher-level IronRDP components as a protocol serialization foundation.

response.notes: The RDPDR channel is registered under the channel name `rdpdr\0\0\0` (constant `Rdpdr::NAME`). The crate implements the pluggable backend pattern: `RdpdrBackend` is a trait with three methods — `handle_server_device_announce_response`, `handle_scard_call`, and `handle_drive_io_request` — that callers implement to supply OS-specific behaviour; the bundled `NoopRdpdrBackend` returns success for everything. Smart card PDUs use RPCE (Remote Procedure Call over Extensions) and NDR (Network Data Representation) encoding defined in sub-modules `pdu/esc/rpce.rs` and `pdu/esc/ndr.rs`. The companion crate `ironrdp-rdpdr-native` (catalog: `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-rdpdr-native`) provides the concrete native backend implementation. The fuzzing harness `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-fuzzing` depends on this crate for fuzz-testing its PDU codecs.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpdr implements the RDPDR (Remote Desktop Protocol Device Redirection) static virtual channel as specified in MS-RDPEFS and MS-RDPESC. It provides a complete PDU parsing and encoding library for the device redirection channel, supporting file system redirection and smart card redirection. The crate defines all RDPDR protocol data units, a pluggable `RdpdrBackend` trait that callers implement to handle actual device operations, and the `Rdpdr` channel processor that implements `SvcProcessor`/`SvcClientProcessor` from ironrdp-svc. It handles protocol negotiation (version exchange, capability advertisement, device announcement) and dispatches decoded requests to the backend, but performs no direct filesystem or network I/O itself.

response.notes: The crate depends on ironrdp-svc (SvcProcessor, SvcClientProcessor, SvcServerProcessor, SvcMessage, SvcProcessorMessages, CompressionCondition, SvcEncode traits), ironrdp-core (Encode/Decode traits, cursor utilities), and ironrdp-pdu (PduResult, gcc::ChannelName, padding helpers). The channel name is the static string 'rdpsnd\0\0'. The client comment in client.rs notes that rdpsnd is required for rdpdr to function (references MS-RDPEFS Appendix A<1>). A companion crate ironrdp-rdpsnd-native appears in the catalog and is the expected platform-specific backend that implements RdpsndClientHandler or RdpsndServerHandler. The catalog names ironrdp-svc, ironrdp-core, and ironrdp-pdu do not appear verbatim as catalog entries so they are not listed in explicit_cross_component_mentions; only ironrdp-rdpsnd-native appears as a catalog sibling that directly relates to this crate. UDP PDU support is noted as a TODO in pdu/mod.rs.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpsnd is a Rust library implementing the RDP Audio Output Virtual Channel Extension (MS-RDPEA / RDPSND static channel). It provides both client-side and server-side state machines (Rdpsnd and RdpsndServer) that manage the RDPSND protocol handshake — format negotiation, training round-trips, wave audio delivery, volume/pitch control, and channel close — over an RDP static virtual channel named 'rdpsnd'. The crate also supplies complete PDU encode/decode implementations for all MS-RDPEA message types (ServerAudioOutputPdu, ClientAudioOutputPdu, and their variants) and a pluggable handler trait (RdpsndClientHandler / RdpsndServerHandler) that callers implement to receive decoded audio events or supply audio data.

response.notes: The crate bridges the output of ironrdp-connector (ConnectionResult) into the active-session phase. The fast-path processor handles bulk decompression (Rdp4/Rdp5/Rdp6/Rdp61) of server-to-client PDUs when compression was negotiated. The slow-path (X.224) processor auto-responds to RTT auto-detect requests internally; NetworkCharacteristicsResult events are surfaced to the caller via ActiveStageOutput::AutoDetect. RemoteFX decoding in rfx.rs implements the full wavelet pipeline: RLGR entropy decode → subband reconstruction → dequantization → DWT → YCbCr-to-RGBA conversion. Optional Cargo features 'qoi' and 'qoiz' (QOI + zstd) add non-standard codec extensions for surface commands. Pointer rendering supports two modes: hardware (emitting PointerBitmap events for the host renderer) and software (compositing directly onto DecodedImage with premultiplied-alpha blending and XOR inversion support). A TODO comment in x224/mod.rs notes that slow-path bulk decompression is not yet wired up. The library has no binary entry point and does not perform I/O directly; all network bytes are passed in and response bytes are returned as Vec<u8> buffers in ActiveStageOutput::ResponseFrame.
response.purpose: ironrdp-session is a Rust library crate providing abstract state machines that drive an active RDP session after the connection phase is complete. It accepts raw byte frames from the transport layer, dispatches them through either a fast-path or X.224/MCS slow-path processor, decodes graphics updates (bitmap RLE, RDP6 compressed streams, RemoteFX wavelet tiles, and optionally QOI/QOI+zstd via feature flags), manages a software-rendered or hardware-accelerated pointer pipeline, processes static and dynamic virtual channel messages, and produces outgoing response frames (frame acknowledgements, input PDUs, shutdown requests, SVC/DVC messages) ready for the caller to write to the network transport.

response.notes: Runtime config is injected via an nginx envsubst template (scripts/secureportal.conf.template) that writes a window.__APP_CONFIG__ block containing keycloak_location, keycloak_client, launcher_role, download_role, and upload_role. Feature flags are written to src/public/features.json at build time by vite.config.mts copying from the @dull/flags workspace package. The app uses Recoil for global state and TanStack Query for server state. Dev proxies in vite.config.mts route /secure-file-transfer/api to http://secure-file-transfer:5190/ and /secure-file-scanner/api to http://secure-file-scanner:5191/. Production build is a multi-stage Docker image (pruner → web-build → nginx), served on container port 9999. The localStorage key 'installer_id' is used to persist a launcher identifier across sessions.
response.purpose: A React single-page application that serves as the user-facing secure file transfer portal within the Dull zero-trust network access system. It provides role-gated file upload, download, archive, and restore capabilities, protected by Keycloak OIDC authentication. The app is served by Nginx under the path /secure-file-transfer-portal and receives its runtime configuration (Keycloak coordinates, role names) via a window-injected __APP_CONFIG__ object generated from an nginx template. It consumes generated API clients for the secure-file-transfer and secure-file-scanner backend services, uses feature flags from the @dull/flags workspace package, and integrates Sentry for error monitoring.

response.notes: KiTTY-fork operates as a standalone Windows desktop GUI with zero runtime coupling to other dull catalog components. The repository contains a .devcontainer/ subdirectory (corresponding to catalog entry dull/kitty-fork/devcontainer) which provides a MinGW/cross-gcc build environment for compiling the Windows binary from Linux; it does not reference other catalog components by name. The component supports two storage backends: Windows Registry (default) and portable directory/INI mode (activated when kitty.ini is present alongside the executable or when KITTY_INI_FILE env var is set). File-transfer features depend on optional co-located binaries (pscp.exe/kscp.exe for SCP, winscp.exe for SFTP). A Dockerfile is present that produces the cross-compilation build image.
response.purpose: KiTTY is a Windows-only terminal client and enhanced fork of PuTTY 0.76 that provides SSH, Telnet, Rlogin, and raw socket connectivity. It extends PuTTY with additional features including session management with folder hierarchies, portable/registry-less operation via INI files, scripting and automation support, SCP/SFTP file transfer via bundled or external tools, port-knocking, transparency, background images, screenshot capture, and a built-in text editor. It is a standalone desktop GUI application with no server component.

response.notes: The ProxyController route (`PUT /api/v1/proxy/notifications/{requestId}`) is protected by a `[RequiresClientSecret]` Bearer-token middleware (env var `CLIENT_SECRET`), distinguishing it as an internal service-to-service endpoint — most likely called by `dull/mfa-proxy-api` — while the device and notification endpoints are called directly by the MFA mobile app using ECDSA signature auth. The service references two shared-backend C# project references (`../../shared-backend/MFA/MFA.csproj` and `../../shared-backend/Model/Model.csproj`) for `SourceType` enum and `ProcessState` enum/model; these are not listed as separate catalog components. NLog is configured to write to an `AggregateLog` table in a separate PostgreSQL instance pointed at by `SYNCH_HOST_CONNECTION_STRING`, distinct from the primary application database. All database access uses PostgreSQL views (`mfa_api.MFADevices_View`, `mfa_api.MFARequests_View`, `mfa_api.MFAResponses_View`, `mfa_api.MFADeviceInvites_View`, `mfa_api.MFAConfiguration_View`, `mfa_api.RealmStates_View`, `mfa_api.RealmConfiguration_View`, `mfa_api.MFADevices_Update_View`) and one direct table (`public.SentMFARequests`). Configuration (timeouts, API location) is loaded from the database view `mfa_api.MFAConfiguration_View` and cached for 30 seconds.
response.purpose: mfa-api is a .NET 8 ASP.NET Core web API service (described as "Dull MFA public webservice API") that acts as the backend for the Dull MFA mobile application. It handles four primary concerns: device registration (pairing a mobile device to a user account via an invite token and public-key exchange), MFA notification delivery (surfacing pending MFA challenge requests to registered devices), challenge approval (accepting signed approve/deny responses from devices and persisting them), and notification token management (updating or revoking push-notification registration keys). All data is read from and written to a PostgreSQL database via Dapper, using a set of views in the `mfa_api` schema. Request authenticity is enforced by verifying ECDSA signatures included in each request against a device's stored public key. A separate proxy endpoint (`/api/v1/proxy/notifications/{requestId}`) accepts Bearer-authenticated callbacks from a backend proxy service to update the delivery state of sent MFA requests.

network_endpoints: http://0.0.0.0:1980 (Policy API: POST /stream/, DELETE /stream/{uuid}, POST /auth/, POST /publish_done/)
http://127.0.0.1:8888 (Policy API internal listener, proxied by Nginx to port 1980)
response.notes: The Policy API (session-recording-server/policy-api) is physically a subdirectory within this component and is listed as a separate catalog entry; its REST endpoints are served internally on 127.0.0.1:8888 and proxied to external port 1980 by Nginx. Required environment variables at runtime: BEARER_SECRET (min 16 chars), LAUNCHER_API_URL, POSTGRESQL_USER, POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD, POSTGRESQL_DBNAME, POSTGRESQL_HOST (default localhost), POSTGRESQL_PORT_NUMBER (default 5432), SESSIONRECORDING_DOMAIN. The dull agent binary embedded at /app/dull/ is initialized via 'dull init' and run as 'dull -d run'; its SQLite database lives at /app/dull/microdata.db3. The component runs as www-data (UID 33) and requires /data/ volume with subdirectories logs/, recordings/, run/ writable by that user. The RTMP auth flow is: realm manager calls POST /stream/ → receives UUID → passes UUID to session-recording-client → client publishes rtmp://server:1935/live/{UUID} → Nginx calls POST /auth/ to validate → Policy API notifies launcher API of recording start/stop.
response.purpose: The session-recording-server is a Docker container-based service that provides RTMP video stream ingestion, authorization, and management for session recordings in the Dull platform. It comprises two tightly coupled subsystems: a Rust-based Policy API microservice that manages one-time UUID recording tokens in a PostgreSQL database and orchestrates lifecycle notifications to the Launcher API, and an Nginx RTMP server (compiled with the nginx-rtmp-module) that receives raw video streams from RDP session recorders, validates them against the Policy API, and persists them as FLV files and HLS segments on a mounted volume.

data_formats: Win32 window messages (WM_DESTROY, WM_DPICHANGED, WM_SIZE, WM_ACTIVATE, WM_FONTCHANGE, WM_FONTCHANGE)
response.notes: This is a standard Flutter-generated Windows runner with minimal custom logic. Plugin registrations reveal the app's Windows dependencies: screen_retriever_windows (multi-monitor support), smart_auth (authentication), sqlite3_flutter_libs (local database), url_launcher_windows (opening URLs), and window_manager (window control). The flutter/CMakeLists.txt invokes the Flutter tool backend (tool_backend.bat) at CMake build time to assemble the Flutter library and Dart assets. The AOT library (app.so) is only installed for Profile and Release build configurations. User32.dll is dynamically loaded at runtime for PerMonitor V1 DPI awareness fallback.
response.purpose: The Windows platform shell for the flutter_launcher Flutter desktop application. It provides the native Win32 window host that bootstraps and embeds the Flutter engine on Windows, creating a 1280x720 window titled 'Dull Launcher' (binary name: DullLauncher.exe). It registers Windows-specific Flutter plugins (screen_retriever_windows, smart_auth, sqlite3_flutter_libs, url_launcher_windows, window_manager), passes command-line arguments to the Dart entrypoint, handles DPI scaling, and routes Win32 window messages to the Flutter controller.

response.notes: The container mounts the `dull` binary at `/dull/dull` at runtime (it is not built into the image). The startup script runs `dull init` if no `microdata.db3` exists, then starts `dull -d run` as a background daemon. A dedicated `vncuser` account is given passwordless sudo rights specifically for `/dull/dull`. The XFCE desktop ships with Chromium and Firefox `.desktop` launchers so an operator can interact with whatever web UI `dull` exposes via the noVNC browser session on port 6901.
response.purpose: A Docker image that provides a full graphical desktop environment accessible over VNC and noVNC (HTML5). Built on Debian 12, it installs TigerVNC server (port 5901), noVNC HTML5 proxy (port 6901), the XFCE4 window manager, Firefox, and Chromium. At container startup it initialises and daemonises the `dull` binary (mounted at `/dull/dull`) via `dull init` / `dull -d run`, then brings up the VNC server and window manager so that the running `dull` service can be interacted with through a browser-based or native VNC session.

response.notes: The entry-point mounts and invokes the `dull` binary from /dull/dull (the main Dull agent) alongside telnetd, implying the container is designed to be run with the dull binary injected at runtime (e.g., via a Docker volume or build step that copies it in). The ten pre-seeded user accounts with weak credentials are clearly for automated integration tests or demo environments — not production use. The inetd.conf is written at image build time and hardcodes the telnet service entry. The service is part of the docker-network-services tooling group, which likely includes sibling services for other protocols (ssh, rdp, vnc, etc.) used to test the platform's protocol-agnostic access-control layer.
response.purpose: A Docker-based Telnet server service intended for development and testing of access-control and session-recording features. The Dockerfile installs `telnetd` and `inetutils-inetd`, configures an inetd listener on the standard Telnet port (23), and pre-creates ten users with predictable credentials (insecure01/password01 through insecure10/password10). The entry-point script optionally bootstraps and runs the `dull` agent binary alongside the inetd daemon, making the container a self-contained network target that exercises Telnet connectivity with the rest of the Dull platform.

response.notes: The 10 insecure accounts (insecure01–insecure10 / password01–password10) are clearly intended as predictable test credentials for automated or manual SSH connectivity testing. The entry-point co-hosts the `dull` binary alongside sshd, initialising a `microdata.db3` SQLite database on first run, suggesting the SSH server provides SSH access to a running `dull` agent/service instance — likely for testing SSH-tunnelled or SSH-authenticated access scenarios. The container image does not bundle the `dull` binary itself; it must be injected at build or run time into /dull/.
response.purpose: A Docker container that provides an SSH server for testing and development purposes. Built on Debian trixie-slim, it installs OpenSSH server and provisions 10 pre-configured test accounts (insecure01–insecure10) with predictable passwords, plus a root account with a randomised password locked to key-based auth. On container start the entry-point optionally initialises and runs the `dull` application binary (if injected at /dull/dull), then starts sshd in the background, handling SIGINT/SIGTERM for clean shutdown of both processes.

response.notes: The tool strips the 'public.' schema qualifier from all identifiers (via string replacement on each kept line), which suggests the output is intended for a code generator that does not expect schema-qualified names. The GENERATED header comment written to each output file explicitly warns consumers not to edit the output. This pattern — sanitizing SQL before passing it to sqlc — is consistent with the parent package name 'Dull.SqlcDal', suggesting this tool feeds into the sqlc DAL (data access layer) generation pipeline. No README or additional documentation files were found in the component directory; all evidence is from Cargo.toml and src/main.rs.
response.purpose: sql_sanitizer is a standalone CLI tool written in Rust that processes SQL files by stripping out PostgreSQL trigger function definitions and comments, then writing cleaned versions to a mirror output directory. It walks an input directory tree, matches all *.sql files, removes lines beginning with '--' or '\', deletes entire trigger function bodies (from 'RETURNS trigger' through the closing '$$;'), strips the 'public.' schema prefix from identifiers, and prepends a 'GENERATED' warning header to each output file. The tool is designed to produce sanitized SQL files suitable for consumption by sqlc or similar code-generation tools that cannot handle trigger definitions.

response.notes: All configuration is via environment variables: BEARER_SECRET (bearer auth token, min 16 chars), LAUNCHER_API_URL, POSTGRESQL_USER, POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD, POSTGRESQL_DBNAME, POSTGRESQL_HOST (default localhost), POSTGRESQL_PORT_NUMBER (default 5432). The PostgreSQL table used is 'sessionrecordingtokens' with a 'token' UUID column. The LauncherApiClient uses the system's native TLS certificate store (rustls-native-certs) for HTTPS connections to the Launcher API. The README references a 'realm process' as the caller of the /stream/ endpoints and mentions future work of moving token management into the Realm Postgres and eliminating the /stream/ endpoints. No nginx subprocess spawning is implemented in current source despite the README describing it as the intended Docker entrypoint supervisor.
response.purpose: The policy_api is a Rust HTTP microservice that acts as an authorization gate for an Nginx RTMP/stream server and as a stream lifecycle notifier to the Launcher API. It manages UUID-based stream tokens in a PostgreSQL database: a realm process calls POST /stream/ (with a bearer token) to mint a UUID that is then given to the screen-recorder as its stream name; Nginx calls POST /auth/ (form-encoded) to verify that a published stream name is a valid UUID before allowing the publish; POST /publish_done/ signals stream termination. On both auth and publish_done events the service calls the Launcher API to register recording started/stopped. The README also states the binary is the Docker entrypoint and is intended to spawn and supervise an Nginx child process, though that supervisory code is not present in the current source.

network_endpoints: rtmp://<endpoint_address> (RTMP publish client, default port 1935/1936, address set at runtime via --endpoint-address CLI arg)
response.notes: The component serves dual roles: a standalone Windows CLI binary (session-recorder.exe) and a Dart-callable Rust library via flutter_rust_bridge (cdylib/rlib crate-type). CI pipeline (Buildkite) pushes the release exe to S3 at s3://dull-unsigned/evm/session-recording/ and signs it with KMS. The RTMP connection retries indefinitely without exponential backoff (explicit design decision in README). H.264 encoding targets 700 kbps at 24 fps with a forced keyframe every 30 frames (~1s). The intended deployment context is an RDP host machine running the Dull Launcher, consistent with the evm-* component family in the catalog. The Dart FFI layer exposes startCapture, stopCapture, getWindowsForPid for integration with the desktop launcher app.
response.purpose: This component implements a cross-platform screen capture and RTMP streaming binary for session recording. On Windows it captures the user's desktop (or a specific window by HWND) using the Windows Graphics Capture API; on Linux it uses x11rb; on other non-Windows targets it falls back to a test pattern. Captured frames are H.264-encoded via OpenH264 and streamed over RTMP to a configurable upstream server. The crate also compiles as a cdylib/rlib Dart library (via flutter_rust_bridge) exposing startCapture, stopCapture, and getWindowsForPid to the Dull desktop launcher application. A Windows system-tray UI is included for the standalone binary form.

response.notes: The generator phase runs as a Docker container (built from server-installer/Dockerfile using python:3.13-slim-bookworm) and writes artifacts to a mounted /out volume. The install.sh template is parameterised with sed during generation and appended with SQL commands to seed admin/launcher users. The installer bootstraps HashiCorp Vault by running 'vault operator init' inside the vault container, extracting unseal keys and root token, then patching them into launcherapi.env, vault-unsealer.env, microprocess.env, and realmapi.env before restarting affected containers. The Keycloak REST API is called extensively post-install to configure client secrets, audience mappers, service account role bindings, and direct-grant flow settings. The Dull Connector Agent installer (.run file) is downloaded from downloads.dull.net and executed with --mode unattended --linkfile connector.db3; a second agent instance is deployed inside the session-recording container mount. The build-push.sh and .buildkite/pipeline.yml suggest the generator Docker image is built and pushed via Buildkite CI. The realm.json example shows dockerRepositoryHostname can be 'dullapp' (public registry) or an AWS ECR hostname; when 'dullapp' is used ECR authentication is skipped. The indexpageContainerChannel and haproxyContainerChannel settings are referenced in gen.py but not documented in the README.
response.purpose: The server-installer is a two-phase provisioning tool for deploying a complete Dull Enterprise Realm on a Linux host. Phase 1 is a Python generator (gen.py) that runs inside a Docker container, reads a realm.json configuration file, performs DNS and SSL validation, then writes a full set of deployment artifacts to /out: docker-compose.yml, per-service .env files, HAProxy config, nginx SSL certs, Keycloak realm JSON, PostgreSQL init scripts, vault config, and parameterised install/uninstall shell scripts. Phase 2 is the generated install.sh that runs on the target host, checks prerequisites, authenticates with AWS ECR, creates Docker volumes, starts all platform containers in dependency order (PostgreSQL, Keycloak, microprocess, realmapi, agentapi, mfaapi, launcherapi, launcherweb, indexpage, HAProxy, Vault, session-recording, secure-file-transfer, secure-file-scanner, secure-portal), bootstraps HashiCorp Vault, configures Keycloak clients and service accounts via its REST API, seeds the dullmanager PostgreSQL database with realm state, downloads and installs the Dull Connector Agent, and wires in the session-recording service.

response.notes: The component integrates with `dull/secure-file-scanner` via HTTP: `FileObjectScannerClientService` POSTs raw file bytes to `{Scanner:Root}/api/v1/scan?remoteObjectId={objectId}` using a bearer client-secret (not a user JWT). The scanner response (`ScanResult`) drives the preprocessing outcome, which in turn gates approval. The `BackgroundPreprocessingTask` uses a PostgreSQL NOTIFY/LISTEN event bus (`PgNotifyTask` from the shared backend library) to wake up and process newly uploaded files, with configurable retry limits and lock expiry intervals (`PreprocessorConfig`). Storage backend is switchable at runtime: `Storage:Backend=garage` selects Garage S3 (AWS SDK path-style), while any other value falls back to native filesystem storage under `Storage:Root`. The Dockerfile shows the image depends on a `shared_backend` build stage (not a catalog component — it is an internal library).
response.purpose: Dull Secure File Transfer API is an ASP.NET Core 8 web service that manages the full lifecycle of secure file objects: upload (both a legacy multipart form endpoint and a modern two-step reserve-metadata/upload-content flow), retrieval, approval gating, archival, deletion, and audit logging. Files are stored either on a local filesystem or in a Garage S3-compatible object store. Before a file can be approved for use, a background preprocessing task submits it to an external file scanner service; files that fail scanning are blocked from approval. All mutating and read operations are recorded in a PostgreSQL-backed audit log, accessible to approver-role users via a dedicated audit endpoint. JWT/Bearer authentication (via a Keycloak OIDC provider) enforces role-based access for user vs. approver operations.

response.notes: The service mounts at the /secure-file-scanner path base (set via app.UsePathBase). Authentication uses a custom SimpleTokenScheme that validates a shared client secret supplied via the Auth:ClientSecret configuration key; tokens are stored in-memory and periodically compacted by CompactTokenStoreTask. Scan audit events are inserted into the file_scanner_api.fileobjectscannerevents PostgreSQL view/table. The maximum upload body size defaults to 100 MB but is overridden to ~60 GB in appsettings.json via ServerConfig:MaxUploadSize. The ENABLE_CLOUD_FEATURES env var (default true) gates the periodic freshclam update loop; when false, the last-update timestamp is read from the CLAM_AV_LAST_UPDATED env var instead. The clamav_update.sh and download_clamav_artifacts.sh shell scripts provide offline/cached ClamAV DB pre-population for development or air-gapped environments. The Dockerfile installs ClamAV in no-daemon (clamscan only) mode and disables the NotifyClamd directive in freshclam.conf.
response.purpose: dull/secure-file-scanner is an ASP.NET Core 8 web API that provides on-demand malware scanning of uploaded files using ClamAV. It exposes an authenticated HTTP POST endpoint that accepts raw file bytes, shells out to the local clamscan binary, computes a SHA-256 hash of the upload, and returns a structured scan result. Audit events for every scan are persisted to a PostgreSQL database. A background hosted service (ClamAvDefinitionUpdateTask) periodically invokes freshclam to keep ClamAV virus-signature databases current; when cloud features are disabled it falls back to a static timestamp supplied via environment variable.

response.notes: The Python class is named 'Echo' but does not echo input — it sends 8096 bytes of random data on any received data. Commented-out code suggests earlier iterations tried to echo or prepend a label. The README describes the trigger as a Carriage Return, but the code responds to any incoming data. The 'dull' binary and connector.db3 are not bundled in the image; they must be bind-mounted at runtime from a mounts/dull directory (consistent with sibling services in docker-network-services). The entry-point initialises the dull database with 'dull init' if microdata.db3 is absent, then starts dull in daemon mode (-d run) before launching the Python server.
response.purpose: The RTU (Remote Terminal Unit) component is a Docker container that simulates an RTU device for testing and demonstration purposes. It runs a Python TCP server on port 20000 using the Twisted framework; when any TCP data is received, it replies with 8096 bytes of random ASCII uppercase characters and digits. The container also starts a co-located Dull agent (the 'dull' binary, mounted at /dull) which is the intended sole means of network access to the container — no ports are exposed to the Docker host. This design mirrors a pattern used across the docker-network-services collection: insecure or legacy services are made accessible only through the Dull secure-access realm.

network_endpoints: ws://0.0.0.0:80 (WebSocket, same port as HTTP via UseWebSockets)
postgresql://localhost/dullmanager (consumes PostgreSQL for data and NOTIFY events)
http://keycloak:8080/realms/dull-manager (consumes OIDC authority for JWT validation)
http://<ndr-provider-host>/api/open/query/do (consumes Nozomi NDR HTTP API, host dynamically read from DB)
http://<credential-store-host> (consumes credential store/vault HTTP API via ICredentialStore)
response.notes: All REST endpoints are namespaced under /api/v1/managerui/ (plus /api/v1/whoami). WebSocket topic subscription uses a Tag/Verb/Args JSON protocol; the sole topic defined in this codebase is NumOpenConnections, which listens on PostgreSQL NOTIFY channels 'connections_change' and 'launchersessions_cache_change'. The Queries/Generated/ subtree is regenerated (likely by dull/model-generation) and must not be hand-edited. VaultOperations delegates credential storage to ICredentialStore (SharedBackend.CredentialStorage), which makes HTTP calls to an external vault service. The NozomiController proxies Nozomi NDR asset API access. The Buildkite pipeline publishes the image to Docker Hub as dullapp/realmapi. In development, DevModeCorsMiddleware adds CORS headers; in production a reverse proxy is expected to handle CORS. The credential-store endpoint address is injected via CredentialStoreFactory from config and not hard-coded in this component.
response.purpose: dull/realm-api is an ASP.NET Core 8.0 web service (running on Kestrel, deployed via Docker) that provides a comprehensive RESTful JSON API and WebSocket interface for front-end SPAs to interact with the system's back-end. It manages a broad domain including realms, devices, persons, services, connections, sessions, access policies, group access, metadata tags, EVM providers (AWS/VMware), MFA settings, launcher sessions, segments, syslog/NDR endpoints, and vault credentials. Authentication is via OIDC/JWT (Keycloak). Data is stored in PostgreSQL (accessed via Dapper and SqlKata). Real-time model updates are delivered to WebSocket subscribers using PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY. The Queries/Generated/ and Models/DTO/Generated/ directories contain files marked 'Do not edit by hand, changes will be lost on regeneration', indicating code-generated CRUD operations from a model generator tool.

response.notes: The entrypoint expects the `dull` binary to be bind-mounted into the container at /dull/dull; if present it runs `dull init` (first boot) and then `dull -d run` as a background daemon alongside the RDP stack. xrdp is pinned to commit 5d8901d5df970ebdb5727ab451ed14091118ed66 and xorgxrdp to b32e8c6fbc1a4a42a774d9e4cb2774ebf9bbac9a, both cloned from GitHub during the Docker build stage. The Mozilla Firefox PPA is configured at pin-priority 1001 to override snap packaging. No README is present; behaviour is inferred entirely from Dockerfile and entrypoint.sh.
response.purpose: A Docker image that provides a full Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) server environment built on Ubuntu with an Xfce desktop. It compiles xrdp and xorgxrdp from source at pinned git commits, installs Firefox and an Xfce desktop suite, and exposes port 3389/tcp. At runtime, the entrypoint creates a local user account, optionally initialises and starts the `dull` agent binary (expected to be mounted at /dull/dull), then launches the xrdp session manager and xrdp daemon so clients can connect via RDP.

response.notes: The Lambda handler is invoked via AWS S3 event trigger (eventSource: aws:s3). The Slack webhook URL is hardcoded in the source. The Docker image is based on public.ecr.aws/debian/debian:stable-slim and installs osslsigncode, 7zip, Python 3, boto3, and awslambdaric to run as a Lambda container image. The README references an Atlassian Confluence page for more details. No direct references to other catalog components were found.
response.purpose: MSI-Scanner is an AWS Lambda function (deployed as a Docker container) that verifies digital signatures on Windows Agent Installer MSI files and the EXE files contained within them. It is triggered by S3 events when an MSI file is uploaded to a bucket, downloads the file from S3, checks its Authenticode signature using osslsigncode, extracts the MSI with 7zip to inspect embedded EXE files for valid signatures, and posts pass/fail status notifications to a Slack webhook.

response.notes: The entry-point.sh sleeps 120 seconds before invoking mosquitto_sub, implying the dull agent needs time to initialize and register on the network before MQTT subscription begins. The /dull directory is volume-mounted at runtime (not baked into the image), so the dull binary and its SQLite databases are supplied externally via bind mount. From the sibling docker-compose.yml (outside the component boundary), MQTT_HOST resolves to mqtt-server.syndrome.realm:1883 and the subscribed topic is dull_mqtt/#, placing this component in the same test network as dull/mqtt-server and dull/mqtt-pub. The mosquitto-clients package is installed at image build time from Debian trixie-slim.
response.purpose: A Docker-based MQTT subscriber utility that runs the dull connector agent alongside mosquitto_sub to subscribe to MQTT topics. On startup it removes any stale PID file, optionally initialises the dull data directory, launches the dull agent in the background, waits 120 seconds for it to settle, then starts mosquitto_sub in verbose mode to receive all messages on the configured topic hierarchy. It is used within the docker-network-services test network to observe MQTT traffic published by other components.

response.notes: The `mounts/dull/` directory is a bind-mount volume that provides both the `dull` binary and its SQLite state databases to the container at `/dull`. The mosquitto daemon runs with default configuration (no custom config file is copied into the image), so it binds on the standard MQTT port 1883. The container is part of the `tools/docker-network-services` collection. SQLite WAL-mode files (`.db3-shm`, `.db3-wal`) are present in the mounts, indicating the dull process uses WAL journalling for its databases.
response.purpose: A Docker-containerised service that co-hosts the Mosquitto MQTT broker with the `dull` connector binary. On startup the entry-point removes any stale PID file, conditionally runs `/dull/dull init` to create the connector's SQLite state databases if they do not yet exist, then launches `/dull/dull -d run` and `mosquitto -d` as background processes. A trap on INT/TERM signals kills both child processes for graceful container shutdown.

response.notes: MQTT broker address, port, and topic are fully configurable via environment variables MQTT_HOST, MQTT_PORT, and MQTT_TOPIC — no broker address is hardcoded. The 120-second initial sleep after starting the dull daemon suggests the script waits for dull to finish initialising before beginning to publish. The mounts/dull/ directory contains SQLite WAL-mode databases (connector.db3, hosts.db3, microdata.db3, etc.) that are bind-mounted into the container as persistent state for the dull daemon. The companion catalog component dull/mqtt-sub likely acts as the subscriber counterpart in the same docker-network-services environment, and dull/mqtt-server is likely the broker target, though neither is referenced by name in the source files.
response.purpose: A Docker-based test harness that continuously publishes timestamp messages to an MQTT broker via mosquitto_pub. On startup it optionally initialises and runs the dull daemon (from a bind-mounted binary) in the background, waits 120 seconds, then enters an infinite loop that publishes a formatted timestamp string (day/week/year/time) to a configurable MQTT topic every second. Intended for development/testing of MQTT publish flows in the docker-network-services environment.

response.notes: The docker-compose.yml defines two service targets: `regen_realm_api` (mounts dull/realm-api's Dull.RealmAPI directory as /src) and `regen_agent_api` (mounts dull/agent-api's Dull.AgentApi directory as /src); both also mount dull/microprocess's ServiceDotNet/Migration directory. The `data/fragments.txt` and all `templates/*.hbs` files are embedded at compile time via `include_str!`. The generated C# files follow a fixed layout: `Models/DTO/Generated/`, `Models/Filters/Generated/`, and `Queries/Generated/` under the target assembly root. The namespace root is auto-detected from the `.csproj` filename in the app_dir. The tool is a proof-of-concept per its own doc comment and may be used for initial development acceleration or integrated into a build/release pipeline.
response.purpose: A Rust CLI tool (`gen`) that introspects PostgreSQL database views via `information_schema`, parses the view SQL with a SQL parser, resolves column types and nullability against base tables, and uses Handlebars templates to emit C# source files: model classes, filter classes, create/update DTOs, and query/write operation base classes. It is driven by a JSON configuration file (`model_generation.json`) in the target C# assembly directory, and is designed to be run against specific backend API assemblies (realm-api, agent-api) to regenerate their auto-generated model layer.

response.notes: The component uses a 'driving view' pattern: each microprocess reads from a PostgreSQL view that surfaces pending work items, processes them, and writes results back through corresponding output views. Database migrations are embedded as assembly resources and applied at startup. The Entities/ directory contains entity YML files processed by an external development-time tool called 'synch' (Alivate internal) to generate SQL migrations, which are then merged and copied to ServiceDotNet/Migration/Init.sql via copy-resource.sh scripts. ProcessIncubation/ contains both generated boilerplate files (*.gen.cs) and hand-written process implementations. The service depends on shared-backend library projects (Common, CredentialStorage, Dull.SqlcDal, Logging, Model, Scheduling, Syslog) referenced via relative project paths. Two environment-variable-controlled code paths exist for connection creation/closing (old stored-procedure style vs. new per-item-transaction style). The AgentInstaller microprocess generates SQLite database files in the OS temp directory as agent installer packages, then writes the bytes to the database and deletes the temp files. The SendLogs microprocess forwards database aggregate log records to a configurable remote HTTP endpoint. MFA notifications can be dispatched either directly via Firebase Cloud Messaging or through a configurable HTTP proxy service (CLIENT_SECRET env var required). EVM support covers both AWS EC2 instances and Docker containers (via evm-docker-controller HTTP API). GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS must be configured for Firebase Admin SDK (application default credentials).
response.purpose: microprocess is a C# .NET 8 background service (PgServiceDotNet.dll) that hosts and runs a large collection of named 'microprocesses' — small, focused workers that poll or listen for PostgreSQL NOTIFY/pg_mq queue events and execute domain business logic. Responsibilities include: VPN tunnel and segment lifecycle management (microtunnels, segments, meet-points), EVM (Ephemeral Virtual Machine) creation/teardown via AWS EC2 and Docker, device agent installer package generation (SQLite databases), authentication provider synchronization with Keycloak, MFA challenge dispatch via Firebase Cloud Messaging and HTTP proxy services, access policy enforcement and scheduling, session recording integration, remote log forwarding, and scheduled task management. On startup the service runs a fixed sequence of embedded SQL migrations against a PostgreSQL database, then bootstraps all processes in parallel, each operating against the same shared database using a 'driving view' pattern where PostgreSQL views act as work queues.

consumes_files: <firebase.credentials_path> (runtime-configured Firebase service account JSON key file)
network_endpoints: https://0.0.0.0:443 (nginx sidecar, TLS termination, reverse-proxies /api/v1/ to external MFA API)
https://fcm.googleapis.com/ (Firebase Cloud Messaging — outbound, via google-fcm1)
http://<external_api.address>/proxy/notifications/<id> (PUT — reports notification delivery result to external MFA API)
response.notes: The component uses an event-driven architecture: the HTTP layer immediately enqueues received notifications onto a Redis pub/sub topic ('notifications'), and the NotificationsTask background worker consumes that topic with exponential-backoff retry (up to 5 attempts, max 120s delay). The nginx sidecar (nginx/templates/nginx.conf.template) is deployed alongside the Rust service; it terminates TLS on port 443 and doubles as a transparent reverse proxy, forwarding /api/v1/ traffic to the upstream external MFA API address configured via EXTERNAL_API_ADDRESS env var. Authentication is bearer-token based on both sides: inbound requests are validated against api.client_secret; outbound calls to the external API use external_api.client_secret. The Firebase service account credentials file path is specified via the firebase.credentials_path config field, read at startup. The develop config points external_api.address to http://localhost:3000, suggesting the external MFA API (likely dull/mfa-api) runs on the same host in development.
response.purpose: mfa-proxy-api is a Rust/Axum HTTP API service that acts as a secure proxy for MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) push notifications. It accepts inbound notification requests from the MFA API (authenticated via bearer token), enqueues them onto a Redis-backed pub/sub event queue, then asynchronously delivers the notifications to mobile devices via Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM). After each delivery attempt it calls back to the external MFA API to report success or failure. The service ships alongside an nginx sidecar that terminates TLS on port 443 and also reverse-proxies /api/v1/ traffic to the upstream MFA API.

response.notes: The API exposes all routes under two path prefixes simultaneously: api/v1/... and mfaapi/api/v1/..., suggesting it may be accessed both directly and through a reverse proxy (likely dull/mfa-proxy-api which is a separate catalog component). Device-facing endpoints authenticate via HMAC/public-key signatures carried in nonce, timestamp, and signature headers; requests are also checked for staleness using a configurable MFAAppWebRequestTimeoutS window. The internal proxy endpoint (PUT api/v1/proxy/notifications/{requestId}) is secured by a CLIENT_SECRET bearer token via ClientSecretMiddleware. The service references shared libraries from ../../shared-backend/MFA and ../../shared-backend/Model (providing SourceType enum and SentMFARequests model), but these are internal project references rather than independently catalogued components. NLog is configured to write structured JSON logs to file (/var/app/webapi_logs/) and also to INSERT into an AggregateLog table in PostgreSQL using the SYNCH_HOST_CONNECTION_STRING environment variable. The SEND_LOG_LEVEL environment variable controls the minimum log level forwarded to the database sink.
response.purpose: A .NET 8 ASP.NET Core Web API that serves as the backend for the Dull MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) mobile application. It enables MFA mobile devices to register via invite tokens, retrieve pending authentication challenge notifications, approve or deny those challenges with cryptographic signatures, and update their push notification registration tokens. An internal proxy endpoint (protected by a client secret) allows other backend services to update the delivery status of sent MFA requests. All device-facing endpoints authenticate requests via public-key cryptographic signatures rather than session tokens.

response.notes: Reads LAUNCHER_API_SECRET, LAUNCHER_API_ENDPOINT, LAUNCHER_USER_NAME, and LAUNCHER_USER_PASSWORD from environment variables at startup. The API endpoint may be http or https; the client uses native OS certificates for TLS trust. The component compiles three binaries: launcher-web-entrypoint (main entrypoint), launcher-web-askpass (SSH_ASKPASS helper that reads from the credential socket or invokes zenity for host-key prompts), and launcher-web-getpw (pipe-source credential reader from the credential socket). The credential socket /var/run/launcher-web-credsvc.sock is owned by UID 1001 and mode 0600, and delivers the credential exactly once. connector-lib's SegmentHost is used for the tunnel segment lifecycle; agent-core's SessionEvent enum drives the application-launch lifecycle callbacks to the launcher API. The XRDP_UID constant (999) is used to chown xrdp key material so xrdp can read it without running as root.
response.purpose: launcher-web-entrypoint is the Docker container entrypoint binary for EVM (External Virtual Machine) RDP containers. It orchestrates the full container startup sequence: generates xrdp TLS and RSA keys, spawns xrdp-sesman and xrdp processes, registers the container with the launcher API by posting its public certificate, polls the API for segment connectivity documents (inbound access, outbound application, optional session-recording), starts connector-lib network tunnel segments, sets up the Linux user account from environment variables, writes a /home/user/.xsession bash script that launches the appropriate target application (WebBrowser via Chrome, RDP via rdesktop, or SSH via xterm+ssh-wrap), and provides session credentials securely via a Unix domain socket without ever writing them to disk. It also ships two helper binaries—launcher-web-getpw and launcher-web-askpass—that read credentials from that socket for use as pipe sources or SSH_ASKPASS helpers.

response.notes: Requires a 'shared-backend' sibling repository (not in the monorepo) that supplies AuthenticationProviderIntegration, Common, MFA, Otp, Syslog, Dull.SqlcDal, and Model projects. The EVMs and SessionRecordings controllers accept token-authenticated (unguarded) registration calls intended for agent-side components such as the EVM launcher agent. The Versions controller streams binary installer payloads directly from database BLOBs. SignalR hubs push real-time MFA challenge status, forgotten-password status, and launcher settings changes to connected clients. The API is reachable under both 'api/' and 'launcherapi/api/' path prefixes to support both direct access and reverse-proxied deployments.
response.purpose: A .NET 8.0 ASP.NET Core REST API that serves as the backend for launcher clients (native and web). It manages user authentication via Keycloak JWTs, connection request workflows (including TOTP MFA challenges), EVM (Execution Virtual Machine) instance registration, session recording lifecycle, MFA device enrollment (TOTP and Dull-native), forgotten-password flows, launch target enumeration, and launcher version/installer delivery. Real-time push updates are delivered to connected clients over SignalR WebSocket hubs. Credentials for downstream systems are retrieved from HashiCorp Vault.

response.notes: The devcontainer.json uses the `docker-from-docker` feature to allow Docker operations from within the container. The postAttach.sh script is copied into /usr/local/scripts/ at image build time and executed on each attach. The HOST_FILES environment variable (defaulting to 'default') controls which host dotfiles are symlinked inside the container. SCRIPTS_DEBUG=1 is set by default in devcontainer.json, enabling verbose logging in postAttach.sh. The cross-gcc base image suggests this dev environment is used for cross-compilation work within the kitty-fork component.
response.purpose: A VS Code Dev Container configuration for the kitty-fork component. It builds a Docker image derived from `docker.io/cyd01/cross-gcc:latest`, remaps the container user to match the host user (including home directory and shell), mounts the host workspace and home directory into the container, and runs a postAttach script that sources a `.devcontainer_rc` customisation file from the host home and creates symlinks for selected host dotfiles (shell history, `.netrc`, `.makerc`) inside the container. It also sets up `/builds` and `/sources` symlinks pointing into the host home, installs VS Code extensions for Go, ESLint, Markdown-Mermaid, and Sonatype IQ, and runs the container in host network mode.

The postbuild script removes 'build/keycloakify-dev-resources' after the React build to clean up dev-only assets. The catalog component 'dull/frontend/keycloak-theme' is the most likely consumer of the theme artifacts this component generates. The component appears to be either a git subtree/submodule placeholder or a stub with only a manifest file checked in.
response.purpose: A Keycloakify 11 Webpack-based starter project for building custom Keycloak login/authentication UI themes using React. It uses the keycloakify CLI to generate Keycloak-compatible theme JARs from a React application built with react-scripts (Create React App / Webpack). The package is named 'dull-keycloak-theme', indicating it is the Keycloak theme customisation package for the Dull platform. It supports Storybook for component development and uses Prettier with Tailwind CSS plugin for formatting.

response.notes: The Dockerfile performs a three-stage build: (1) turbo prune extracts the keycloak-theme workspace from the frontend monorepo; (2) a Node/Maven build stage compiles the Keycloakify theme to a JAR (keycloak-theme-for-kc-22-to-25.jar); (3) the JAR is copied into the official Keycloak image as /opt/keycloak/providers/dull-keycloak-theme.jar. The README example docker-compose mounts ./scripts/keycloak-import/realm as /opt/keycloak/data/import (realm JSON import), TLS certificates from ./nginx/ssl/, and a custom entrypoint script. The entrypoint runs kc.sh build then kc.sh start --import-realm --optimized --proxy-headers xforwarded. Health is checked via curl to https://127.0.0.1:9000/health/live. CI tags images as dullapp/keycloak:{branch-name|latest|release} and pushes to DockerHub using AWS Secrets Manager credentials.
response.purpose: A Docker image that wraps the official Keycloak 25.0.6 identity and access management server, adding curl and jq utilities and bundling a custom login theme built from the frontend/apps/keycloak-theme workspace. At runtime it imports a pre-configured realm from a mounted JSON file, exposes OAuth2/OIDC authentication endpoints, and provides user management, role assignment, and TOTP MFA capabilities for the Dull platform. The image is built and published to DockerHub via Buildkite CI as dullapp/keycloak with feature-branch, latest, and release tags.

response.notes: The wrapper script (dull-wrapper.sh) appends to /docker-entrypoint.sh by commenting out nginx's original `exec "$@"` and replacing it with background execution of both nginx and `/dull/dull -d run`, with a SIGINT/SIGTERM trap for cleanup. The dull agent is initialised with `dull init` if microdata.db3 does not yet exist. The bundled snakeoil certificates are self-signed and intended for demo/test use only. The nginx site.conf mounts are expected at /etc/nginx/conf.d/ and /site inside the container, and the certs at /etc/sslcerts/. The landing page text ('You have successfully connected to a HTTPS endpoint using Dull') confirms its role as a connectivity verification target.
response.purpose: A Docker container that runs an Nginx HTTPS server (port 443) alongside the Dull agent as co-processes. It serves a static demo landing page over TLS using bundled self-signed (snakeoil) certificates. The container modifies the default nginx docker-entrypoint to run both nginx and the Dull agent in the background under a shared cleanup trap, so both are properly terminated when the container stops. Its primary function is to provide a reachable HTTPS endpoint for demonstrating or testing HTTPS connectivity through the Dull MicroNetworking system.

response.notes: The wrapper script is appended (not replaced) to the existing nginx docker-entrypoint.sh by commenting out its final exec line and concatenating dull-wrapper.sh. The /dull/dull binary and /dull directory are expected to be bind-mounted or injected at runtime — they are not built into the image. The mounts/site/ directory is similarly expected to be mounted into nginx's webroot. The wrapper removes dull.pid on startup to avoid stale PID files from previous runs, then calls '/dull/dull init' on first boot to create microdata.db3. Cleanup is handled via a SIGINT/SIGTERM trap that kills both child PIDs.
response.purpose: A Docker image that packages an nginx HTTP server together with the Dull agent as a co-process. It extends the official nginx image by appending a wrapper shell script to the container entrypoint that (a) initialises the Dull agent database if absent, (b) starts the Dull agent daemon and nginx concurrently in the background, and (c) ensures both processes are cleanly terminated on container exit. The component ships a static demo landing page confirming that an HTTP endpoint has been successfully reached through the Dull platform. It is part of the docker-network-services tooling set used for network-service integration testing and demonstration.

response.notes: The tool hardcodes PostgreSQL credentials (user=postgres, password=_F69^39MGTIH63ID, port=5436, database=dullmanager) directly in the Python source. The docker-compose.yml mounts a local ./output directory into the container at /app/output so CSV files are accessible on the host. The scripts/ directory contains ad-hoc SQL queries used for manual investigation of individual persons and realms — these are not executed by the Python scripts. realm-upgrade-gen.py prints SQL UPDATE statements to stdout rather than executing them, intended for manual DBA review. Three tunable constants (AGENT_SELF_TEST_BURST_THRESHOLD=20, MINEOPS_CONNECTION_THRESHOLD=35s, OAS_GROUP_MAX_CONNECTION_DELTA_MINS=5) control the de-duplication heuristics.
response.purpose: hexagon-stats is a standalone data-analysis tool that connects to a PostgreSQL database (the 'dullmanager' database) and generates per-person and per-realm connection statistics for a configurable date range. It queries the connections, devices, people, services, realms, and realmstates tables, applies several de-duplication heuristics (agent self-test burst filtering, MINEOPS session merging, OAS session grouping), and writes the results as CSV files to a local output directory. It is designed to be run inside Docker via docker-compose. A companion script (realm-upgrade-gen.py) generates SQL UPDATE statements to close stale open connections in the same database.

response.notes: The package exports both a JavaScript entry point (`./src/index.ts`) and a CSS stylesheet (`./styles.css`); consuming packages must import both. Workspace dependency `@dull/icons` (catalog: `dull/frontend/icons`) is a runtime production dependency used for the `Icon` component and icon rendering throughout. `@dull/config` (catalog: `dull/frontend/config`) is a dev-only dependency used for shared ESLint/TypeScript configuration. The library requires peer dependencies: React 18, Tailwind CSS v4+, and `tailwind-variants`. Component development is done via Ladle (a Storybook alternative) with `.stories.tsx` files co-located next to each component. Visual regression tests use Playwright with optional SmartUI integration (PROJECT_TOKEN env var). No catalog components other than icons and config are referenced.
response.purpose: A shared React UI component library for the Dull frontend monorepo. It wraps React Aria Components and Radix UI primitives with Tailwind CSS styling and exports 50+ accessible, composable components (form fields, dialogs, menus, tables, navigation, progress indicators, etc.), context providers, hooks, and utilities. Consuming packages import components from the `@dull/ui` package entry point (`./src/index.ts`) and include its pre-built stylesheet via the `./styles.css` export. The library is purely presentational and side-effect-free at runtime — it performs no network I/O, filesystem access, or subprocess execution.

response.notes: The PermissionDenied component integrates with Keycloak (via peer dependency @react-keycloak/web and keycloak-js) to generate logout URLs using keycloak.createLogoutUrl() and reads window.location.href for redirect URI construction — this is the only runtime external interaction beyond rendering. The unwrapForReactQuery utility is designed to bridge @hey-api generated API clients with react-query by re-throwing errors or returning unwrapped data. The package uses Ladle (not Storybook) as its component development environment, with *.stories.tsx files co-located alongside each component. All workspace dependencies are pinned with workspace:* protocol: @dull/icons and @dull/ui are runtime deps; @dull/config is a dev-only dep for shared TypeScript and Vitest configuration.
response.purpose: @dull/shared is a React component library that provides shared UI building blocks for the Dull frontend monorepo. It exports layout primitives (Hero, HeroLayout, Layout), page-level components (NotFound, PermissionDenied), a navigation-blocking hook (useNavBlocker), error-handling utilities (failable, failableAsync, unwrapForReactQuery), and the Result<T> discriminated-union type. It is consumed as an importable package by other frontend applications in the monorepo rather than run as a standalone application.

network_endpoints: http://localhost:5188/api/v1/realm (launcher web API, default base URL overrideable at runtime)
http://localhost:5188/launcherapi (duplicate launcher path prefix in merged spec)
http://localhost:5188/secure-file-transfer (secure file transfer API, path-prepended from merge)
response.notes: The `generate` npm script invokes `openapi-ts` (a devDependency) to regenerate `src/generated/` from the merged `openapi.json`; `openapi-merge` is implied by the presence of `openapi-merge.json` but is not listed explicitly in devDependencies — likely invoked as a separate pipeline step. The only confirmed monorepo consumer is `dull/frontend/secure-portal` (its package.json depends on `@dull/secure-portal-api-client`). The default base URL `http://localhost:5188` is defined in the generated `client.gen.ts` and is expected to be overridden at runtime via `setApiClientConfig`. The merged spec duplicates all Launcher API paths under both `/api/...` and `/launcherapi/api/...` prefixes, likely to support two different proxy routing configurations.
response.purpose: A TypeScript library that provides a generated, type-safe HTTP API client for the Dull Secure Portal frontend. It merges two OpenAPI specifications — the Launcher Web API (covering connections, application launches, EVM management, MFA devices, launch targets, session recordings, and user authentication) and the Secure File Transfer API — into a single unified `openapi.json` using openapi-merge. The `@hey-api/openapi-ts` code generator then produces typed TypeScript interfaces and SDK functions backed by Axios. A hand-written runtime wrapper (`src/runtime/client.ts`) adds authentication token support and dot-notation query-parameter serialization via `qs`.

response.notes: realm-manager imports seven internal workspace packages as npm dependencies — @dull/ui, @dull/form, @dull/generic, @dull/icons, @dull/config, @dull/flags, @dull/shared — which correspond to the catalog components dull/frontend/ui, dull/frontend/form, dull/frontend/generic, dull/frontend/icons, dull/frontend/config, dull/frontend/flags, and dull/frontend/shared respectively. It also depends on @dull/realm-api-client (catalog: dull/frontend/realm-api-client), a fully generated OpenAPI client used for all realm-api communication. These are not listed in explicit_cross_component_mentions because their catalog names do not appear verbatim in README or code comments. The app uses no WebSockets — real-time data is achieved by polling (30s for connections, 5s for open-connection nav badge counts). Session recording video is played back via video.js consuming HLS streams from a dedicated session recording server, with Keycloak bearer tokens injected into HLS requests. Runtime configuration can be injected via window.__APP_CONFIG__ (set by an Nginx entrypoint script templating environment variables) or via Vite build-time VITE_* variables. The Vercel deployment proxies /api, /launcherapi, /mfaapi, and /secure-file-transfer/api paths to troy.realm.dull.net. SQL-escaping of filter parameters is performed client-side in src/generic/api/index.js before sending query strings. Feature flags are loaded from {BASE_URL}/features.json at runtime; the file is copied from @dull/flags at build time by a Vite plugin.
response.purpose: realm-manager is the administrative web UI for the Dull zero-trust network access platform. It provides a comprehensive management interface covering device inventory, user management, access policies (launcher and machine-to-machine), connection monitoring and session recording playback, MFA device management, service/endpoint configuration, EVM management, integrations (Splunk SIEM, Nozomi NDR), secure file transfer audit logs, agent/launcher version management, and meetpoint device administration. It is a React 18 single-page application served under the /realm-manager base path, authenticated exclusively via Keycloak JWT, and communicates with backend services entirely through a generated API client and direct HTTP calls.

response.notes: The Makefile at `frontend/Makefile` shows the generation workflow: `curl` fetches live OpenAPI specs from running instances of the Realm API (port 5287) and Secure File Transfer API (port 5190), `openapi-merge-cli` merges them into a single `openapi.json`, and `pnpm run sync` generates the TypeScript client. The only consumer found in the workspace is `frontend/apps/realm-manager` (the `dull/frontend/realm-manager` catalog component), which imports this package under the alias `api-client`. The `secure_file_transfer_openapi.json` paths are prepended with `/secure-file-transfer` during the merge, meaning SFT API routes are served under that path prefix relative to the same base URL as the Realm API. The library uses `qs` for query-string serialisation with dot-notation (`allowDots: true`), which is important for the complex filter query parameters in the Realm API. Auth is passed as a bearer token via `@hey-api/client-axios`'s `AuthToken` abstraction.
response.purpose: A generated TypeScript client library (`@dull/realm-api-client`) that provides type-safe bindings for the Dull Realm API and the Dull Secure File Transfer API. It is produced by merging two OpenAPI specifications (`realm_openapi.json` and `secure_file_transfer_openapi.json`) with `openapi-merge-cli` and then generating TypeScript types and an axios-based SDK with `@hey-api/openapi-ts`. It exports strongly-typed request/response types and SDK functions covering dozens of Realm API domains (access intentions, access policies, devices, connections, groups, sessions, EVM providers, launcher configs, secure file transfer, etc.) plus a thin `setApiClientConfig` wrapper that configures the underlying axios client (base URL, auth token, query serialisation via `qs`).

network_endpoints: ws://<caller-supplied stream> (WebSocket to RDP gateway, stream passed in by caller)
http://<host>/webrdp.js (WASM module served to browser, as referenced in rdp.html)
response.notes: Cargo.toml path dependencies confirm direct local references: ironrdp-web at ../IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-web, iron-remote-desktop at ../IronRDP/crates/iron-remote-desktop, and common at ../common. The RDP destination hostname is hardcoded as 'Dull-Launcher-Web'. The HTML asset declares setClipBoard/getClipBoard JavaScript functions that are declared extern in lib.rs — the two sides must be deployed together. The Makefile.toml build tasks install both WASM artefacts and the assets directory to $INSTALL_PATH, an environment variable expected to be set by the consuming build system. The connect() function accepts an already-open async stream rather than opening a network connection itself, meaning the WebSocket creation and any WebSocket URL resolution are the caller's responsibility.
response.purpose: webrdp is a Rust/WebAssembly library (cdylib + rlib) that implements a browser-based RDP client. It wraps the ironrdp-web and iron-remote-desktop crates to establish and drive RDP sessions over an externally-supplied async stream (expected to be a WebSocket), rendering the remote desktop into an HTML canvas element. It is built with wasm-pack targeting the web platform and exposes JavaScript-callable functions (connect, disconnect) along with extern JS callbacks for clipboard integration. The accompanying rdp.html asset provides the DOM scaffolding (canvas, clipboard UI) that the compiled WASM code manipulates at runtime.
