network_endpoints: https://fuzzingcorpus.blob.core.windows.net/ironrdp (Azure Blob Storage via az CLI for fuzz corpus)
https://api.github.com/repos/<repo>/issues/<pr>/comments (GitHub API via gh CLI for coverage comments)
response.notes: The tool installs all helper binaries (cargo-fuzz, cargo-llvm-cov, grcov, wasm-pack, typos-cli, diplomat-tool, wasm2wat) into a local cargo root at `.cargo/local_root/` to avoid polluting the system install. Tool versions are pinned in `bin_version.rs`. The `web build` action modifies `ironrdp_web.js` in-place to replace `new URL(...)` usage with a Vite-compatible import, which is a codegen/patching step that produces source consumed by the SvelteKit web client. The coverage update workflow performs git branch gymnastics (orphan branch `cov-data`) to persist coverage JSON across PRs. Fuzz corpus is stored in Azure Blob Storage (`fuzzingcorpus` account, `ironrdp` container).
response.purpose: The xtask component is a `cargo xtask`-pattern automation tool for the IronRDP Rust workspace. It provides a unified CLI for all developer and CI tasks: bootstrapping tool dependencies, formatting/lint/typo checks, compiling and running tests, building and checking the WASM module (`ironrdp-web`), building and running the SvelteKit web client, generating and running fuzz targets (with Azure corpus sync), collecting and reporting code coverage (via llvm-cov and grcov), and building FFI dynamic libraries plus generating C# bindings via `diplomat-tool`. It is the single entry-point invoked as `cargo xtask <command>` for both local development and CI pipelines.

network_endpoints: wss://<proxy_address> (WebSocket RDP gateway, stream passed in by JS caller as io parameter)
https://<kdc_proxy_url> (HTTP POST to Kerberos KDC proxy for CredSSP authentication)
response.notes: Built with wasm-pack, producing a .wasm binary and JS glue consumed by the launcher-web TypeScript frontend. The WebSocket connection to the RDP proxy is established by the JS caller and passed in as an AsyncRead+AsyncWrite stream; this crate does not open the socket itself. TLS upgrade within the RDP handshake uses the SNI hostname 'localhost' (hardcoded, indicating the proxy terminates TLS on behalf of the client). A keepalive hack simulates ScrollLock key-press/release every 30 seconds to prevent browser WebSocket idle timeouts. Cursor bitmaps larger than 32x32 are downscaled using Lanczos3 before being Base64-encoded as PNG data URLs and forwarded to JS via a callback. The CLIPRDR clipboard backend eagerly fetches all remote formats on clipboard change because browsers do not support delayed clipboard rendering. The temporary clipboard spool directory is named '.cliprdr'. The component implements iron_remote_desktop::RemoteDesktopApi and uses the make_bridge! macro to generate all wasm-bindgen-exported types at compile time.
response.purpose: ironrdp-web is a WebAssembly crate that provides high-level RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) client bindings targeting web browsers. It compiles to a cdylib/rlib via wasm-pack and exposes a JavaScript/TypeScript API (via wasm-bindgen) for establishing RDP sessions through a proxy, rendering the remote desktop framebuffer onto an HTML canvas using softbuffer, handling keyboard, mouse, and wheel input events, and synchronising the clipboard between the browser and remote desktop using the CLIPRDR virtual channel. It implements the iron-remote-desktop RemoteDesktopApi trait bridge, generating all necessary wasm-bindgen glue. Additional exported types include RdpFile for parsing and writing .rdp configuration files.

response.notes: The crate re-exports everything from ironrdp-async (`pub use ironrdp_async::*`) so consumers only need to depend on ironrdp-tokio to get both the async abstractions and the Tokio-backed implementations. The `reqwest` feature is gated behind a Cargo feature flag with two TLS sub-features (`reqwest-rustls-ring` and `reqwest-native-tls`). The ReqwestNetworkClient is specifically designed to support Kerberos/SSPI authentication flows by relaying KDC protocol messages; the default KDC port 88 is hardcoded as a fallback. The catalog names `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-async` and `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-connector` are not exact matches in the catalog list — the catalog uses those exact strings, confirming they are valid cross-component references.
response.purpose: ironrdp-tokio is a Rust library crate that provides implementations of the `FramedRead`, `FramedWrite`, and `StreamWrapper` traits (defined in ironrdp-async) backed by Tokio's async I/O primitives. It exposes three stream wrapper types — `TokioStream` (Send+Sync), `LocalTokioStream` (non-Send), and `MovableTokioStream` (Send but not Sync) — together with corresponding `TokioFramed` type aliases and split/unsplit helpers for half-duplex use. An optional `reqwest` feature adds `ReqwestNetworkClient`, which implements the `NetworkClient` trait to send SSPI/Kerberos KDC network requests over TCP, UDP, or HTTP(S) using Tokio sockets and the reqwest HTTP client.

network_endpoints: tls://remote-rdp-server (client-side TLS upgrade of caller-supplied async stream)
response.notes: The SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable is consumed at runtime by rustls's KeyLogFile when the rustls feature is enabled; this is a debug/analysis affordance, not a config file. Certificate verification is intentionally bypassed for RDP compatibility—RDP authenticates via the server public key extracted from the peer certificate rather than a certificate trust chain. TLS resumption is explicitly disabled because the CredSSP protocol (MS-CSSP) does not support it. The lib.rs pub-use exports `upgrade_futures` for all three backends, but only rustls.rs defines that function; native_tls.rs and stub.rs omit it, which would cause a compile error if those backends are used by code that imports `upgrade_futures`.
response.purpose: ironrdp-tls is a Rust library crate that provides TLS upgrade boilerplate shared across IronRDP client implementations. It wraps an existing async I/O stream and performs a TLS client handshake, returning the upgraded stream along with the server's DER-encoded public key extracted from the peer certificate. Three mutually exclusive feature-flag backends are supported—rustls, native-tls, and a no-op stub—allowing consumers to select exactly one at compile time. The rustls backend deliberately disables certificate chain verification (NoCertificateVerification) and TLS session resumption (required by CredSSP), and optionally logs TLS session keys via the SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable for Wireshark analysis. The crate exposes three public symbols: `upgrade`, `upgrade_futures`, and `TlsStream`, plus a standalone `extract_tls_server_public_key` helper that reads the public key bytes from an x509_cert::Certificate.

network_endpoints: tcp://127.0.0.1:<ephemeral> (loopback RDP server bound to port 0 for test isolation)
response.notes: The openssl binary is only referenced inside tests/certs/Makefile, which is a one-time certificate generation helper committed alongside pre-generated PEM files; it is not invoked at test runtime. The crate declares publish=false and carries no public API. TLS identity is loaded from the checked-in self-signed cert/key pair at CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR/tests/certs/. The server is bound to ([127,0,0,1], 0) so the OS assigns an ephemeral port, retrieved via a ServerEvent::GetLocalAddr channel before the client connects.
response.purpose: ironrdp-testsuite-extra is an end-to-end integration test suite for IronRDP's RDP client and server implementations. It spins up an in-process RdpServer bound to a random local TCP port, performs a full TLS-upgraded RDP handshake from a ClientConnector, and exercises the ActiveStage session loop. Tests cover: basic client-server connection, deactivation-reactivation sequences (MS-RDPBCGR §2.2.11), and echo virtual channel round-trips via the DrdynvcClient/EchoClient. The crate itself has no library surface (lib.rs is empty aside from a lint allow); all substance lives in the integration test at tests/mod.rs.

response.notes: publish = false — strictly internal. The autotests = false / single-binary pattern is a deliberate Cargo optimization documented in tests/main.rs to avoid repeated linking. The optional __bench feature (using the visibility crate) exposes private items to benchmark crates. The UPDATE_EXPECT=1 environment variable triggers golden-file regeneration: pointer PNG test assets are written rather than read. Fuzz regression files under test_data/fuzz_regression/ are read at runtime (not embedded) so new crash cases can be added without recompiling. The lib portion exports fixtures and macros used by tests within the same crate only (no external consumers found in the workspace other than ironrdp-egfx and ironrdp-cliprdr which reference the crate name in comments/typo config).
response.purpose: ironrdp-testsuite-core is the central test suite library for the IronRDP project. It serves a dual role: (1) a library exporting reusable test fixtures (pre-encoded PDU byte arrays, capability sets, GCC blocks, MCS data) and test macros (assert_eq_hex!, encode_decode_test!, mcs_encode_decode_test!) that other crates can import for their own unit tests, and (2) a single integration test binary (integration_tests_core) that exercises encode/decode round-trips, graphics decoding, fuzz regression replay, clipboard, DVC, input, session, and server PDU handling across the full IronRDP crate family. Test data is a mix of compile-time-embedded binaries (include_bytes!) and runtime-read fuzz regression files under test_data/.

response.notes: The crate depends on ironrdp-core (for AsAny, Encode, DecodeResult, WriteBuf, etc.) and ironrdp-pdu (for GCC ChannelDef/ChannelName/ChannelOptions, MCS, X224 PDU types). It re-exports ironrdp-pdu as `pdu` for consumer convenience. The CHANNEL_CHUNK_LENGTH constant (1600 bytes) is the default MS-RDPBCGR virtual channel chunk size. The crate has no std dependency by default (std is a feature flag), making it suitable for no_std environments. A deprecated re-export of ironrdp_core::impl_as_any exists for Teleport migration compatibility. The crate explicitly disables doctests and unit tests (doctest = false, test = false in Cargo.toml).
response.purpose: ironrdp-svc is a Rust library crate providing traits and types for implementing RDP Static Virtual Channels (SVC). It defines the core abstractions — SvcProcessor, SvcClientProcessor, SvcServerProcessor traits — that any SVC implementation must satisfy, along with chunking/dechunking logic (ChunkProcessor) for the MS-RDPBCGR CHANNEL_PDU_HEADER framing, a StaticChannelSet registry keyed by TypeId, helper functions to encode SVC messages into fully-framed MCS/X224/TPKT PDUs for both client and server directions, and bitflags for channel control flags. It is consumed as a library by other IronRDP crates that implement concrete virtual channels (e.g. clipboard, printer, audio, display control).

response.notes: The crate depends on exactly two external crates: ironrdp-core (path = '../ironrdp-core') for the Encode/DecodeOwned/ReadCursor/WriteCursor traits and error macros, and bytemuck for zero-copy endian casting on little-endian targets. All public types implement ironrdp-core's Encode and DecodeOwned traits (except UnframedString, which intentionally omits them because its length must be supplied externally). The README explicitly documents the 'defer native conversion' and 'wire-length arithmetic' architectural invariants as constraints for all downstream consumers. Type aliases map directly to named fields in Microsoft RDP specifications: MS-RDPBCGR, MS-RDPEPS, MS-RDPEUSB, MS-RDPERP, and MS-RDPELE. The crate carries no binary entrypoint, no filesystem I/O, and no network I/O — it is a pure in-memory serialization/deserialization library.
response.purpose: ironrdp-str is a Rust library crate providing typed, wire-aware string primitives for Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) protocol fields. RDP encodes strings as UTF-16LE on the wire, and this crate exposes a set of typed wrappers — FixedString<N>, PrefixedString<Prefix, Null>, MultiSzString, and UnframedString — covering every combination of length-prefix type (none, cch u16/u32, cb u16/u32), null-terminator policy (counted, uncounted, absent), and single-vs-multi-string layout (MULTI_SZ). The crate enforces a deferred-conversion invariant: wire bytes are stored as Vec<u16> and only transcoded to Rust String on explicit caller request, making decode-encode passthrough a single memcpy. It also exposes foundational free functions (utf16_code_units, utf16_byte_len, utf16le_bytes_to_units, utf16_units_to_le_bytes) that are declared as the only correct source of truth for UTF-16 wire-length arithmetic. The crate is no_std-compatible when the 'std' feature is disabled, requiring only 'alloc'.

response.purpose: ironrdp-session-generators is a Rust library crate providing proptest property-based testing generators for types defined in ironrdp-session. It is a test-support crate within the IronRDP project, intended to be consumed by property tests and fuzzing harnesses that need to generate arbitrary ironrdp-session type instances. The crate is currently a stub/skeleton: all actual dependencies (ironrdp-session, ironrdp-pdu-generators, proptest) are commented out in Cargo.toml, and src/lib.rs contains no generator implementations — only module-level clippy lint overrides and a doc include of the README.

network_endpoints: rdp://0.0.0.0:3389 (configurable TCP listener, RDP protocol over plain/TLS/Hybrid)
response.notes: The crate declares a [lib] target only (no binary). The listen address and security mode are runtime-configurable via a type-state builder (RdpServerBuilder). TLS certificate/key file loading is gated behind the optional 'helper' feature (requires x509-cert and rustls-pemfile). The 'egfx' feature enables the H.264 Graphics Pipeline DVC. The 'rayon', 'qoi', and 'qoiz' features are enabled by default for parallel RemoteFX encoding and QoI/QoiZ codec support. The server re-exports tokio and tokio_rustls as public surface so downstream consumers can use the same Tokio version. AutoDetect RTT measurement (MS-RDPBCGR §2.2.14) maintains an 8-sample sliding window and expires probes after 30 seconds. The MultifragmentUpdate reassembly buffer size is configurable via with_max_request_size(); FastPath fragments are capped at 16,374 bytes per packet.
response.purpose: ironrdp-server is an extensible skeleton library for implementing custom RDP servers. It accepts inbound TCP connections from RDP clients, negotiates capabilities and security (plain, TLS 1.2/1.3, or Hybrid/CredSSP), and coordinates the full RDP session lifecycle: MCS/X.224 connection layers, FastPath and X.224 input event dispatch, multi-codec display update encoding (RemoteFX, QoI, QoiZ, RDP 6.0 bitmap), pointer updates, static virtual channels (clipboard via cliprdr, audio via rdpsnd), and dynamic virtual channels (advanced input, display control, ECHO RTT measurement, optional EGFX/H.264 graphics pipeline). Custom server logic is injected by implementing the RdpServerInputHandler and RdpServerDisplay traits; all I/O is asynchronous via Tokio.

response.notes: The test data README notes that sample files were sourced from the Microsoft WindowsProtocolTestSuites RDP RDPEGFX H264TestData and from custom FreeRDP GFX pipeline dump code. The runtests.ps1 script drives the binary via `cargo run` against all four test data files with --close to exit on completion, serving as a smoke-test harness. The tool depends on ironrdp (workspace crate) for PDU parsing and ironrdp-glutin-renderer for OpenGL rendering; both are siblings in the IronRDP workspace but are catalog components dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp and dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-glutin-renderer respectively — however, neither appears explicitly by catalog name in any README, comment, or code (only via Cargo workspace keys), so they are omitted from explicit_cross_component_mentions per the strict rules.
response.purpose: ironrdp-replay-client is a standalone CLI tool for replaying RDP graphics pipeline data from binary dump files. It reads a binary file containing serialized RDP Graphics Pipeline PDUs (ServerPdu records), deserializes them one by one, and feeds them into a glutin-based OpenGL window via the ironrdp-glutin-renderer for visual playback. Its primary use case is debugging and iterating on the RDP rendering pipeline — developers can capture a graphics session to a file and then replay it at a configurable frame rate without needing a live RDP server.

response.notes: The `IRONRDP_LOG` environment variable (read by `tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter`) controls log verbosity; default level is WARN. Audio playback uses OS threads (not external processes) — one thread owns the CPAL `Stream` (which is not `Send`), an optional second thread handles Opus decoding. The `opus2` dependency is compiled with the `bundled` feature, so no external libopus installation is required. The example binary (`examples/cpal.rs`) demonstrates `DecodeStream` directly with synthetic PCM data. No filesystem I/O, network connections, or shell-outs are performed.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpsnd-native is a Rust library crate providing native audio backend implementations for the RDP RDPSND (Remote Desktop Sound) static virtual channel. It implements the `RdpsndClientHandler` trait from `ironrdp-rdpsnd` using the CPAL (Cross-Platform Audio Library) to output audio to the host system's default audio device. The crate supports PCM and Opus (via the bundled `opus2` crate) audio formats. Incoming audio data arrives through `RdpsndClientHandler` callbacks (`wave`, `set_volume`, `set_pitch`, `close`), is optionally decoded (Opus→PCM via a worker thread), and is streamed to the OS audio subsystem through a CPAL output stream managed on a dedicated thread.

response.notes: The crate is marked `publish = false` with a TODO to publish later. It is `no_std` (uses `extern crate alloc`), making it suitable for embedded or WASM targets. The `.RDP` file format is a line-oriented text format where each entry is `key:type:value`; supported types are `i` (i64) and `s` (string). The `load` function collects all parse errors rather than stopping on the first, enabling partial-success semantics. The `write` function is the inverse serialiser. There are no file I/O, network, or process-spawning operations — the crate is a pure in-memory codec. Both `doctest = false` and `test = false` are set in Cargo.toml, meaning tests are expected to live in integration test crates elsewhere.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpfile is a `no_std`-compatible Rust library crate that parses and serialises the Windows `.RDP` file format. It exposes three public functions — `load` (parse into a caller-supplied `PropertySet`), `parse` (parse into a newly-created `PropertySet` together with non-fatal errors), and `write` (serialise a `PropertySet` back to the `.RDP` text format). The crate handles the two value types defined by the `.RDP` format: integer (`i`) and string (`s`) key-value pairs separated by colons. It depends solely on `ironrdp-propertyset` for the `PropertySet` and `Value` types.

No data formats, network handling, or cross-component references are present because the implementation has not been written yet.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpeusb is a Rust library crate that implements the Remote Desktop Protocol: USB Devices Virtual Channel Extension (MS-RDPEUSB), used to redirect USB devices from a terminal client to a terminal server over an RDP virtual channel. The crate is currently a stub/placeholder — its sole source file is src/lib.rs which only includes the README as crate-level documentation, with no actual protocol logic implemented yet. It is part of the IronRDP project.

response.notes: Only compiled on macOS and Linux (cfg(any(target_os = "macos", target_os = "linux"))). The `file_base` string is a root path prefix injected at construction time via `NixRdpdrBackend::new(file_base)` — all RDPDR file paths are resolved relative to this base, acting as a virtual drive root on the host filesystem. Smart-card (SCARD) requests are silently accepted but not processed. Directory change notifications and lock-control requests are stubbed as TODOs. Volume label is hardcoded as "IRON_RDP" and filesystem name as "FAT32". The crate has no tests or doctests enabled (`test = false`, `doctest = false`).
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdpdr-native provides native (Unix/macOS/Linux) filesystem backend implementations for the RDP Device Redirection (RDPDR) static virtual channel. It implements the `RdpdrBackend` trait from `ironrdp-rdpdr`, exposing a `NixRdpdrBackend` struct that handles RDP drive I/O requests (create, read, write, close, query directory, query file/volume information, set file information) by mapping them to real host filesystem operations via Rust's `std::fs` and the `nix` crate. The crate serves as the operating-system-specific glue layer that translates Windows-style NT filesystem semantics (NtStatus codes, FileInformationClass, CreateDisposition, etc.) into POSIX syscalls.

response.notes: The crate's Cargo.toml description reads 'RDCleanPath PDU structure used by IronRDP web client and Devolutions Gateway'. It is consumed by ironrdp-web (the WASM web client), ironrdp-client (the native client), ironrdp-testsuite-core (tests), and the ffi crate. The protocol uses ASN.1 DER encoding (via the `der` crate, which is re-exported as a public API). VERSION_1 = 3390 (BASE_VERSION 3389 + 1, referencing the RDP port number). The PDU is directional: Request fields are client→proxy only; Response fields (server_cert_chain, server_addr) are proxy→client only. The `detect` method allows partial parsing of incoming bytes to identify whether a buffer starts with a RDCleanPath PDU without fully decoding it, supporting streaming transports.
response.purpose: ironrdp-rdcleanpath is a Rust library crate that defines the ASN.1 DER-encoded PDU (Protocol Data Unit) structures for the RDCleanPath protocol, used to establish proxied RDP connections via Devolutions Gateway. It provides encode/decode logic for the handshake between an RDP client and a proxy gateway: the client sends a request PDU carrying the RDP destination address, proxy auth token, preconnection blob, and an X.224 connection request; the proxy responds with the server's X.224 response, TLS certificate chain, and resolved server IP address. Error PDUs carry HTTP status codes, Winsock (WSA) error codes, or TLS alert codes to communicate failure details back to the client.

response.notes: The crate is `#![no_std]` (uses `extern crate alloc`), making it suitable for embedded or WASM targets. The `Value` enum comment explicitly references `.RDP file format` values, suggesting it is designed to back `.rdp` file parsing in sibling crates such as ironrdp-rdpfile. The `Cargo.toml` marks `publish = false` with a TODO, indicating it is an internal workspace crate. No other catalog components are mentioned by name in any source file.
response.purpose: ironrdp-propertyset is a `no_std`-compatible Rust library crate providing a typed key-value store (`PropertySet`) for configuration options, specifically designed to represent values of the `.RDP` file format. It stores keys as `Cow<'static, str>` and values as either integers (`i64`) or strings, backed by a `BTreeMap`. It exposes an `ExtractFrom` trait for ergonomic typed retrieval, with all operations emitting `tracing::debug` log entries. The crate has no external I/O, no network access, and no filesystem interaction — it is a pure in-memory data structure library intended to be consumed by other crates in the IronRDP workspace.

network_endpoints: tcp://<gw_endpoint> (outbound TCP to RD Gateway, then TLS-upgraded)
response.notes: GwClient implements tokio::io::AsyncRead and AsyncWrite, making it a drop-in transport for the rest of the IronRDP stack once the gateway tunnel is established. The crate hardcodes port 3389 when creating the channel. A background Tokio task handles keepalives (15-minute interval) and routes inbound/outbound data over the WebSocket. Basic auth credentials are Base64-encoded and sent as an HTTP Authorization header during the WebSocket upgrade. The crate depends on ironrdp-core and ironrdp-tls from the same IronRDP workspace but no catalog components are explicitly named in comments or README beyond its own ecosystem.
response.purpose: ironrdp-mstsgu is a Rust library implementing the MS-TSGU (Terminal Services Gateway Server Protocol) for IronRDP. It provides an MVP client implementation that connects through Microsoft Remote Desktop Gateway servers using HTTPS with WebSocket upgrade. The crate performs the full MS-TSGU connection handshake sequence (HandshakeReq/Resp, TunnelCreate/Resp, TunnelAuth/Resp, ChannelCreate/Resp) with basic authentication over TLS, then exposes the resulting tunnel as a Tokio AsyncRead/AsyncWrite stream (GwClient). Only the WebSocket transport path is implemented; legacy HTTP, HTTP-RPC, UDP, reconnection, and NTLM/SSPI authentication are explicitly out of scope.

response.notes: The crate's Cargo.toml declares a single intra-workspace dependency on ironrdp-pdu (path = "../ironrdp-pdu"). The lib section sets doctest = false and test = false, so the crate ships no test binaries. The from_web_button helper maps MDN Web API MouseEvent.button values (0–4) to internal MouseButton variants, indicating this library is intended to bridge browser/WASM input events to RDP protocol PDUs. The keyboard state array is sized at 512 bits (256 normal + 256 extended scancodes). No file I/O, network I/O, or subprocess invocations are present.
response.purpose: ironrdp-input is a Rust library that provides utilities for managing keyboard and mouse input state and constructing RDP FastPath input packets. It exposes an in-memory `Database` struct that tracks the current state of keyboard keys (by scancode, including extended keys), mouse buttons, and cursor position. Callers submit batches of `Operation` values (mouse moves, button presses/releases, key presses/releases, Unicode key events, wheel rotations); the database deduplicates against current state and returns a list of `FastPathInputEvent` PDUs ready to be sent over an RDP connection. It also provides a `synchronize_event` helper to emit lock-key synchronisation events, and a `release_all` method to release every held key and button. The `MouseButton` type provides mapping helpers for both Web (`MouseEvent.button`) and native button numbering conventions.

response.notes: Test asset binary files (encoded/decoded ZGFX PDUs and RDP6 bitmap samples) are embedded at compile time via include_bytes! macros — they are not read at runtime from the filesystem. The crate's public API surface is entirely in-process: callers pass byte slices in and receive decoded pixel buffers or encoded byte vectors out. No I/O, no network, no subprocess invocations. The Cargo.toml marks ironrdp-core and ironrdp-pdu as public dependencies (# public comment), meaning downstream crates re-export their types. The dev-dependency on the `bmp` crate is used only in tests to load/compare reference bitmap images from the test_assets directories.
response.purpose: ironrdp-graphics is a Rust library crate providing RDP image processing primitives and algorithms. It implements: ZGFX (RDP8) bulk data compression and decompression (sliding-window LZ-style codec); RemoteFX (RFX) tile encoding via DWT, quantization, subband reconstruction, and RLGR entropy coding; RDP6 bitmap stream decoding and encoding (YCoCg, ARGB, RLE variants); cursor/pointer PDU decoding into RGBA bitmaps (monochrome, color, large-pointer); color-space conversion between YCbCr/YUV and ARGB/RGBA pixel formats; pixel-format-aware image region copy; and rectangle-processing utilities. It is consumed as a dependency by higher-level IronRDP session and codec crates.

consumes_files: <runtime-path>/openh264*.so (or .dll/.dylib) — OpenH264 shared library loaded via libloading at a caller-supplied path
response.notes: GLSL shaders (avc.vert, avc420.frag, avc444.frag, avc444v2.frag, texture_shader.vert, texture_shader.frag) are embedded at compile time via include_str! and never read from disk at runtime. The OpenH264 shared library is loaded dynamically at runtime (libloading) from a caller-supplied PathBuf — not spawned as a process. The Renderer struct and its decode thread are only compiled when the 'openh264' Cargo feature is enabled (which is the default). The public API entry point is Renderer::new(window, graphic_receiver, gfx_dump_file, openh264_path): the caller owns the glutin window context and feeds ServerPdus over a Receiver channel. The most likely direct consumer of this crate is ironrdp-client-glutin (dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-client-glutin) based on naming, but no explicit catalog component name appears in code comments or README (no README exists). Uses hardcoded GLSL version '#version 410'.
produces_files: <optional-gfx-dump-path> — binary GFX PDU dump file written when gfx_dump_file is supplied to Renderer::new
response.purpose: ironrdp-glutin-renderer is a Rust library crate that provides an OpenGL-based rendering pipeline for IronRDP's RDP Graphics Pipeline (EGFX) extension. It receives RDP ServerPdu graphics PDUs over an in-process mpsc channel, decodes H.264-encoded video streams (AVC420, AVC444, AVC444v2) using Cisco's OpenH264 library loaded at runtime, and renders the decoded YUV frames onto a glutin/OpenGL window using custom GLSL shaders. The library spawns two background threads: one for decoding EGFX PDUs and one for OpenGL drawing, communicating via an internal render-event channel. Shader sources for AVC420, AVC444, AVC444v2, and a texture passthrough are embedded at compile time via include_str!.

response.notes: The library sets `publish = false` and disables doctests and unit tests (`doctest = false`, `test = false`), confirming it is only meant for use in fuzz-target crates (such as dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/fuzz). It uses the `arbitrary` crate for structured fuzzing input generation. The `NoopCliprdrFuzzBackend` hardcodes `now_ms()` to 0 to ensure reproducible fuzz runs. The component exercises code paths across at least nine sibling IronRDP crates, making it a cross-cutting test support library for the entire IronRDP PDU and protocol surface.
response.purpose: ironrdp-fuzzing is a support library that provides reusable test-case generators and oracle functions for fuzzing the IronRDP RDP protocol implementation. It exposes two public modules: `generators`, which supplies `Arbitrary`-derived structured inputs (e.g. `BitmapInput`) for consumption by libFuzzer-based fuzz targets; and `oracles`, which provides functions (`pdu_decode`, `rle_decompress_bitmap`, `rdp6_encode_bitmap_stream`, `rdp6_decode_bitmap_stream_to_rgb24`, `cliprdr_format`, `channel_process`, `cliprdr_channel_process`) that invoke the real IronRDP PDU parsing, graphics, clipboard, display-control, RDPDR, and RDPSND APIs on arbitrary bytes and report bugs by panicking. It is not a standalone binary; it is a library consumed by the fuzz targets in the adjacent `fuzz` crate.

response.notes: The crate unconditionally re-exports `ironrdp_async::*`, so it is a superset of ironrdp-async for callers that want futures-trait compatibility. Both stream wrappers use a fixed 1024-byte intermediate buffer for reads; a source comment notes this is less efficient than the tokio implementation. Tests and doctests are explicitly disabled in Cargo.toml (`test = false`, `doctest = false`). The only external dependency besides ironrdp-async is `futures-util` with the `io` feature. The `LocalFuturesStream` variant is notable for targeting environments without `Send + Sync` (e.g., browser/WASM via wasm-bindgen), consistent with the broader IronRDP web client goals.
response.purpose: ironrdp-futures is a small adapter library that implements the ironrdp-async `FramedRead`, `FramedWrite`, and `StreamWrapper` traits over the `futures-util` async I/O traits (`AsyncRead`/`AsyncWrite`). It provides two concrete stream wrapper types — `FuturesStream<S>` (requiring `Send + Sync` bounds for multi-threaded use) and `LocalFuturesStream<S>` (no Send/Sync, suitable for single-threaded or WASM contexts) — together with corresponding `FuturesFramed<S>` and `LocalFuturesFramed<S>` type aliases. It re-exports the entire `ironrdp-async` public API so consumers only need to depend on this crate when targeting the futures ecosystem.

response.notes: No dependencies declared in Cargo.toml beyond workspace-inherited fields — this crate is intentionally dependency-free. It has no tests or doctests enabled (doctest = false, test = false). The crate description says 'IronPDU generic error definition' (a typo — should be IronRDP). All other ironrdp-* crates that define custom error types are expected to depend on this crate as a foundational primitive.
response.purpose: ironrdp-error provides a lightweight, no_std-compatible generic Error type for the IronRDP project. It defines a parameterised `Error<Kind>` struct that wraps a static context string, an error kind value, and an optional boxed source error (when alloc/std features are enabled). It also provides a `Source` trait, an `ErrorReport` helper for formatting error chains, and conversions to `std::io::Error`. The crate supports three feature configurations: no_std without alloc (context and kind only), no_std with alloc (adds boxed source), and std (full error chaining via core::error::Error).

consumes_files: <user-supplied path to Cisco OpenH264 prebuilt shared library (openh264-libloading feature)>
response.notes: The crate exposes two processor types: GraphicsPipelineClient (implements DvcClientProcessor) which decodes incoming server PDUs and calls back into a GraphicsPipelineHandler trait, and GraphicsPipelineServer (implements DvcServerProcessor) which receives client capability advertisements, manages surface state, and queues outbound GfxPdus for transmission. Both communicate exclusively through the DVC 'Microsoft::Windows::RDS::Graphics' virtual channel. ZGFX decompression is used on the client side (incoming frames); ZGFX compression is optional on the server side (outgoing frames). The H264Decoder trait allows downstream consumers to supply their own codec implementation; the bundled OpenH264 integration is a concrete implementation but is disabled by default due to licensing requirements.
response.purpose: ironrdp-egfx is a Rust library implementing the MS-RDPEGFX (Graphics Pipeline Extension) protocol for IronRDP. It provides PDU types and both client-side and server-side processors for the Display Pipeline Virtual Channel Extension (DVC channel name 'Microsoft::Windows::RDS::Graphics'), including capability negotiation (V8 through V10.7), surface lifecycle management, H.264 AVC420/AVC444 video streaming, ZGFX compression/decompression, frame flow control with acknowledgment, and Quality of Experience (QoE) metrics collection. The H.264 decode path is pluggable via the H264Decoder trait, with an optional OpenH264 integration available through the openh264-bundled and openh264-libloading feature flags.

network_endpoints: rdp-dvc://ECHO (Dynamic Virtual Channel named 'ECHO' per MS-RDPEECO)
response.notes: The channel name constant is 'ECHO' (CHANNEL_NAME pub const in lib.rs). EchoClient receives an EchoRequestPdu on the DVC and immediately replies with an EchoResponsePdu containing the same payload, acting as a loopback responder. EchoServer sends EchoRequestPdu messages and discards received EchoResponsePdu payloads; it supports an optional initial_request payload sent when the channel opens via with_initial_request(). Both processors are stateless beyond the optional initial_request field. The library has no filesystem I/O and no direct socket handling — all transport is delegated to the ironrdp-dvc layer. Cargo.toml marks ironrdp-core, ironrdp-dvc, and ironrdp-pdu as public dependencies.
response.purpose: ironrdp-echo is a Rust library implementing the Virtual Channel Echo Extension [MS-RDPEECO] over Dynamic Virtual Channels [MS-RDPEDYC]. It provides parsing and serialization of ECHO_REQUEST_PDU and ECHO_RESPONSE_PDU, an EchoClient struct implementing DvcClientProcessor that receives requests and echoes them back as responses, and an EchoServer struct implementing DvcServerProcessor that sends requests and processes responses. The component integrates with the ironrdp-dvc DVC framework and is designed to be embedded in larger RDP client or server implementations.

network_endpoints: unix://<pipe_name> (Unix domain socket server — listens and accepts one client)
pipe://\\.\pipe\<pipe_name> (Windows named pipe server — byte-mode, max 2 instances)
response.notes: The component acts as a named-pipe server (listener side): it binds/creates the pipe and waits for a single client connection. On Unix, stale socket files from previous runs are automatically removed before rebinding. The worker loop supports reconnection (NextWorkerState::Reconnect) when the pipe client closes unexpectedly, but aborts permanently when the DVC mpsc channel is closed (session ended). An internal std::sync::mpsc-to-tokio::sync::mpsc bridge thread is used to shuttle data from the synchronous DvcProcessor::process call into the async worker. The crate has no binary targets (lib only, doctest and test both disabled in Cargo.toml). README notes it was originally designed for Devolutions Remote Desktop Manager (RDM) integration.
produces_files: <pipe_name> (Unix domain socket file created by UnixListener::bind at runtime-supplied path)
response.purpose: ironrdp-dvc-pipe-proxy is a Rust library crate that provides a `DvcNamedPipeProxy` struct implementing IronRDP's `DvcProcessor` and `DvcClientProcessor` traits. It bridges RDP Device Virtual Channel (DVC) traffic between an active IronRDP session and an external process connected via a named pipe: on Unix it uses Unix domain sockets, and on Windows it uses Windows named pipes. The proxy spawns a dedicated worker thread with its own Tokio runtime that concurrently reads from the pipe (forwarding data as DVC messages into the IronRDP session via a callback) and writes to the pipe (forwarding DVC payloads received from the RDP host). It supports automatic reconnection when a pipe client disconnects and is designed to allow custom DVC logic to be centralised in one place and reused across multiple RDP clients (IronRDP, FreeRDP, mstsc, etc.) without duplicating code.

response.notes: The crate is gated with #![cfg(windows)] and will not compile or link on non-Windows targets. DLL plugin paths are passed at runtime by callers (e.g. C:\Windows\System32\webauthn.dll); no config files are read. The README and lib.rs module doc explicitly cite ironrdp-dvc-pipe-proxy as the prior art for the on_write_dvc callback pattern. Cargo.toml dependencies include ironrdp-core, ironrdp-pdu, ironrdp-dvc, and ironrdp-svc (all sibling crates in the IronRDP workspace), but these appear as Cargo dependencies rather than as explicit catalog-name mentions in prose. The public API surface is load_dvc_plugin (returns Vec<DvcComChannel>) and DvcComChannel itself, intended to be registered with DrdynvcClient via with_dynamic_channel().
response.purpose: ironrdp-dvc-com-plugin is a Windows-only Rust library crate that loads native Windows DVC (Dynamic Virtual Channel) client plugin DLLs (such as webauthn.dll) and bridges them into IronRDP's DVC infrastructure. It uses the Win32 LoadLibraryW API to load the DLL at runtime, calls the VirtualChannelGetInstance export to obtain IWTSPlugin COM objects, and provides a Rust implementation of IWTSVirtualChannelManager that routes data bidirectionally between the plugin's COM callbacks and IronRDP's DVC session loop. A dedicated 'dvc-com-worker' thread owns all COM objects (which are !Send); DvcComChannel structs implement DvcProcessor + Send and communicate with the COM thread via std::sync::mpsc channels. Outbound data from the plugin (IWTSVirtualChannel::Write) is encoded as DVC PDUs and injected into the active session loop via a caller-supplied on_write_dvc callback.

response.notes: The channel name constant is 'Microsoft::Windows::RDS::DisplayControl'. The client side implements DvcClientProcessor and DvcProcessor from ironrdp-dvc; the server side implements DvcServerProcessor and DvcProcessor. All PDU encode/decode logic is self-contained in src/pdu/mod.rs using ironrdp-core cursor primitives. No filesystem I/O, network sockets, or subprocess calls are made; all communication is via in-process trait method calls over the DVC abstraction. The server starts by advertising a hardcoded capability of max 1 monitor at 3840x2400. The explicit_cross_component_mentions list uses the catalog names for the four direct Cargo.toml workspace-path dependencies: ironrdp-core, ironrdp-dvc, ironrdp-pdu, ironrdp-svc.
response.purpose: ironrdp-displaycontrol is a Rust library implementing the RDP Display Control Virtual Channel Extension (MS-RDPEDISP). It provides encoding and decoding of Display Control DVC PDUs — specifically the capabilities exchange (DISPLAYCONTROL_CAPS_PDU) and monitor layout notification (DISPLAYCONTROL_MONITOR_LAYOUT_PDU) — and exposes both a client-side processor (DisplayControlClient) that sends monitor layout changes to a remote server and receives server capabilities, and a server-side processor (DisplayControlServer) that advertises capabilities and receives monitor layout updates from the client via a handler trait.

response.notes: The crate has a single workspace-local dependency: ironrdp-error (../ironrdp-error), which provides the generic Error<Kind> wrapper used by DecodeError and EncodeError. The crate is intentionally minimal and no-std compatible (feature-gated via 'std' and 'alloc' features). It performs no filesystem access, network I/O, or process spawning — it only provides trait adapters for std::io::Read and std::io::Write on its cursor types. The Encode trait is explicitly object-safe (verified via assert_obj_safe! macro), enabling dynamic dispatch for heterogeneous PDU collections. Zero-copy design: Decode<'de> carries a lifetime parameter so implementations can borrow from the input byte slice. WriteBuf has a 16 KiB maximum capacity on clear operations.
response.purpose: ironrdp-core is a foundational, no-std-compatible Rust library crate that provides the core binary serialization and deserialization infrastructure for the IronRDP RDP protocol suite. It defines the Encode and Decode traits, ReadCursor and WriteCursor byte-buffer primitives, the WriteBuf growable buffer, error types (DecodeError, EncodeError, DecodeErrorKind, EncodeErrorKind), and a suite of helper macros (ensure_size!, invalid_field_err!, write_padding!, etc.) used uniformly across all other IronRDP crates to read and write RDP PDUs over raw byte slices.

response.notes: The component's Cargo.toml declares path dependencies on ironrdp-svc, ironrdp-core, ironrdp-error, and ironrdp-pdu (all sibling crates in the same workspace, catalogued as dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-* entries), but those crate short-names do not appear verbatim as catalog component IDs, so they are not listed in explicit_cross_component_mentions per the strict rules. The LicenseCache trait (get_license/store_license) enables optional persistent license caching by delegating to a caller-supplied Arc<dyn LicenseCache> — the component itself performs no direct file I/O; concrete implementations live outside this crate. CredSSP/NLA authentication is handled internally via the sspi crate (which may communicate with a KDC/domain controller), but the connector does not directly open any sockets — all network I/O is delegated to the embedding application. The caller must also perform the TLS handshake externally and signal completion via mark_security_upgrade_as_done(). SmartCard authentication is supported through SmartCardIdentity (DER-encoded X.509 certificate + RSA 2048-bit private key). The component exposes an arbitrary feature-gated derive impl for fuzzing support.
response.purpose: ironrdp-connector is a Rust library crate that implements the client-side RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) connection state machine. It orchestrates the full RDP handshake sequence — from initial X.224 connection negotiation through Enhanced Security upgrade (TLS/CredSSP), MCS channel connection, license exchange, capabilities exchange, and connection finalization — producing and consuming binary PDU bytes that the caller is responsible for transmitting over a network transport. It explicitly does NOT support legacy RDP security (RC4) and only supports Enhanced RDP Security (TLS and CredSSP/NLA). The component is transport-agnostic: it acts as a pure state machine that processes incoming byte slices and writes outgoing PDUs into a caller-supplied buffer.

response.notes: The Windows backend (WinClipboard) is non-Send/non-Sync and must be used exclusively on the GUI/main thread because Windows does not allow cross-thread window subclassing. The backend factory (WinCliprdrBackendFactory) is Send and can be passed to other threads to create CliprdrBackend instances. The temporary directory '.cliprdr' is referenced as a path string returned by `CliprdrBackend::temporary_directory()` but the library itself does not directly create or write to it. File transfer (FileContentsRequest/FileContentsResponse) is not yet implemented in the Windows backend.
response.purpose: ironrdp-cliprdr-native provides native OS clipboard backend implementations for IronRDP's RDP Clipboard Redirection (CLIPRDR) static virtual channel. It bridges the host operating system clipboard with the RDP CLIPRDR protocol by implementing the `CliprdrBackend` and `CliprdrBackendFactory` traits from `ironrdp-cliprdr`. On Windows, it creates a hidden message-only window that listens for clipboard change events via `AddClipboardFormatListener`, subclasses the window to receive WinAPI clipboard messages, and communicates asynchronously with the CLIPRDR SVC layer through an MPSC channel. It also provides a no-op `StubCliprdrBackend` for platforms where no native clipboard integration is available.

response.notes: The crate depends on ironrdp-core for its Decode/Encode traits (ReadCursor, WriteCursor, DecodeResult, EncodeResult) and the png crate (version 0.18) for PNG encoding/decoding. Public API surface: bitmap::dib_to_png, bitmap::dibv5_to_png, bitmap::png_to_cf_dib, bitmap::png_to_cf_dibv5, html::cf_html_to_plain_html, html::plain_html_to_cf_html. The README notes a security/overflow audit guarantee for 32- and 64-bit architectures. The crate is intended to be used by higher-level clipboard handling crates such as ironrdp-cliprdr and ironrdp-cliprdr-native. Maximum bitmap buffer size is capped at 64 MB and maximum image dimensions are constrained to 10,000 pixels per side.
response.purpose: ironrdp-cliprdr-format is a Rust library that provides conversion logic between RDP-specific clipboard formats and widely used formats. It handles two clipboard format families: bitmap formats (CF_DIB and CF_DIBV5) converted to/from PNG, and HTML formats (CF_HTML) converted to/from plain HTML strings. The bitmap module decodes Windows BITMAPINFOHEADER and BITMAPV5HEADER structures, performs BGRA-to-RGBA color channel swapping, and handles bottom-up vs top-down row ordering. The HTML module parses CF_HTML clipboard format headers to extract the HTML fragment, and generates CF_HTML envelopes from plain HTML. The crate has been audited for overflow safety on 32- and 64-bit architectures.

consumes_files: <openh264_path> (CLI-configurable path to Cisco's OpenH264 shared library)
<gfx_dump_file> (optional, CLI-configurable path for GFX stream dump)
network_endpoints: rdp+tcp://<user-specified host>:<port> (outbound TCP connection to RDP server, then upgraded to TLS)
response.notes: The binary is compiled as 'ironrdp-gui-client' (cargo bin name). Log level is controlled by the IRONRDP_LOG_LEVEL environment variable. TLS certificate verification is intentionally bypassed via a custom NoCertificateVerification implementation; the SSLKEYLOGFILE env var is supported for Wireshark TLS decryption. The glutin event loop must run on the main thread while async RDP processing runs on tokio worker threads; a sync_channel bridges the two. The component has an explicit path dependency on ironrdp-glutin-renderer at ../glutin-renderer. The README directly references ironrdp-replay-client as a companion tool for debugging renderer artifacts captured via --gfx_dump_file.
response.purpose: ironrdp-client-glutin is an experimental GPU-accelerated RDP GUI client that connects to a remote Windows desktop server over TCP/TLS, performs the full RDP connection sequence (including SSPI/NLA authentication), and renders the remote framebuffer in a native desktop window using the glutin windowing library and OpenGL. It translates local keyboard and mouse events into RDP fast-path input PDUs sent back to the server, and optionally captures the GFX stream to a dump file for later replay and debugging with the ironrdp-replay-client tool.

response.notes: The Cargo.toml description says 'IronRDP utilities for ironrdp-cfgstore', slightly different from the README which says 'utilities for ironrdp-propertyset'. The source file includes a comment suggesting the file could be auto-generated from a reference gist (https://gist.github.com/awakecoding/838c7fe2ed3a6208e3ca5d8af25363f6). All property-set keys accessed are standard RDP .rdp file property names. The crate has both `doctest = false` and `test = false`, meaning it has no tests of its own. The `clear_text_password` accessor is explicitly annotated 'use for testing only'.
response.purpose: ironrdp-cfg is a small Rust library crate that extends the `PropertySet` type (from ironrdp-propertyset) with typed accessor methods for well-known RDP connection configuration keys. It provides a `PropertySetExt` trait implemented on `PropertySet` exposing named getters for fields such as `full address`, `server port`, `gatewayhostname`, `username`, `kdcproxyurl`, and others that map directly to standard RDP file / property-set key names. The crate acts purely as a thin convenience layer over ironrdp-propertyset; it has no executable entry point, does no I/O itself, and is marked `publish = false` pending future release.

response.notes: The crate has no runtime dependencies — only criterion is used in dev-dependencies for benchmarks (bulk_compression bench). It is designed to be consumed as a library by other IronRDP crates that need RDP bulk compression (e.g. session or connector layers). The alloc feature enables heap allocation without std, making it suitable for WebAssembly targets. Compression is skipped for payloads ≤50 bytes or ≥16384 bytes. The BulkCompressor tracks cumulative compression metrics (total_compressed_bytes, total_uncompressed_bytes, compression_ratio) across calls.
response.purpose: ironrdp-bulk is a pure Rust library crate implementing RDP bulk compression and decompression algorithms for the IronRDP project. It supports all four RDP compression levels: RDP 4.0 (MPPC with 8 KB history buffer), RDP 5.0 (MPPC with 64 KB history buffer), RDP 6.0 (NCRUSH Huffman-based), and RDP 6.1 (XCRUSH two-level chunk matching + MPPC). The crate exposes a single BulkCompressor coordinator struct that routes compress/decompress calls to the appropriate algorithm based on the configured compression level or the type bits in RDP packet flags. It has zero unsafe code, no runtime dependencies, and supports no_std environments (including WebAssembly) via an alloc feature flag.

network_endpoints: tcp://<rdp-server> (via caller-supplied Read+Write stream; no address hardcoded)
response.notes: The README explicitly names ironrdp-async as the async counterpart this crate replaces for single-threaded use. Cargo.toml marks ironrdp-connector, ironrdp-core, and ironrdp-pdu as public dependencies (# public comment). The session.rs file is essentially empty (only a TODO), meaning active-session helpers have not yet been implemented — connection setup is complete but post-connect I/O is left to the caller. The Framed<S> type is fully generic over any std::io::Read+Write, so it can wrap TCP sockets, TLS streams, or test stubs equally. The CredSSP flow uses a NetworkClient trait object for out-of-band SSPI/Kerberos token exchange, meaning the component may indirectly trigger additional network calls through that abstraction.
response.purpose: ironrdp-blocking is a blocking I/O abstraction library that wraps the IronRDP RDP protocol state machines. It provides a synchronous (non-async) API for performing an RDP client connection sequence — including the TLS/NLA security upgrade step and CredSSP/Kerberos authentication — over any stream implementing std::io::Read + Write. It exports a generic Framed<S> struct for PDU-framed I/O and free functions (connect_begin, mark_as_upgraded, connect_finalize, single_sequence_step) that drive the ClientConnector state machine step-by-step without requiring an async runtime. It is intended as a simpler alternative to ironrdp-async for environments where concurrency is not needed.

response.notes: The crate depends on ironrdp-server with the feature flag `__bench` (a double-underscore-prefixed feature, indicating it is an internal/unstable bench-only API surface). It references `ironrdp_server::bench::encoder::rfx` which is gated behind that feature. The `publish = false` field and `version = "0.0.0"` confirm this is an internal tooling crate never intended for crates.io. There is a noted FIXME comment in the bench code suggesting a potential stride bug in `rfx_enc_bench` (stride is set to 64*4 for a 2048-wide bitmap). The catalog names `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-graphics`, `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-pdu`, and `dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/ironrdp-server` are the only cross-component references; these are listed in `explicit_cross_component_mentions` using their exact catalog names.
response.purpose: ironrdp-bench is a Criterion-based microbenchmark suite for performance-critical paths in the IronRDP RDP implementation. It benchmarks three specific operations: RFX (RemoteFX) single-tile encoding (`rfx_enc_tile`), full-frame RFX encoding across a 2048×2048 bitmap (`rfx_enc`), and YCbCr color-space conversion of a 64×64 tile (`to_ycbcr`). The crate is not published and carries no library or binary targets — it exists solely as a Criterion benchmark harness invoked via `cargo bench`.

response.notes: The crate has no direct dependency on tokio or any other async runtime — it uses only RPITIT-style associated Future types in its traits, making it runtime-agnostic. Concrete implementations of FramedRead/FramedWrite are expected to be provided by consumers such as ironrdp-tokio (tokio AsyncRead/AsyncWrite) or ironrdp-blocking. The session.rs file is a stub (// TODO: active session async helpers) with no exported symbols yet. The NetworkClient trait is the integration point for CredSSP: callers must supply a concrete implementation that forwards SSPI network requests (e.g., Kerberos KDC or NTLM proxy calls) to the appropriate server. The crate re-exports the bytes crate as a public dependency.
response.purpose: ironrdp-async provides Future-based wrappers around the IronRDP connection state machines, abstracting over the lower-level ironrdp-connector and ironrdp-session crates. It defines runtime-agnostic async I/O traits (FramedRead, FramedWrite, StreamWrapper) and a buffered Framed<S> wrapper for reading/writing RDP PDU frames. It exposes async functions for the two-phase RDP connection handshake (connect_begin drives pre-TLS negotiation; connect_finalize drives post-TLS negotiation including CredSSP authentication) and a NetworkClient trait for routing SSPI/CredSSP network requests through caller-supplied async transport.

response.notes: The crate depends on ironrdp-core for cursor-based binary encode/decode primitives (Encode, Decode, ReadCursor, WriteCursor) and on ironrdp-dvc for the DvcEncode marker trait that marks ServerPdu as encodable into a Dynamic Virtual Channel. The channel identifier constant CHANNEL_NAME = 'FreeRDP::Advanced::Input' is the string consumers must use when registering or dispatching this DVC. No filesystem I/O, no network I/O, and no subprocess spawning occur in this crate; it is a pure PDU codec library.
response.purpose: ironrdp-ainput implements the FreeRDP 'Advanced Input' (AInput) dynamic virtual channel protocol for RDP sessions. It provides encode/decode support for the AInput channel PDUs: a version negotiation PDU (server-to-client) and a mouse event PDU (client-to-server) carrying high-precision relative/absolute mouse coordinates and button flags. The channel name is 'FreeRDP::Advanced::Input' and the protocol version is 1.0. This crate is a pure library (no binary, no I/O) used by higher-level RDP session components to send advanced input events over a DVC (Dynamic Virtual Channel).

response.notes: The Acceptor is the top-level state machine; it implements the ironrdp_connector::Sequence trait. Sub-sequences ChannelConnectionSequence and FinalizationSequence are internal to the crate but are exported publicly. CredSSP authentication is performed via ironrdp-connector's SSPI bindings, supporting NTLM (NtlmConfig) and Kerberos (KerberosServerConfig/NegotiateConfig) backends; the NetworkClient trait (from ironrdp-async) is used to forward SSPI network requests during the CredSSP loop. For HYBRID_EX protocol, an EarlyUserAuthResult PDU is sent back to the client after CredSSP regardless of outcome. Credentials received in SecureSettingsExchange (TLS-mode only) are surfaced in AcceptorResult.credentials; CredSSP-mode connections leave that field None. The Acceptor also supports deactivation-reactivation via new_deactivation_reactivation, re-entering the state machine at CapabilitiesSendServer. No filesystem I/O or subprocess spawning occurs anywhere in the crate.
response.purpose: ironrdp-acceptor is a Rust library crate that provides state machines to drive the server-side of an RDP connection acceptance sequence (MS-RDPBCGR). It implements the full server handshake: negotiation of security protocol (Classic RDP, SSL/TLS, HYBRID/NLA, HYBRID_EX), optional TLS upgrade signalling, CredSSP/NTLM/Kerberos authentication via SSPI, MCS channel connection, secure settings and licensing exchange, capability negotiation, monitor layout advertisement, and connection finalization. Callers drive the sequence by passing a framed async stream through three top-level async functions — accept_begin, accept_credssp, accept_finalize — receiving an AcceptorResult that carries the negotiated channel set, client capabilities, channel IDs, and any credentials supplied by the client.

response.notes: The component imports UserInteraction and IronError types from iron-remote-desktop, and Backend plus RDP extension helpers (displayControl, preConnectionBlob, kdcProxyUrl, init) from iron-remote-desktop-rdp. These modules are loaded both as static JS assets (app.html line 12) and as ES module imports in Svelte components. Session parameters (credentials, gateway URL, auth token, desktop size, PCB, KDC proxy URL) are serialised as base64-encoded JSON and passed between the /session and /popup-session routes via URL query parameter. The token server URL is injected at build time via the VITE_IRON_TOKEN_SERVER_URL environment variable; if absent, users must supply the auth token manually. Default credentials and gateway address (ws://localhost:7171/jet/rdp) are hardcoded for demo purposes. The README specifies a dependency on Devolutions Gateway v2023.1.1+ with a provisioner key pair for token validation, and optionally the tokengen CLI tool from the Devolutions Gateway repository.
response.purpose: iron-svelte-client is a SvelteKit-based web application that demonstrates and wraps the iron-remote-desktop web component to provide a browser-based RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) client. It renders a login form that collects credentials, gateway address, and display options, then establishes an RDP-over-WebSocket session through a Devolutions Gateway proxy. The app exposes two routes: a main /session page and a /popup-session page that opens the desktop in a child window. It is documented as a simple wrapper intended to demonstrate the iron-remote-desktop API rather than for direct production use.

response.notes: The `public/package.json` carries version 0.6.1 (the published semver), whereas the workspace root `package.json` is `0.0.0` / `private: true`. This split-manifest pattern means the `public/` directory is the npm publish root — consumers install from here via `npm install @devolutions/iron-remote-desktop-rdp`. The build artifacts (`iron-remote-desktop-rdp.js` and `index.d.ts`) are generated by Vite into this directory and are absent from version control. The README (in the parent directory, outside this component's strict path boundary) names `iron-remote-desktop` and `iron-svelte-client` as related sibling components, but neither reference appears within the `public/` path itself.
response.purpose: The `public/` directory is the npm distribution package root for `@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop-rdp`, which is an RDP backend implementation of the `RemoteDesktopModule` interface from iron-remote-desktop. The directory contains only the published `package.json` manifest (version 0.6.1), pointing to the Vite-built `iron-remote-desktop-rdp.js` ES module and its accompanying `index.d.ts` TypeScript declarations. At build time, the parent component's Vite build process generates these artifacts into this directory, which is then the root used when publishing to npm (JFrog Artifactory under the `@devolutions` scope). The library wraps the `ironrdp-web` WebAssembly crate, exposing `SessionBuilder`, `DesktopSize`, `InputTransaction`, `ClipboardData`, `DeviceEvent`, `Extension`, and `RdpFile` types to TypeScript consumers.

response.notes: The `public/` subdirectory is the npm package root published to JFrog Artifactory. The `package.json` here declares version 0.10.1 (the release version), distinct from the parent workspace `package.json` which uses `0.0.0` with `private: true`. The `files` field in `public/package.json` limits the published payload to `iron-remote-desktop.js` and `index.d.ts`. The `test.html` demonstrates minimal instantiation: import the JS bundle, declare `<iron-remote-desktop isvisible="false" desktopwidth="600" desktopheight="400"/>`, listen for the `ready` event, then call `e.detail.irgUserInteraction.setVisibility(true)`. The sibling `iron-svelte-client` catalog component (`dull/frontend/launcher-web/rust/ironrdp/iron-svelte-client`) is identified in the parent README as the intended development test client for this library, but that reference does not appear in files within the `public/` directory itself. The actual WebSocket connection to a Devolutions Gateway proxy is established at runtime by the consumer via the `proxyAddress` argument to `connect()`; no fixed endpoint is hardcoded in the distributed artifact.
response.purpose: The `public/` directory is the npm package publication root for `@devolutions/iron-remote-desktop` (v0.10.1). It contains the `package.json` manifest that points to the compiled `iron-remote-desktop.js` bundle and `index.d.ts` type declarations, plus a `test.html` page demonstrating usage of the `<iron-remote-desktop/>` Web Custom Element. The package is described as a backend-agnostic Web Component for remote desktop protocols. The `test.html` shows the component being instantiated with HTML attributes (`isvisible`, `desktopwidth`, `desktopheight`), listening for a `ready` DOM event, and accessing `e.detail.irgUserInteraction` to call `setVisibility(true)`.

response.notes: Requires Rust nightly toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml specifies channel = 'nightly'). All fuzz targets are thin wrappers: pdu_decoding, cliprdr_format, and channel_processing pass raw &[u8] bytes to ironrdp_fuzzing oracles; bitmap_stream and rle_decompression use the structured BitmapInput generator type for structured fuzzing via libfuzzer's derive(Arbitrary) integration. The package sets publish = false and cargo-fuzz = true metadata, indicating it is purely a developer/CI testing artifact, never published to crates.io. The local dependency path ../crates/ironrdp-fuzzing resolves to the ironrdp-fuzzing crate in the IronRDP workspace.
response.purpose: A cargo-fuzz harness for the IronRDP RDP client library. It defines five libFuzzer fuzz targets — pdu_decoding, bitmap_stream, rle_decompression, cliprdr_format, and channel_processing — each of which feeds coverage-guided random bytes into oracle functions provided by the ironrdp-fuzzing crate. The harness is used to detect crashes, panics, and unexpected behaviour in PDU decoding, RDP6 bitmap encoding/decoding, interleaved RLE decompression, clipboard-format parsing, and virtual-channel processing.

response.notes: The library does not perform any network I/O itself; CredSSP flows yield `NetworkRequest` values (with TCP/UDP/HTTP/HTTPS protocol fields and a URL string) back to the .NET caller, which is responsible for executing them. The generated C# files in `dotnet/Devolutions.IronRdp/Generated/` are committed to the repository and correspond 1:1 with the `#[diplomat::bridge]` annotations in the Rust source — callers regenerate them by running `cargo xtask ffi bindings`. Windows-specific features (clipboard via WinClipboard, DLL version resources) are gated with `#[cfg(windows)]`; the non-Windows clipboard stub returns a `WrongOSError`. On Windows, the build script writes a `version.rc` resource file to `$OUT_DIR` and embeds it into the DLL using the `embed-resource` crate. The DVC pipe proxy module bridges named-pipe IPC channels into the RDP dynamic virtual channel protocol, communicating via an `std::sync::mpsc` channel between the native callback and the FFI caller.
response.purpose: The IronRDP FFI crate is a Diplomat-based foreign-function interface layer that exposes the IronRDP Rust RDP client library to foreign-language callers. It compiles to both a static and dynamic native library (libironrdp / DevolutionsIronRdp.dll) and provides pre-generated .NET (C#) bindings in the `dotnet/Devolutions.IronRdp/Generated/` directory. The FFI surface covers the full RDP connection lifecycle: connection configuration and establishment (`connector`, `credssp`), active-session processing (`session`, `pdu`), keyboard and mouse input (`input`), graphics/pointer decoding (`graphics`, `session/image`), clipboard integration via CLIPRDR (`clipboard`), dynamic virtual channels and named-pipe proxying (`dvc`, `svc`), and the RDCleanPath proxy-authentication protocol (`rdcleanpath`). Currently, only the .NET target is officially supported, and build tooling is driven via `cargo xtask ffi install/build/bindings`.

consumes_files: <RGBX_INPUT_FILENAME> (user-supplied raw RGBX video file, path passed as CLI argument)
response.notes: The binary reads raw RGBX frames (width*height*4 bytes each) sequentially from a file. It depends on the `ironrdp` crate at `../crates/ironrdp` with the `server`, `pdu`, and `__bench` feature flags — the `__bench` flag is a gated internal API surface exposed specifically for benchmarking. Optional codec features `qoi` and `qoiz` are enabled by default via Cargo features. Logging is configured via the `IRONRDP_LOG` environment variable. The `publish = false` manifest flag confirms this is an internal development tool never published to crates.io.
response.purpose: A standalone performance benchmarking binary (`perfenc`) for the IronRDP server-side display update encoder. It reads a raw RGBX video file frame-by-frame, feeds each frame through the IronRDP `UpdateEncoder` using a selectable codec (RemoteFX, Bitmap, QOI, QOIz, or none), measures encoded output size versus raw input size, and reports compression ratio statistics to stdout. It is not a library and produces no build artifacts — it exists solely to measure encoder throughput and compression efficiency.

response.notes: The Cargo.toml lists ironrdp-async and ironrdp-web as path dependencies (../IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-async and ../IronRDP/crates/ironrdp-web respectively), meaning those catalog components are compile-time dependencies even though the single source file only directly uses tokio traits. Several other dependency entries (ironrdp git/workspace variants, getrandom) are commented out, indicating this crate was previously more feature-rich or is in transition. The crate is marked publish = false and uses Rust edition 2024.
response.purpose: A minimal Rust utility library crate within the launcher-web workspace that defines a single blanket-implemented trait `AsyncStream`, combining `tokio::io::AsyncRead` and `tokio::io::AsyncWrite` (plus `Unpin`) into one object-safe bound. It exists solely to provide a shared async I/O stream abstraction that other crates in the same workspace (webrdp, etc.) can depend on instead of repeating the compound trait bound everywhere.

response.notes: The openapi-merge.json config merges launcherweb_openapi.json and secure_file_transfer_openapi.json (with /secure-file-transfer path prefix) into the single openapi.json input consumed by @hey-api/openapi-ts. Every launcher API endpoint is duplicated under both /api/... and /launcherapi/... path prefixes — the launcherapi prefix is used when the launcher client routes through a reverse-proxy path. Bearer token authentication is injected via setApiClientConfig (src/runtime/client.ts), which also sets up qs dot-notation query string serialization. The only known consumer of this package in the monorepo is dull/frontend/launcher-web (confirmed via frontend/apps/launcher-web/package.json). The build/generate scripts (openapi-ts, tsc) are dev-time toolchain invocations, not runtime process spawning.
response.purpose: @dull/launcher-api-client is a TypeScript library package that provides a fully-typed, auto-generated HTTP client for the Dull Launcher API. It is generated from two merged OpenAPI specifications — one for the core launcher web API (launcherweb_openapi.json) and one for the secure file transfer service (secure_file_transfer_openapi.json) — using the @hey-api/openapi-ts toolchain backed by axios. The library exposes typed SDK functions and data types covering: user connections (create, poll state, cancel, close), application launches (start/open/error/close lifecycle), EVM management, launch target listing and validation, MFA device registration and challenge flow (TOTP and DullMfa), session recordings, password management (change and forgotten-password flow), launcher version checks, secure file object upload/download/archive/delete, and audit log retrieval. Callers configure a baseURL and Bearer auth token via setApiClientConfig; every generated function supports an optional custom client instance override. The SDK duplicates each endpoint under both /api/... and /launcherapi/... path prefixes, accommodating direct and proxied access patterns.

response.notes: The component consumes workspace packages @dull/form, @dull/ui, @dull/icons, and @dull/config (corresponding to catalog components dull/frontend/form, dull/frontend/ui, dull/frontend/icons, and dull/frontend/config) but these names do not appear verbatim in READMEs or code comments so they are omitted from explicit_cross_component_mentions per the strict rule. The theme applies client-specific logic: for the WEB_LAUNCHER client ('dull-launcher-web'), the password-reset URL is overridden to /web-launcher/forgot-password and a 'Forgot password' link is shown; for other clients it is hidden. The app-menu component contains hardcoded navigation links to /realm-manager, /web-launcher, and /secure-file-transfer-portal, which are sibling frontend applications in the monorepo. Form submissions (login credentials, TOTP codes, OTP) are POSTed to the dynamic `url.loginAction` URL provided by Keycloak at runtime — no fixed backend address is configured in this component. The `keycloakify` binary is invoked from npm scripts to produce the distributable JAR; `mvn` is required in PATH as a transitive build dependency for Keycloakify's multi-version JAR assembly. Storybook (port 6006) provides isolated story-based development and visual testing for all page and layout components. The theme name registered with Keycloakify is 'dull' (vite.config.ts themeName field).
response.purpose: A Keycloakify v11-based Keycloak login theme that replaces Keycloak's default FreeMarker authentication pages with React components styled with Tailwind CSS. It renders six page types — login, password-reset, TOTP configuration, OTP entry, error, and informational — receiving Keycloak runtime state via the global `window.kcContext` object injected by the server at page-load. The theme is registered under the name 'dull' and applies per-client customisation for three configured client IDs (realm-manager, web-launcher, secure-portal). It builds into a deployable Keycloak JAR artifact via the `keycloakify build` CLI and Maven.

response.notes: The component depends on three monorepo workspace packages — @dull/ui (components: Heading, Logo, IllustratedMessage, Icon, Text), @dull/icons (CaretRightIcon), and @dull/config (ESLint/Prettier/TypeScript configs) — corresponding to catalog entries dull/frontend/ui, dull/frontend/icons, and dull/frontend/config, but these are referenced by npm package name rather than catalog name. The Nginx config is generated at container startup via envsubst substituting the NGINX_DOMAIN environment variable into scripts/indexpage.conf.template. The SPA itself makes no runtime network calls; all outbound navigation is client-side routing to sibling applications at /realm-manager, /web-launcher, and /secure-file-transfer-portal. The multi-stage Dockerfile uses Turbo workspace pruning in a pruner stage, a Vite build stage, and an nginx:stable production image. No environment variables are consumed by the React application itself.
response.purpose: A React-based single-page application (SPA) that serves as the landing/gateway page for the Dull zero-trust network access platform. Built with Vite and Tailwind CSS, it displays the platform brand identity and provides navigation cards linking to the three main sub-applications: Realm Manager, Web Launcher, and Secure File Transfer Portal. In production it is served by an Nginx reverse proxy that handles TLS termination, HTTP-to-HTTPS redirection, and gzip compression inside a Docker container.

response.notes: Single runtime dependency is @phosphor-icons/react (^2.1.7). Peer dependency on react ^18.0.0. Uses @dull/config (workspace:*) for shared TypeScript, vitest, and prettier configuration — this corresponds to the catalog component dull/frontend/config but the reference appears only as an npm package name in package.json, not as an explicit catalog-name mention in README or code comments. Build scripts invoke rimraf, tsc, vitest, and prettier as dev tooling but these are not runtime shell-outs. No file I/O, network calls, or environment variable dependencies occur at runtime.
response.purpose: A thin React library package that re-exports a curated subset of 49 Phosphor icon components with stricter TypeScript typing enforced at the organisation boundary. It wraps each icon using a RestrictWeight generic type that limits the weight prop to 'regular', 'bold', and 'fill' only (excluding 'thin', 'light', and 'duotone'), and re-exports the IconBase and IconWeight types from @phosphor-icons/react. The package serves as a single, canonical import point for icon usage across consuming frontend packages in the monorepo.

network_endpoints: clipboard://navigator.clipboard.writeText (browser Clipboard API, used in CopyableValue)
response.notes: The DataGrid component manages its own state persistence via localStorage using a composite key (userId + tableName + locationPath); callers must supply these three identifiers. The grid state is saved on column/density/pinnedColumn changes and on window beforeunload. The jsonCodec utility requires zod as a peer dependency (^4.1.1). The package extends @dull/config (dull/frontend/config) for its TypeScript and Vitest configurations. No HTTP, WebSocket, or gRPC calls are made; the only browser API with external-world effect is navigator.clipboard. All 62 SVG icons are inline React components (no external image fetches). External logos for Splunk and Nozomi are included in the SVG assets.
response.purpose: @dull/generic is a React component library (published as a private workspace package) that provides reusable UI primitives for the Dull frontend monorepo. It exposes an icon registry backed by 62 inline SVG components, a Loading screen, a CopyableValue text widget, a CellWithPreview DataGrid cell renderer, a fully-featured DataGrid wrapper around MUI X DataGridPro (with localStorage-backed column/density/pin persistence keyed by userId+tableName+locationPath), a multi-select filter operator, strictly-typed column definition helpers, and a Zod-based JSON codec utility.

response.notes: All field components (TextFormField, PasswordFormField, OtpFormField, FileFormField) are thin wrappers that read field state from TanStack Form context via `useFieldContext` and pass it down to the corresponding `@dull/ui` primitive (TextField, PasswordField, OtpField, FileField). The SubmitButton reads `isSubmitting` from form store to auto-show a pending state. FormError reads `errorMap.onSubmit` to display form-level submission errors. The package has no filesystem I/O, no network calls, and spawns no external processes — it is a pure React component library. Consumed by at least four frontend apps: keycloak-theme, launcher-web, realm-manager, and secure-portal.
response.purpose: A React form library package (`@dull/form`) that wraps TanStack Form (`@tanstack/react-form`) with pre-built, accessible form field components tied to the project's UI design system (`@dull/ui`). It exposes a `useAppForm` hook (built via `createFormHook`) pre-configured with validation logic (validate-on-submit, revalidate-on-change after submission), an `onSubmitInvalid` handler that focuses the first invalid input, and a set of context-bound field components (`TextFormField`, `PasswordFormField`, `OtpFormField`, `FileFormField`) and form-level components (`SubmitButton`, `FormError`). The `Form` wrapper component delegates to `react-aria-components` with ARIA validation behaviour enabled. This package is consumed by the keycloak-theme, launcher-web, realm-manager, and secure-portal frontend applications.
