response.notes: The generate.mts script reads process.env at build/deploy time and writes features.json to the package root; that file is then served at a URL supplied to FeatureFlagProvider as the featuresUrl prop. At runtime the library fetches that URL, validates the JSON against Zod schemas (featuresSchema / featureSchema), then overlays any overrides found in localStorage under the key 'feature-flags'. FeatureId is a TypeScript string-union type derived from the runtime return of getFeatures(), so it is structurally widened to string in practice. @dull/config appears only in devDependencies (ESLint/Prettier config) and is not imported at runtime.
response.purpose: @dull/flags is a React library that provides feature flag management for Dull frontend applications. It fetches a feature-flag manifest from a configurable HTTP URL at runtime, merges in per-user overrides stored in browser localStorage, and exposes a React Context provider (FeatureFlagProvider), hooks (useFeatureFlag, useFeatureFlags), and a guard component (FeatureFlaggedRoute) for conditional rendering. A companion Node.js code-generation script (scripts/generate.mts) reads environment variables matching FLAG__<ID>__ENABLED=true|false and writes a features.json file that is intended to be served as the runtime manifest.

response.notes: The package self-consumes its own prettier export: `prettier.config.mjs` at the package root imports from `@dull/config/prettier`. The `library` tsconfig preset extends `react.json` which in turn extends `base.json`, forming a three-level inheritance chain. The prettier config references the `prettier-plugin-tailwindcss` plugin with `tailwindFunctions: ['tv']`, suggesting consumers use the `tailwind-variants` utility. The package is marked `private: true`, so it is internal to this monorepo only. `external_tools_spawned` reflects npm build scripts (`tsc`, `prettier --write`, `rimraf dist`) rather than runtime invocations.
response.purpose: A shared tooling-configuration library for the frontend monorepo, published as the private package `@dull/config`. It centralises ESLint flat-config presets (base JavaScript, TypeScript, and React flavours), a Prettier config (with tailwindcss plugin and `tv` function support), a base Vitest config, and three TypeScript `tsconfig` presets (base, react, library) so that all other frontend packages can extend a single consistent set of rules rather than each defining their own.

response.notes: The README explicitly warns that fake MFA records inserted by this tool may be picked up and forwarded to Firebase by the dull/microprocess C# service if it is running concurrently, potentially generating tens of thousands of spurious Firebase requests. The PostgreSQL connection defaults are host=localhost, dbname=dullmanager, user=postgres. Constant values for NIC details and IP context are hardcoded in src/constants.rs and the README directs users to edit that file for customisation. No config files are read from disk; all configuration is via CLI flags.
response.purpose: A standalone Rust CLI tool that generates an arbitrary number of fake session and MFA records directly into a dullmanager PostgreSQL database. It is intended for load testing. Given CLI arguments for database connection details, a row count, and a starting peer_id, it queries existing services, segments, and devices, then inserts synthetic session lifecycle records (segmentsessions, segmentsessionstarts, segmentsessionopens, segmentsessioncloses) and MFA approval records (connectionapprovalrequests, mfarequests, mfaresponses, connectionapprovalresponses) with randomised timestamps and values.

consumes_files: HKLM\Software\Dull\EVM (Windows Registry: Token and APILocation values)
response.notes: Configuration (API authentication token and API endpoint URL) is read from the Windows Registry at HKLM\Software\Dull\EVM via values 'Token' and 'APILocation'. The process supervision violation response (crashing the host via InitiateSystemShutdownExA) is fully commented out — a violation is logged but no destructive action is taken. The PID list to monitor is received over the named pipe \\.\ pipe\dull-evm-reply-server as an AgentProcessStatus JSON payload, likely written by another component (such as a session launcher) running in the same guest. The status pipe \\.\ pipe\dull-evm-status-server pushes AgentTunnelStatus JSON to all connected readers whenever status changes, making it a push-broadcast IPC channel. The binary is built exclusively for the x86_64-pc-windows-gnu target. The Buildkite pipeline includes S3 upload and KMS signing steps, indicating this artifact is distributed as part of the EVM guest image installation.
response.purpose: evm-process-monitor is a Windows NT service (registered as 'EVMProcessMonitor') that runs inside an EVM (Enterprise Virtual Machine) guest. On startup it waits for the Windows setup phase to complete (by tailing setupact.log), then registers the EVM with a backend launcher API by posting a TLS certificate, polls the API for network segment configuration (inbound access segments and outbound application/session-recording segments), connects those segments via connector-lib's SegmentHost, signals readiness back to the API, and then supervises a set of designated Windows processes for the duration of the user session — reporting a violation if any monitored process terminates unexpectedly. In parallel it exposes two Windows named pipes: one that broadcasts AgentTunnelStatus JSON to any connected reader, and one that accepts an AgentProcessStatus JSON payload (a list of PIDs to monitor) from another process. Session lifecycle events (start, open, error, close) emitted by the SegmentHost are forwarded to the launcher REST API.

response.notes: Windows-only binary cross-compiled to x86_64-pc-windows-gnu target inside a Linux Docker container. The named pipes dull-evm-status-server (read) and dull-evm-reply-server (write) strongly imply dull/evm-process-monitor as the counterpart server, but no catalog component name is explicitly stated in code comments, README (absent), or memory files. The companion binary evm_app_launcher.exe is hardcoded at C:\Program Files\Dull\EVM\evm_app_launcher.exe and is not a catalogued component. LaunchTarget contains plaintext credentials (username, password, host, port) serialised to launch_target.json. The trayicon.rgba asset is embedded at compile time via include_bytes!. The Buildkite CI pipeline cross-compiles, uploads to S3 (s3://dull-unsigned/evm/launcher-agent/), signs with AWS KMS, then uploads to s3://dull-signed/evm/launcher-agent/. The images_to_execute field is a 2-D Vec<Vec<String>> where each inner vec is [executable_path, arg1, arg2, ...]; these arbitrary executables are spawned with fully detached stdio.
response.purpose: evm-launcher-agent is a Windows-only system tray application (GUI subsystem binary) written in Rust that acts as an intermediary between the EVM process monitor and application launcher. It connects as a client to two Windows named pipes: one to receive AgentTunnelStatus messages (readiness state, tunnel counts, applications to spawn, and launch target credentials) and one to send back AgentProcessStatus messages containing PIDs of spawned processes. On receiving a ready signal it spawns zero or more pre-launch executables listed in the status message, then invokes a companion binary (evm_app_launcher.exe) with a JSON file containing LaunchTarget credentials, and reports the collected PIDs to the process monitor. A system tray icon with tooltip status updates provides lightweight user-visible feedback throughout the lifecycle.

response.notes: Docker is accessed via the Bollard Rust library over the Unix socket at /var/run/docker.sock — no Docker CLI binary is shelled out to. Container names follow the pattern evm-docker-<epoch_millis>. The seccomp profile (chrome-docker-seccomp.json) is embedded into the binary at compile time via include_str!. The component injects LAUNCHER_API_ENDPOINT, LAUNCHER_API_SECRET, LAUNCHER_USER_NAME, and LAUNCHER_USER_PASSWORD into every container it starts — strongly implying it serves as a bridge to dull/launcher-api, though that catalog name does not appear literally in the source or README. The config file default listen address (0.0.0.0:9988) differs from the CLI default (0.0.0.0:9998); the systemd unit uses the config file path. Authentication requires a bearer secret of at least 16 characters, sourced from TOML config or the BEARER_SECRET environment variable. The service exits with code 255 if run as root without a configured user to drop to.
response.purpose: EVM Docker Controller is a thin HTTPS REST API facade over the Docker socket that allows Dull microprocesses to start and manage 'EVM' containers on a Docker host. It exposes four endpoints — start, stop, destroy, and status-query — for container lifecycle management, authenticates all requests via a Bearer token, and injects launcher API credentials (URL, token, username, password) as environment variables into every container it creates. It runs as an unprivileged user, drops privileges after reading TLS material, and applies an embedded Chrome seccomp profile to all spawned containers. It is distributed as a Debian package with a systemd unit and a dedicated system user in the docker group.

data_formats: bincode (standard configuration, binary encoding for all events)
network_endpoints: redis://<connection_string> (Redis pub/sub, address supplied at runtime by caller)
response.notes: Two Queue backend implementations are provided: RedisQueue (wraps the redis crate's async pub/sub, used for real deployments) and ChannelQueue (wraps tokio broadcast channels with a capacity of 64, used for in-process testing). The PubSub wrapper applies a configurable key_prefix to every topic name before forwarding to the backend. All events must implement bincode Encode+Decode and the EventWithCorrelationId trait (which exposes an optional correlation ID string used for tracing spans). Retry backoff strategy is fully generic — each RetryableTask implementation supplies its own BackoffBuilder type via an associated type. The README explicitly names mfa-proxy-api as a reference usage example.
response.purpose: dull-event-queue is a Rust library crate providing an asynchronous event queue with pub/sub messaging and configurable retry policies. It abstracts over two pub/sub backends — Redis (for production/distributed use) and in-process tokio broadcast channels (for testing) — and exposes an EventQueue that spawns background jobs which subscribe to named topics, deserialize incoming events using bincode, and invoke user-defined RetryableTask handlers with automatic retry/backoff logic via the backon crate. Tasks receive three lifecycle callbacks: on_invoke, on_complete, and on_error.

response.notes: The var_templates directory contains 13 .env template files (db, feature_flags, garage, keycloak, launcher_api, launcher_web, mfa_api, microprocess, realm_api, secure_file_scanner, secure_file_transfer, secure_portal, web) — one per dull service. These templates embed 1Password secret references in `op://Development/vars/{service}/{VAR_NAME}` format and are compiled directly into the binary via the `include_dir!` macro, so the binary is fully self-contained at runtime. `op inject` is spawned in parallel (via rayon) for each template file, with the template content piped to stdin and the injected output written to the target .env file. The compiled binary (`./dev_setup`) is committed to the repository root for others to use without needing Rust installed locally. The `keycloak`, `reqwest`, `clap`, `dotenvy`, and `open` crates are declared as Cargo dependencies but are not actively used in main.rs, suggesting planned but not yet implemented features (e.g. Keycloak realm setup, HTTP calls, URL opening). Network communication with 1Password is entirely delegated to the `op` subprocess — the component itself opens no sockets.
response.purpose: dev_setup is a standalone Rust CLI tool that bootstraps and updates a local development environment for the dull monorepo. It checks that the 1Password CLI (`op`) is installed and authenticated (offering to install it via Homebrew if missing), prompts the developer for a local dev directory (defaulting to `~/dev`), then generates populated `.env` files for every dull microservice by piping embedded template files through `op inject` in parallel. The resulting files are written to `{dev_dir}/dull/envs/`, alongside empty `*.overrides.env` files for local overrides.

response.notes: This is an upstream RustCrypto crate vendored into the monorepo. It is a no_std library (with alloc). Its Cargo.toml pins exact pre-release versions of sibling crates (der =0.8.0-pre.0, cms =0.3.0-pre, cmpv2 =0.3.0-pre, x509-cert =0.3.0-pre), indicating tight coupling with other vendored format crates. The test suite uses openssl CLI commands to generate test fixtures (commented in source), but openssl is not spawned at runtime — only used out-of-band to produce the hardcoded hex test vectors.
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of the Timestamping Protocol (TSP) as described in RFC 3161. It provides ASN.1/DER-encoded data structures for creating and parsing timestamp requests (TimeStampReq) and responses (TimeStampResp/TstInfo), operating as a no_std library. It depends on the der, cms, cmpv2, and x509-cert crates for lower-level ASN.1 encoding, CMS content handling, PKI status, and X.509 certificate types respectively.

response.notes: The crate depends on sibling crates within the same formats workspace: der (ASN.1 DER encoding), spki (SubjectPublicKeyInfo/AlgorithmIdentifier), const-oid (OID database including RFC 6960 OIDs), and x509-cert (Certificate, CRL, and extension types). The builder feature additionally depends on digest, rand_core, and signature crates. Test fixtures are DER-encoded binary files under tests/examples/ covering various OCSP request/response scenarios including signed requests, multiple extensions, error responses, and certificates with different hash algorithms (SHA-1, SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512). The crate is no_std by default with an optional std feature.
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of the X.509 Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) data formats as specified in RFC 6960. The crate provides ASN.1 DER encoding/decoding of all OCSP message structures — requests (OcspRequest, TbsRequest, Request, Signature), responses (OcspResponse, BasicOcspResponse, ResponseData, SingleResponse), certificate status (CertStatus, RevokedInfo), certificate identifiers (CertId), responder identifiers (ResponderId), and OCSP extensions (Nonce, CrlId, AcceptableResponses, ArchiveCutoff, ServiceLocator, PreferredSignatureAlgorithms). An optional 'builder' feature adds OcspRequestBuilder and OcspResponseBuilder for constructing signed OCSP messages. The crate is no_std compatible and is part of the RustCrypto formats ecosystem.

response.notes: The component writes temporary PEM certificate files and a TOML zlint config file to OS temp directories (via the `tempfile` crate) during test execution, but these are ephemeral and cleaned up automatically — they are not persistent outputs. The zlint config is hardcoded as an inline string constant (`ZLINT_CONFIG`) covering Apple, CABF, Mozilla, RFC5280, RFC5480, RFC5891, and Community policy sets. The `openssl` module invokes `openssl x509` for certificates and `openssl req -verify` for CSRs. The `zlint` module parses JSON output from `zlint -pretty -config`. Both modules assert success/failure, making them suitable only for test contexts. No README was present in the directory.
response.purpose: A Rust library crate (x509-cert-test-support) that provides test utilities for running X.509 certificates and certificate signing requests through external certificate validation tools. It exposes two modules: `openssl` for verifying PEM-encoded certificates and CSRs via the `openssl` CLI, and `zlint` for running certificates through the `zlint` linter and parsing its JSON output into structured lint-result types (`LintResult`, `LintStatus`, `Status`). It is intended to be used as a dev-dependency in tests of the x509-cert crate and related format crates.

response.notes: The Cargo.toml uses `[patch.crates-io]` to redirect `der`, `der_derive`, `pem-rfc7468`, and `spki` to their local workspace paths, ensuring the fuzz targets exercise the in-tree implementations. The crate intentionally declares its own `[workspace]` to isolate itself from the parent workspace, which is a standard pattern for cargo-fuzz sub-crates. The seed corpus file `inputs/rsa2048-csr.der` is a real RSA-2048 CSR in DER format used to seed the fuzzer with a valid starting input. The `corpus/` and `artifacts/` directories are gitignored and produced at runtime by the fuzzer engine.
response.purpose: A libFuzzer-based fuzz testing harness for the x509-cert crate's certificate request parsing code. It provides two fuzz targets — `certreq` and `certreqinfo` — that feed arbitrary byte sequences to the DER deserialization logic for `CertReq` and `CertReqInfo` types respectively. A seed input (`rsa2048-csr.der`) is included to bootstrap the fuzzer corpus. The crate patches its local workspace dependencies (der, pem-rfc7468, spki) so that fuzzing exercises the in-tree versions of those libraries rather than crates.io releases.

response.notes: Depends on the parent `tls_codec` crate via a relative path (`..`) with the `arbitrary` feature enabled, which is required for libFuzzer to generate structured `VLBytes` inputs. The `.gitignore` excludes `target`, `corpus`, `artifacts`, and `coverage` — all runtime outputs of `cargo fuzz`. The package declares `[workspace] members = ["."]` to prevent the fuzz crate from being absorbed into the parent workspace. No README is present; purpose is entirely evident from the fuzz target source and Cargo.toml metadata.
response.purpose: A cargo-fuzz harness for the tls_codec crate. It defines a single fuzz target (`inverse`) that exercises the round-trip property of `VLBytes`: it serializes an arbitrary `VLBytes` value, deserializes it back, and asserts that the result equals the original, that all bytes were consumed, and that the predicted serialized length matches the actual serialized length. The package is isolated in its own workspace so it does not interfere with the parent workspace, and is intended to be driven by libFuzzer via `cargo fuzz`.

response.notes: This is a pure compile-time proc-macro crate (lib.proc-macro = true). It has no runtime file I/O, network activity, or subprocess spawning. Its only runtime dependency relationship is with the parent tls_codec crate (at path '../'), which it references as a dev-dependency for integration tests and in generated code token streams (e.g. 'tls_codec::Serialize'). The 'conditional_deserialization' feature requires 'syn/full' and enables the conditionally_deserializable attribute macro, which rewrites struct definitions to introduce a const boolean generic (IS_DESERIALIZABLE) and emits two type aliases with 'Deserializable' and 'Undeserializable' prefixes. The trybuild dev-dependency is used for compile-fail tests in tests/attributes/.
response.purpose: tls_codec_derive is a Rust procedural macro crate that provides derive macros for automatically implementing the TLS encoding and decoding traits defined in the tls_codec crate. It exposes five derive macros (TlsSize, TlsSerialize, TlsDeserialize, TlsDeserializeBytes, TlsSerializeBytes) and one attribute macro (conditionally_deserializable) that generate trait implementations for Rust structs and enums compliant with the TLS RFC 8446 wire format. The generated code handles struct fields, enum variants with discriminants, skipped fields, custom serialization prefix delegation via the 'with' attribute, and an optional conditional deserialization pattern that uses a const boolean generic to produce 'Deserializable' and 'Undeserializable' type aliases.

response.notes: The crate is a vendored copy of the upstream RustCrypto `tai64` crate (v5.0.0-pre). It has no filesystem I/O, no network I/O, and shells out to nothing. Its public API surface is purely in-process: the two timestamp structs plus an error enum. Optional features are `std` (enabled by default), `serde`, and `zeroize`. It is categorised under `network-programming` upstream because TAI64 timestamps appear in network protocols (e.g. NTS, DNSSEC), but the crate itself performs no networking.
response.purpose: A Rust library implementing the TAI64 and TAI64N (Temps Atomique International) timestamp formats, as specified at cr.yp.to/libtai/tai64.html. It provides two public structs — `Tai64` (8-byte second-precision) and `Tai64N` (12-byte nanosecond-precision) — with serialization to/from big-endian byte arrays, conversion to/from Unix timestamps and `std::time::SystemTime`, arithmetic over `Duration`, and optional `serde` and `zeroize` support. The crate is `no_std`-compatible with an optional `std` feature gate.

response.notes: Depends on dull/connector-lib/formats/der for its core ASN.1 DER codec primitives (Choice, Decode, Encode, Sequence, Document, etc.) and optionally on dull/connector-lib/formats/base64ct for fingerprint Base64 encoding. These are Cargo dependencies rather than explicit textual catalog-name references in README or comments, so they are omitted from explicit_cross_component_mentions per the rules. The DecodePublicKey and EncodePublicKey traits provide filesystem I/O methods (read_public_key_der_file, read_public_key_pem_file, write_public_key_der_file, write_public_key_pem_file) gated on the std feature, explaining the file-path entries. Test fixtures (ed25519, p256, rsa2048) in tests/examples/ are the concrete file artefacts consumed during testing. The PEM label constant is 'PUBLIC KEY'.
response.purpose: A no-std Rust library implementing X.509 Subject Public Key Info (SPKI) as defined in RFC 5280 § 4.1. It provides encoding and decoding of public keys and their associated AlgorithmIdentifiers (OIDs) in ASN.1 DER and PEM (RFC 7468) formats. The library exposes core types SubjectPublicKeyInfo and AlgorithmIdentifier, along with traits (DecodePublicKey, EncodePublicKey, AssociatedAlgorithmIdentifier, SignatureAlgorithmIdentifier, SignatureBitStringEncoding) that downstream cryptographic key types implement to gain SPKI serialisation capabilities. An optional fingerprint feature computes SHA-256 fingerprints of encoded SPKI documents per RFC 7469 § 2.1.1.

response.notes: The component depends on base16ct (catalogued as dull/connector-lib/formats/base16ct) for constant-time hex encoding/decoding. It is a #![no_std] crate with optional `alloc` and `zeroize` features. The `zeroize` feature enables memory-zeroing of secret data held in the HexOrBin newtypes. Tested against bincode v1, ciborium v0.2 (CBOR), rmp-serde v1 (MessagePack), serde-json-core v0.5, serde_json v1, and toml v0.8. The distinction between `array` and `slice` modules is that array deserialization enforces exact buffer length while slice deserialization allows the buffer to be larger than the data.
response.purpose: serdect is a Rust library providing constant-time serde serializer/deserializer helpers for data that potentially contains secrets such as cryptographic keys. It addresses timing side-channel vulnerabilities in serialization by conditionally encoding data as constant-time hexadecimal (via the base16ct crate) when using human-readable formats like JSON or TOML, and as raw binary when using binary formats like bincode or MessagePack. The library exposes two primary modules: `array` for fixed-size byte arrays and `slice` for variable-length byte slices, each offering serialize/deserialize free functions as well as newtype wrapper types (`HexOrBin`, `HexLowerOrBin`, `HexUpperOrBin`) that implement serde's `Serialize`/`Deserialize` traits.

response.notes: The crate is no_std-compatible with optional alloc and std feature gates. Optional features include: 'der' (ASN.1 DER encoding via the der crate), 'pem' (PEM encoding), 'point' (EncodedPoint via base16ct and hybrid-array), 'serde' (via serdect), 'pkcs8' (PKCS#8 interoperability), 'zeroize' (secure memory zeroing), and 'subtle' (constant-time conditional selection). The PKCS#8 integration in traits.rs allows round-tripping between SEC1 ECPrivateKey format and PKCS#8 PrivateKeyInfo format using the id-ecPublicKey OID (1.2.840.10045.2.1). File I/O methods (read_sec1_der_file, read_sec1_pem_file, write_sec1_der_file, write_sec1_pem_file) are gated behind the 'std' feature. Supports EC modulus sizes: 192-bit (U24), 224-bit (U28), 256-bit (U32), 384-bit (U48), and 521-bit (U66).
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of SEC1: Elliptic Curve Cryptography encoding formats. The crate provides two main capabilities: (1) encoding and decoding of ASN.1 DER-serialized elliptic curve private keys in the ECPrivateKey structure defined in SEC1 Appendix C.4 and RFC5915, with optional PEM encoding; and (2) the Elliptic-Curve-Point-to-Octet-String and Octet-String-to-Elliptic-Curve-Point algorithms for encoding EC points in compressed, uncompressed, compact, or identity form. It is a no_std-compatible library crate with optional alloc/std feature gates, and bridges between SEC1 and PKCS#8 key formats via optional pkcs8 integration.

response.notes: Depends on `der`, `spki`, and optionally `pkcs5` crates which correspond to catalog components dull/connector-lib/formats/der, dull/connector-lib/formats/spki, and dull/connector-lib/formats/pkcs5 respectively — but those crate names do not appear verbatim as catalog IDs in the source. The `DecodePrivateKey` trait's `read_pkcs8_der_file` / `read_pkcs8_pem_file` methods and `EncodePrivateKey`'s `write_pkcs8_der_file` / `write_pkcs8_pem_file` methods perform filesystem I/O on caller-supplied paths (std feature only). Test fixtures are DER/PEM files covering Ed25519, P-256, RSA-2048, X25519, and BIGN-256 key types, including encrypted variants using AES-128/256-CBC with PBKDF2-SHA1/SHA256 and scrypt. The crate is `no_std` by default; heap allocation, PEM, encryption, and file I/O are each behind separate feature flags (alloc, pem, encryption, std).
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of Public-Key Cryptography Standards (PKCS) #8 (RFC 5208 and RFC 5958) for encoding and decoding cryptographic private key information. The library provides parsing and serialization of `PrivateKeyInfo` (unencrypted, PKCS#8 v1/v2) and `EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo` (password-encrypted) structures in both ASN.1 DER binary format and RFC 7468 PEM text format. It exposes `DecodePrivateKey` and `EncodePrivateKey` traits that downstream algorithm crates implement to gain PKCS#8 I/O support. Encryption support (behind the `encryption` feature) uses PKCS#5 PBES2 with scrypt or PBKDF2 key derivation and AES-CBC symmetric encryption. The crate is `no_std`-compatible with reduced functionality on heapless targets.

response.notes: The crate is #![no_std] with optional alloc/pbes2 features. 'openssl' and 'dd' appear only in tests/examples/re-gen.sh, a fixture-generation script not part of library runtime. The library explicitly depends on the local 'der' and 'spki' workspace crates (both pinned at pre-release versions matching connector-lib/formats/der and connector-lib/formats/spki respectively) but these catalog names do not appear literally in source text. scrypt parameters are deliberately chosen for OpenSSL v3 compatibility (log_n=14, r=8, p=1) as noted in a code comment referencing RustCrypto/formats#1205. PBES1 decryption/encryption is structurally unsupported (returns NoPbes1CryptSupport error); PBES1 support is parse-only.
response.purpose: A pure Rust, no_std-compatible library implementing PKCS#5 Password-Based Cryptography Specification Version 2.1 (RFC 8018). It provides ASN.1 DER encoding and decoding for both PBES1 (Password-Based Encryption Scheme 1, legacy/read-only) and PBES2 (Password-Based Encryption Scheme 2, full encrypt/decrypt) algorithm identifiers and parameters. When the optional 'pbes2' feature is enabled, it performs actual symmetric encryption/decryption using AES-128/192/256-CBC ciphers with keys derived from PBKDF2 (SHA-1/224/256/384/512 variants) or scrypt key derivation functions. The main public API surface is the EncryptionScheme enum and pbes2::Parameters struct, both implementing der Decode/Encode traits.

response.notes: Runtime Cargo dependencies include `cms`, `der`, `spki`, `x509-cert`, and `const-oid` — all sibling components in the formats workspace — but none are referenced by their full catalog names in README or code comments. Dev-dependencies include `pkcs8` and `pkcs5`; `safe_bag.rs` has a comment 'PrivateKeyInfo is defined in the pkcs8 crate' and `kdf.rs` references the `pkcs5` crate for PKCS#5 alternatives, but again not by exact catalog names. The `tests/examples/gen.sh` script generates the test fixture PFX/PEM/DER files (likely via openssl), but the library itself spawns no external processes at runtime. The `kdf` feature is gated behind a Cargo feature flag and brings in the `digest` crate. The library is `no_std` compatible (uses `extern crate alloc`). TODO comments in `lib.rs` note that decryption support, RC2, and a builder API are not yet implemented.
response.purpose: Pure Rust library implementing PKCS#12 Personal Information Exchange Syntax v1.1 (RFC 7292). It provides DER encoding and decoding of PFX/P12 container files, which bundle private keys, X.509 certificates, and CRLs with optional password-based encryption (PBE/PBES2) and MAC-based integrity protection. It also includes an optional `kdf` feature implementing the PKCS#12 key-derivation function described in RFC 7292 Appendix B, along with supporting ASN.1 types for safe-bags, authenticated-safe, digest-info, and PBE/PBKDF2 parameter structures.

response.notes: The README explicitly references the base64ct crate (dull/connector-lib/formats/base64ct) as its Base64 dependency, specifically calling out constant-time decoding to mitigate the Util::Lookup timing attack against RSA private keys in SGX enclaves (arxiv 2108.04600). The encoder supports a non-standard line wrap width of 70, documented as intended specifically for the OpenSSH private key format. The PemLabel trait provides a zero-cost marker interface so downstream types can self-describe their expected PEM label string (e.g. 'PRIVATE KEY', 'CERTIFICATE'). All format crates that wrap DER-encoded structures (pkcs1, pkcs8, sec1, spki, x509-cert, etc.) are natural consumers of this library for their PEM I/O layers.
response.purpose: Pure Rust library implementing PEM encoding and decoding according to RFC 7468 ('Strict' grammar), targeting PKIX, PKCS, and CMS structures. It parses and emits the familiar '-----BEGIN ...-----' / '-----END ...-----' encapsulation format used for cryptographic keys, certificates, and messages. The implementation is no_std-friendly, performs no heap allocations in its core path, and deliberately avoids branching on potentially secret Base64-encoded data to prevent timing side-channel leakage. Optional alloc/std features add buffered helpers and Vec/String return types. The sole dependency is the base64ct crate for constant-time Base64 codec operations.

response.notes: Depends on workspace-local crates: der (for ASN.1 DER encoding/decoding), spki (for ObjectIdentifier/MechType), and x509-cert (as a dev-dependency with pem feature for tests). Has a 'rfc2478' feature flag that switches between the older RFC 2478 NegTokenInit/NegTokenTarg types and the newer RFC 4178 NegTokenInit2/NegTokenResp types. The library is no_std with alloc. It implements MS-SPNG (Microsoft's SPNEGO extension) via the NegTokenInit2 and NegHints types. The spki catalog component is not listed individually in the catalog but dull/connector-lib/formats/spki corresponds to the spki dependency used here.
response.purpose: A pure Rust no_std library implementing the Generic Security Service Application Program Interface (GSS-API) as specified in RFC 1508/1509, RFC 2478, RFC 4178, and MS-SPNG. It provides DER encoding/decoding of GSS-API token structures used during security context establishment, including InitialContextToken, SubsequentContextToken, PerMsgToken, SealedMessage, and SPNEGO negotiation types (NegotiationToken, NegTokenInit, NegTokenInit2, NegTokenResp, NegTokenTarg, NegHints, NegResult, NegState). The library enables applications to exchange GSS-API opaque tokens for authentication protocol negotiation (e.g., Kerberos, NTLM via SPNEGO) without depending on platform GSS-API libraries.

response.notes: This is a Rust proc-macro crate (Cargo.toml declares `[lib] proc-macro = true`) operating entirely at compile time: it parses Rust token streams from annotated types and emits new token streams implementing ASN.1 encode/decode logic. No filesystem I/O, network access, or subprocess spawning occurs at runtime. The README.md is embedded into the compiled library docs via `include_str!("../README.md")` in lib.rs (line 1), which is the only file read. The `#[asn1(...)]` attribute namespace is registered with each derive macro and is parsed by the `attributes.rs` module to control tagging modes, optionality, default values, context-specific tag numbers, and ASN.1 type coercions. Dependencies are limited to `proc-macro2`, `quote`, and `syn` — no `der` runtime dependency, meaning the generated code references `::der::*` paths but the crate itself does not link against `der` at macro-expansion time.
response.purpose: A Rust procedural macro crate (`der_derive`) that provides custom derive support for the `der` crate's ASN.1 encoding and decoding traits. It exposes four proc-macro derives: `Choice` (maps ASN.1 CHOICE to a Rust enum, generating `Decode`/`Encode`/`Choice` impls), `Enumerated` (maps ASN.1 ENUMERATED to a C-like Rust enum with `TryFrom` impl), `Sequence` (maps ASN.1 SEQUENCE to a Rust struct with full decode/encode support), and `ValueOrd` (derives lexicographic DER ordering for use with ASN.1 SET OF). The crate is not intended to be used directly; instead it is re-exported through the `derive` feature of the parent `der` crate. It is designed for embedded/no-std environments where code size is critical, deliberately using trait-object-based encoders to minimise generated code compared to serde-derived approaches.

response.notes: The crate depends on cms (for EnvelopedData used in EncryptedKey and POPOPrivKey), der (for ASN.1 encoding/decoding primitives and derive macros), spki (for AlgorithmIdentifierOwned and SubjectPublicKeyInfoOwned), and x509-cert (for certificate attribute, extension, and name types). The dev-dependency on const-oid is used in tests. Binary test fixtures (*.bin) in tests/examples/ are consumed by the test suite for round-trip DER decode/encode verification. The library is `#![no_std]` with optional `alloc` and `std` features, and an optional `pem` feature for PEM encoding support via the der crate.
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of the Certificate Request Message Format (CRMF) as described in RFC 4211. It provides ASN.1/DER-encoded data structures for certificate request messages, including CertReqMessages, CertReqMsg, CertRequest, CertTemplate, ProofOfPossession, and registration controls/info types. The library is `no_std`-compatible and is used to encode and decode CRMF certificate enrollment requests exchanged between PKI clients and Certificate Authorities (CAs) or Registration Authorities (RAs).

response.notes: All input data files are embedded at compile time via `include_str!` macros, so the tool reads no files at runtime — the file consumption is a compile-time artifact. The README command `cargo run --manifest-path=oiddbgen/Cargo.toml | rustfmt > src/db/gen.rs` (run from the const-oid directory) makes explicit that this tool's sole output is the committed source file `src/db/gen.rs` consumed by the const-oid crate. `rustfmt` is invoked externally by the user in the shell pipeline, not spawned by the binary itself. The generated file carries the header comment `!! DO NOT EDIT !!: This file is auto-generated by oiddbgen.`
response.purpose: oiddbgen is a standalone Rust CLI tool that generates a Rust OID (Object Identifier) database source file for the parent const-oid crate. It parses IANA LDAP parameters from a bundled CSV file, ASN.1 OBJECT IDENTIFIER definitions from bundled IETF RFC text files and custom Markdown/ASN.1 spec files, resolves full OID arc chains, and emits a Rust source module to stdout. The generated module populates a static `Database` constant with all known OID name-to-value mappings. Per the README, the output is intended to be piped through `rustfmt` and written to `src/db/gen.rs` inside the parent `const-oid` crate directory.

response.notes: This crate is a `no_std` compatible library (with optional `alloc`/`std` features). Its Cargo.toml dependencies — `der`, `spki`, `x509-cert`, and `const-oid` — correspond to sibling catalog components (`dull/connector-lib/formats/der`, `dull/connector-lib/formats/spki`, `dull/connector-lib/formats/x509-cert`, `dull/connector-lib/formats/const-oid`) and dev-dependencies include `pem-rfc7468` and `pkcs5` (also catalog siblings), but none of these are named using their catalog identifiers in the README or code comments, so they are not listed under explicit_cross_component_mentions. The test suite exercises real-world samples including Apple MDM signatures, Microsoft authroot.stl certificate bundles, SCEP protocol data, and PKITS (NIST PKI Test Suite) certificate chains. The optional `builder` feature gates high-level construction APIs that depend on AES, CBC mode, RSA, SHA digest crates, and the `x509-cert` builder sub-feature.
response.purpose: Pure Rust library implementing the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) as specified in RFC 5652, RFC 5911, and RFC 3274. It provides ASN.1/DER encoding and decoding of CMS data structures — ContentInfo, SignedData, EnvelopedData, EncryptedData, AuthenticatedData, AuthEnvelopedData, DigestedData, CompressedData, and TimestampedData — used in cryptographic standards such as S/MIME, PKCS#12, and the RFC 3161 digital timestamping protocol. An optional `builder` feature enables runtime construction of signed and enveloped CMS messages with AES-CBC encryption and RSA key transport.

response.notes: This crate is part of the RustCrypto formats workspace and depends on four sibling crates in the same workspace: crmf (RFC 4211 CRMF types, providing CertReqMessages, CertId, CertTemplate, EncryptedValue, PkiPublicationInfo), der (DER encoding/decoding with derive macros), spki (AlgorithmIdentifierOwned, SubjectPublicKeyInfo), and x509-cert (Certificate, CertReq, CertificateList, Extensions, GeneralName). In the catalog these correspond to dull/connector-lib/formats/crmf, dull/connector-lib/formats/der, dull/connector-lib/formats/spki, and dull/connector-lib/formats/x509-cert respectively — they are used as code-level imports, not mentioned by catalog path. The Nested message body variant (PkiBody::Nested mapping to NestedMessageContent) is currently commented out with a TODO note due to a recursion error in the type system. Test binary fixtures under tests/examples/ are pre-encoded CMP messages used to validate round-trip DER encode/decode correctness.
response.purpose: Pure Rust library implementing the Certificate Management Protocol (CMP) as defined in RFC 4210. It provides ASN.1/DER-encoded message types covering the full CMP message lifecycle: initialization requests and responses, certification requests, key update requests, key recovery, revocation requests and responses, cross-certification, CA key update announcements, certificate and CRL announcements, general-purpose messages, polling, proof-of-possession challenges, and error messages. The crate is a no-std library in the RustCrypto ecosystem and is published to crates.io as 'cmpv2'.

response.notes: The README explicitly names the `pem-rfc7468` crate as a primary consumer, noting this library exists to safely encode/decode PEM-format cryptographic keys. The constant-time design is motivated by the Util::Lookup paper (arxiv 2108.04600) demonstrating RSA key extraction from SGX enclaves via non-constant-time Base64 timing leakage. Dev dependencies include the `base64` crate (for cross-validation in tests) and `proptest` for property-based testing. No filesystem I/O, network, or process spawning occurs — this is a pure encoding/decoding library crate.
response.purpose: Pure Rust implementation of Base64 (RFC 4648) providing constant-time encoding and decoding across multiple alphabets (Standard, URL-safe, bcrypt, crypt(3), and SHA-crypt). The library avoids data-dependent branches and lookup tables to provide portable side-channel-resistant operation, targeting use cases such as PEM format encoding of cryptographic private keys. It supports `no_std` environments with no heap allocations in the core API, plus optional `alloc`/`std` feature flags for convenience methods.

response.notes: This is a vendored copy of the RustCrypto base32ct crate (upstream: https://github.com/RustCrypto/formats). It has no runtime file I/O, no network access, and spawns no subprocesses. Its only dev-dependencies are the upstream `base32` crate (used as a reference implementation in tests) and `proptest` for property-based testing. The proptest regression file at tests/proptests.proptest-regressions is a test artifact consumed only by the test suite. The crate exposes a stable Rust API surface: the `Encoding` trait plus the four concrete alphabet structs.
response.purpose: base32ct is a pure Rust, no_std-compatible library that implements constant-time Base32 encoding and decoding as specified by RFC 4648. It provides four alphabet variants (lower-case padded Base32, lower-case unpadded Base32Unpadded, upper-case padded Base32Upper, and upper-case unpadded Base32UpperUnpadded) through a single Encoding trait. The implementation deliberately avoids data-dependent branches and lookup tables to provide portable best-effort constant-time operation with respect to data values (not message length), making it suitable for use in cryptographic contexts where timing side-channels are a concern. It supports both stack-only no_std environments and optional heap-allocating alloc/std convenience APIs.

response.notes: No dependencies beyond the Rust standard library (and optional alloc/std features). The crate is `#![no_std]` by default. Exported public API surface: `base16ct::lower::{encode, decode, encode_str, encode_string}`, `base16ct::upper::{encode, decode, encode_str, encode_string}`, `base16ct::mixed::decode`, `base16ct::{decoded_len, encoded_len, HexDisplay, Error, Result}`. The constant-time guarantee applies to data values but not message length. Originates from RustCrypto/formats repository.
response.purpose: A pure Rust `no_std` library implementing Base16 (hexadecimal) encoding and decoding as specified in RFC 4648. It provides constant-time encode/decode operations that avoid data-dependent branches or lookup tables, making it suitable for cryptographic contexts where timing side-channels are a concern. The library exposes three submodules: `lower` (lowercase hex encode/decode), `upper` (uppercase hex encode/decode), and `mixed` (decodes either case). Optional `alloc`/`std` features enable heap-allocating convenience APIs. This is a vendored copy of the upstream RustCrypto `base16ct` crate (v0.2.0).

response.notes: The library uses dart:ffi to load the native connector-lib binary at a caller-supplied path. C bindings are auto-generated by ffigen from connector-lib's exported C header (make_ffi_gen_config.yaml references ../connector-lib/target/connector.h); the generated file is lib/src/connector_bindings.gen.dart. Segment configuration is passed as JSON strings to the Rust layer; the JSON schema includes Name, SegmentID, ServiceID, MicrotunnelID, FuseType (Manual|Automatic), Factory ({TCP-Server|TCP-Client|Maintain-One: address}), and Sequences fields. Session events arrive as a C tagged-union (SessionEventWrapper, tag values 0-3 for Starts/Opens/Closes/Errors). Memory returned from get_logs() and get_sessions() must be explicitly freed via drop_logs()/drop_sessions() — the Dart wrapper handles this automatically. The Buildkite CI pipeline (.buildkite/pipeline.yml) declares a dependency on the connector-lib pipeline and runs on a 'flutter-build' queue agent. The README instructs consumers to add the package via git dependency pointing to the dull-networking/connector-lib-dart GitHub repo. TCP addresses shown in network_endpoints come from the bundled example (connector_example.dart) and are illustrative; actual addresses are runtime-configurable via JSON segment config.
response.purpose: connector-lib-dart is a Dart FFI (Foreign Function Interface) wrapper library around the dull/connector-lib Rust native library. It dynamically loads the compiled Rust shared library at runtime (platform-specific: libconnector.so on Linux, libconnector.dylib on macOS, connector.dll on Windows), exposes a high-level Dart API for managing network 'Segments' (configurable protocol handler intermediaries supporting TCP server/client and other factory types), and provides reactive streams (via rxdart BehaviorSubject) for consuming log events (polled every 2 seconds) and session lifecycle events (polled every 5 seconds). It also supports generating local TLS certificates via the Rust backend.

response.notes: The container has no pre-populated mounts/dull directory in the repository (unlike the mqtt-pub, mqtt-server, mqtt-sub, etc. siblings which have SQLite databases checked in). Instead the top-level net.sh provisioning script copies the dull binary and a per-container connector.db3 link file into client/mounts/dull at setup time. The README confirms no network ports are exposed to the host — all access to peer service containers (http, https, telnet, ssh, rtu, rdp, vnc, mqtt-*) is expected to flow through the Dull realm network managed by the embedded agent.
response.purpose: A Docker container image that runs an embedded `dull` agent alongside CLI networking tools (telnet, lsof, curl, wget) to act as a network client within the docker-network-services test environment. It is explicitly designed as the client-side peer in a collection of containers that demonstrate TCP services accessible only via the Dull realm — no ports are exposed to the Docker host. On startup the entry-point initialises the dull agent (running `dull init` if `microdata.db3` is absent) and then daemonises it, leaving a long-lived container that operators can `docker exec` into for manual connectivity tests.

The explicit_cross_component_mentions field lists dull/mfa-api because the app label is 'Dull MFA' and the package namespace is net.dull.mfa_app, suggesting it is the Android client for the MFA API service; however, no direct code-level import or URL reference to dull/mfa-api was found within this path — the connection is inferred from naming only, so this mention should be treated with low confidence. NDK version 27.0.12077973 is pinned (flutter.ndkVersion is commented out), suggesting native code compilation requirements. minSdkVersion is explicitly set to 21 (Android 5.0) rather than the Flutter default.
response.purpose: This is the Android application module for the Dull MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) Flutter app. It provides the Android-specific host shell for a Flutter-based MFA application, with application ID net.dull.mfa_app. It integrates Firebase (via google-services and Firebase Crashlytics plugins), supports biometric authentication (USE_BIOMETRIC permission), and is configured for release signing via a keystore. The MainActivity is a minimal FlutterFragmentActivity delegate that hands off all UI logic to the Flutter engine.

response.notes: Despite its catalog path under agent/rust-client, this component contains no Rust code — it is purely SQL and Docker infrastructure. The Docker image is based on the official postgres image and copies src/??_*.sql scripts into the PostgreSQL Docker entrypoint directory for automatic execution at container startup. Change notification is implemented via PostgreSQL NOTIFY/LISTEN: triggers on every major table fire notify_change_insert(), notify_change_update(), or notify_change_delete() functions that publish to channels named {TableName}_change, {TableName}_insert, {TableName}_update, and {TableName}_delete. Row-level security is enforced through named PostgreSQL roles (peer_realm, connector_agent) and security-barrier views grouped into schemas: connector, realm_exchange, ManagerUI, mfa_api, system_integrity. Session-context functions GetCurrentDeviceId() and GetCurrentRealmID() derive the acting device/realm from the PostgreSQL session username (format d{id} and r{id} respectively). The Microtunnels table stores external network metadata (Meet_IP, Meet_Port, Meet_Topic, Encryption_Key, db_user_name, db_user_password) for inter-realm tunnel configuration, but the component itself does not establish those connections. The docker-compose.yml configures POSTGRES_USER, POSTGRES_PASSWORD, and POSTGRES_DB via environment variables.
response.purpose: The realm-manager component is a PostgreSQL database schema component implementing a multi-tenant, federated realm management system. It provides the database layer for managing realms, devices, services, organisations, agreements, access intentions, segment sessions (secure tunnels), MFA/identity integration (Keycloak), port forwarding metadata, certificates, device configuration, and connection statistics. It is packaged as a Docker image derived from the official postgres image, with initialisation SQL scripts loaded at container startup. It implements row-level security via PostgreSQL roles and security-barrier views, change notification via PostgreSQL NOTIFY/LISTEN triggers, and versioning via shadow version tables for every domain entity.

response.notes: The library is loaded directly by the OS libc resolver (glibc/nsswitch) as a plugin — not by any Dull component directly. It reads the SQLite database at /opt/dull/hosts.db3 (table `LocalHostnameBindings`, columns Hostname/Address/RefCount) that is presumably written by another Dull component. It also reads /opt/dull/dull.pid to verify the agent is alive before attempting any DB query, avoiding hangs when the agent is stopped. The crate is Linux-specific (uses /proc filesystem). The Cargo.lock is present (not a workspace member). Tests are designed to be run under `cargo miri` for memory-safety validation of the unsafe pointer manipulation.
response.purpose: nss_module (crate name: nss_dull) is a Linux Name Service Switch (NSS) shared library plugin for the Dull agent. It exports the standard NSS C ABI symbols `_nss_dull_gethostbyname_r` and `_nss_dull_gethostbyname2_r` so that the OS resolver can call it during hostname lookups. When queried, the module first confirms the Dull agent is running by checking its PID file, then resolves the hostname by querying a SQLite database (`hosts.db3`) maintained by the Dull agent, returning an IPv4 address from the `LocalHostnameBindings` table. It is compiled as a `cdylib` (`libnss_dull.so`) and is intended to be installed in the system NSS library path on Linux.

network_endpoints: unix:///tmp/<tempdir>/socket (AF_UNIX SOCK_SEQPACKET, IpcOneShotServer bootstrap on Linux/FreeBSD/OpenBSD)
mach-port://localhost (Mach port bootstrap server on macOS)
response.notes: This is a vendored fork of the upstream servo/ipc-channel crate (version 0.18.0, repository https://github.com/servo/ipc-channel). On Unix, IpcOneShotServer creates a temporary directory via the tempfile crate (e.g. /tmp/<random>/socket) that holds the rendezvous socket; the directory and socket are automatically deleted when the TempDir guard is dropped. The router module spawns an internal background thread to drive IPC select events. IpcSender and IpcReceiver are themselves Serialize/Deserialize, enabling channels to be sent over channels. The Windows backend uses the windows-rs crate targeting Win32 APIs for pipes, shared memory, and IO completion.
response.purpose: ipc-channel is a Rust library that implements the Rust channel API (IpcSender/IpcReceiver) over native OS IPC primitives: Mach ports on macOS, AF_UNIX SOCK_SEQPACKET socket pairs with file-descriptor passing on Linux/FreeBSD/OpenBSD, and named pipes plus shared-memory objects on Windows. The serde/bincode stack serializes typed messages for transmission. The library also exposes IpcOneShotServer for bootstrapping cross-process connections by name, IpcReceiverSet for multiplexed select-style polling across multiple receivers, IpcSharedMemory for zero-copy shared memory regions, raw-bytes variants (IpcBytesSender/IpcBytesReceiver) that skip serde, and a Router module that bridges IPC receivers onto crossbeam in-process channels. It is designed as a drop-in cross-process replacement for std::sync::mpsc channels.

response.notes: The crate depends on agent-core for ambient environment abstractions (naming, app version, TCP client), agent-db for SQLite pool/worker access and schema constants, and agent-api for the HTTP and WebSocket API client types. PublishedHostMappingTask has three propagation modes: direct hosts-file edit (Unix /etc/hosts or Windows equivalent), local SQLite database update, or IPC channel delivery to a parent process. UpgradedAgentTask writes the downloaded agent binary to std::env::temp_dir() with a SHA256-based filename prefix '_dull_download_', then signals the owning main loop via a Tokio MPSC channel — the task does not exec the binary itself. TestedAccessSegmentsTask only tests TCP-Server factory type segments whose host-and-port resolves to a local address via ambient naming. The impl_task! macro includes exponential-backoff restart logic with a 3-minute reset window. task_test! macro stubs an AmbientMockHTTP client and real SQLite databases in a per-test /tmp directory for integration-style unit tests.
response.purpose: agent-tasks is a Rust library crate that implements a collection of long-running async background tasks for the Dull agent. It defines the BackgroundTask trait and impl_task!/task_test! macros for building cancellable, auto-restarting Tokio tasks, then provides concrete implementations for: bidirectional data replication between local SQLite databases and the remote realm API (segments, certificates, session records, agent versions, device config, hostname); OS hosts-file management (PublishedHostMappingTask); auto-upgrade binary download and verification (UpgradedAgentTask); TCP connectivity testing against access segment endpoints (TestedAccessSegmentsTask); local database housekeeping/trimming for session, debug, and network log tables; and periodic batched query flushing.

response.notes: The macro hard-codes `agent_core` (via `format_ident!("agent_core")`) as the crate from which `Result` is imported in the generated code, creating a compile-time coupling to dull/agent/rust-client/agent-core. It also uses `format_ident!("crate")` for the trait/RequestData import path, meaning it must be invoked from a crate that exposes `crate::api_client::{Param, RequestData}` — almost certainly dull/agent/rust-client/agent-api, though that crate name does not appear literally in the source. The only direct dependency chain evident is: invoking crate (provides api_client module) → this macro → agent_core for Result type.
response.purpose: A Rust procedural macro crate that exposes a single function-like macro `impl_param!`. Given a parenthesised list of type identifiers (e.g. `(A, B, C)`), the macro generates a blanket `Param` trait implementation for the corresponding tuple type. The generated `impl` defines `type Value = (A::Value, B::Value, …)` and implements `update_request_data` by delegating to each element in sequence, threading a `RequestData` value through all of them. The generated code hard-codes references to `crate::api_client::{Param, RequestData}` (the invoking crate's own module) and to `agent_core::Result` for the return type. The crate has no binary, no tests, and no README — it exists solely to be compiled as a proc-macro dependency.

response.notes: All SQLite databases are encrypted using SQLCipher (bundled-sqlcipher-vendored-openssl). The per-instance encryption key is stored in dull.key and is itself encrypted with XChaCha20Poly1305 using a hardcoded key-encryption-key (KEK) embedded in the binary via the obfstr crate. Key rotation/migration logic is present: on open, the library tries the latest KEK then falls back through previous KEK indices, and will transparently encrypt a previously-unencrypted database file. ConnectorState and ConnectorStaging are in-memory SQLite databases (no file path); all others are file-backed. The SqlitePool provides 8 worker threads per database with WAL mode enabled. The hook-fanout task bridges rusqlite's synchronous update_hook callback to async Tokio channel receivers, enabling other components (e.g. session-disconnect handlers) to subscribe to table-level change notifications via register_hook_listener. The BatchedQueryManager queues session and network-connection writes and commits them in a single pass for efficiency. The component implements the AmbientDb trait defined in agent-core, making it the concrete provider of that interface for the rest of the workspace.
response.purpose: agent-db is a Rust library crate that implements the local SQLite database layer for the Dull agent/rust-client. It manages multiple named, encrypted SQLite databases (via SQLCipher) storing connector configuration, session lifecycle events, network connection logs, TLS certificates, segment definitions, and hostname bindings. It implements the AmbientDb trait from agent-core, providing concrete database-backed operations for session event logging, network connection recording, TCP keep-alive config reads, and certificate loading/generation. The library exposes an async thread-pool abstraction over rusqlite, with a SQLite update-hook fanout system for reactive change notifications, a batched-write manager for efficient persisting of high-frequency events, and automatic schema initialisation and column migration on startup.

consumes_files: TLS certificate files (paths resolved via AmbientDb/environment trait at runtime)
network_endpoints: ws://[runtime-configured-address] (WSServer module — HTTP/1.1 upgrade to WebSocket via hyper)
ws://[runtime-configured-address] (WSClient module — outbound WebSocket connect via tokio-tungstenite)
response.notes: ifconfig is spawned only on macOS/FreeBSD to add loopback aliases for TCP server bind addresses (socket2 workaround). The Invoke binary protocol uses magic bytes 0xc0da feed (CODAFEED). The Meet protocol maintains a lazy-static global HashMap of publisher stacks keyed by topic string. The ambient trait system (AmbientDb, AmbientApp, AmbientNaming, AmbientMFA, AmbientHTTP) means this crate has no direct database or HTTP client calls — those are injected by the consuming binary crate. The ipc-channel workspace dependency is used for IPCMessage/IPCResponse passing (DoUpgrade, HostsUpdated, Exit). The builder registry (MODULE_REGISTRY, FACTORY_REGISTRY) maps string names from JSON config to async constructor functions, enabling fully dynamic pipeline construction. The crate targets both Windows (windows-service, winapi) and Unix (libc, exec) platforms with conditional compilation.
response.purpose: agent-core is a Rust library crate providing a dynamic, configuration-driven network protocol pipeline framework for the Dull agent system. It defines the core abstractions — Segment, Sequence, Module, Factory, Context — that compose ordered chains of protocol modules (TCP, WebSocket, TLS, mTLS, HTTP upgrade, and custom binary protocols named Invoke, Meet, and Operator) into network tunnels. Modules are instantiated at runtime from JSON configuration via a builder/registry system. The crate also defines ambient-environment trait interfaces (AmbientDb, AmbientApp, AmbientNaming, AmbientMFA, AmbientHTTP) that decouple the pipeline engine from its platform-specific implementations, and owns session lifecycle management with cancellation, context propagation, and session event recording.

response.notes: HTTP transport goes through NamedStreamConnection (an IPC abstraction from agent-core), not a direct TCP socket — the named stream 'dull-web' is resolved at runtime by the ambient environment. The AmbientHyperHTTP implementation caches HTTP/1.1 connections per named stream. The WS_ENDPOINT_PATH constant ('ws://dull-web/api/v1/notify') is used as the HTTP Upgrade target on the named stream connection rather than a real TCP address. The ipc-channel workspace crate appears in Cargo.toml dependencies but no direct usage was observed in the examined source files — it may be a transitive dependency or used by dependent crates. The .env file is consumed only in dev/test builds via the dotenv dev-dependency. Authentication is JWT bearer token with automatic refresh when the token has less than 60 seconds remaining. The NotifyTopic enum defines five WS pub/sub topics: certificates_change, connectorversion, deviceconfig_change, segments_change, and sessiondisconnects_change.
response.purpose: agent-api is a Rust library crate that provides a typed, async HTTP REST and WebSocket client for communicating with the 'dull-web' backend API server. It exposes generated endpoint structs for CRUD operations on resources such as segments, segment sessions, certificates, device configuration, connection approvals, debug logs, and connector versions. HTTP requests are routed through named stream connections (an IPC abstraction provided by agent-core) rather than direct TCP, with JWT bearer token authentication managed automatically including token refresh. A singleton WebSocket client (WsApiClient) maintains a persistent connection to the dull-web notify endpoint and supports pub/sub topic subscriptions with automatic reconnection.

response.notes: The component ships a code-generation sub-project (Dull.AgentAPI.GenerateTypes) that reads model_generation.json and emits C# source for DTOs, filters, and query-operation classes into the Generated/ subdirectories; these generated files are checked into source control and consumed by the main API project at compile time. Authentication delegates to a Keycloak OpenID Connect provider (configured at http://localhost:8080/auth/realms/dull-manager, client ID react-user). Authorization is role-based (dullread / dullwrite / dulladmin Keycloak roles) and all database queries are automatically scoped to the authenticated device via a deviceId JWT claim. Real-time events flow from PostgreSQL NOTIFY channels (segments_change, deviceconfig_change, sessiondisconnects_change, connectorversions, certificates_change) through an in-memory SimpleEventBus to connected WebSocket clients. The CI pipeline (Buildkite) pushes Docker images tagged dullapp/agentapi to DockerHub; the Docker container exposes port 80. The component references shared-backend projects (Common, Logging, AuthenticationProviderIntegration, WebAPI, DatabaseBackedAPI) that are not listed in the catalog.
response.purpose: Agent API is a .NET 8 ASP.NET Core REST API that serves as the gateway for agents (Launchers, both Native and Web) to interact with the Dull Realm. It exposes a versioned HTTP/WebSocket API under /api/v1 for CRUD operations on connector resources (segments, sessions, certificates, device config, etc.), JWT-based authentication and refresh, and real-time event delivery over WebSocket. All data is persisted in a PostgreSQL database under the agent_api schema, with every query automatically scoped to the authenticated device. PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY is used as an internal event bus that feeds the WebSocket notification channel. A companion code-generation sub-project (Dull.AgentAPI.GenerateTypes) reads model_generation.json to produce C# DTO, filter, and query-operation classes at build time.
